Spaced repetition — cards you struggle with come back sooner.

quiz intermediate

When should you use DuckDB's Quack protocol?

-- Attach a remote DuckDB instance via Quack protocol
ATTACH 'quack://hostname/mydb' AS db;



graph LR
    A["Single app"] -->|"in-process\n(default)"| B["DuckDB\n(library)"]
    C["Multiple clients"] -->|"Quack HTTP v2"| D["Quack Server"] --> E["DuckDB"]


Key insight: In-process mode remains first-class even after Quack. Quack doesn't replace it — it extends DuckDB into client-server territory for teams that specifically need shared access.

DuckDB v1.5.2 added the Quack HTTP protocol. When is it the right choice over the default in-process mode?

→ tools/duckdb
quiz beginner

What Is the /triage Goal State for a Bug Issue?

stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> needs_triage : issue opened
    needs_triage --> needs_info : awaiting reporter
    needs_triage --> ready_for_agent : /diagnose confirms root cause
    needs_triage --> ready_for_human : needs judgement
    needs_triage --> wontfix : matches .out-of-scope/
    ready_for_agent --> [*] : agent picks up + closes


The /triage state machine uses two roles per issue: one category (bug / enhancement) and one state (needs-triage, needs-info, ready-for-agent, ready-for-human, wontfix).

Key insight: The .out-of-scope/ directory stores ADRs for features explicitly not building. When the agent triages an enhancement, it checks .out-of-scope/*.md first — if matched, the issue is closed immediately without surfacing to a maintainer.

| |

In Matt Pocock's /triage skill, which label means an AI agent can pick up the issue and fix it without any clarification?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-backlog-skills
insight intermediate

TDD as AI Verification: Write the Test, Then Prompt

Kent Beck — who pioneered TDD — argues it's "more relevant than ever" for AI-generated code. The workflow flips: you write the spec (tests), AI writes the implementation, tests verify correctness.

// 1. RED — write failing tests that define the exact contract (no code yet)
describe("parseAuthToken", () => {
  it("throws on empty token", () =>
    expect(() => parseAuthToken("")).toThrow("empty token"));
  it("returns payload for valid JWT", () => {
    expect(parseAuthToken(VALID_JWT).sub).toBe("user_123");
  });
});
// All tests FAIL — good. Now prompt:
// "Implement parseAuthToken() so all tests pass"
// 2. GREEN — Claude generates code; tests verify it
// 3. REFACTOR — safe, because tests catch regressions


Key insight: The test is the spec. The spec was written by a human. The implementation was written by AI. You review correctness by running tests, not by reading AI output — trust moves from the generation step to the verification step.

This maps to : instead of hoping AI got it right across 50 micro-decisions, you encode the correct behavior in tests first. The test suite is verification infrastructure that makes AI output trustworthy.

→ ai-agents/agent-quality-gates
connection intermediate

Three Protocols, Three Layers: MCP vs A2A vs ACP

MCP, A2A, and ACP are often confused — they sound similar but solve completely different integration problems at different layers of the stack.

graph BT
    DATA["Databases · Files · APIs"] -->|"MCP"| AGENT["Coding Agent"]
    AGENT -->|"A2A"| OTHER["Other Agents"]
    EDITOR["Code Editor / IDE"] -->|"ACP"| AGENT

    style AGENT fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style EDITOR fill:#0891b2,color:#fff
    style DATA fill:#374151,color:#fff
    style OTHER fill:#065f46,color:#fff


| Protocol | Who talks to whom | What it solves | |----------|-------------------|----------------| | MCP (Anthropic) | Agent ↔ Data/tools | Connect agent to APIs, databases, files without custom integration code | | A2A (Google → Linux Foundation) | Agent ↔ Agent | Agents discover and delegate to other agents across vendors and frameworks | | ACP (Zed Industries) | Editor ↔ Agent | Standardize how code editors invoke and communicate with coding agents |

Key insight: MCP connects agents to data. A2A connects agents to other agents. ACP connects agents to editors. They are complementary layers in the same stack — not competing alternatives.

ACP reuses MCP's JSON type representations by design, reducing implementation burden for agents already supporting MCP.

| | |
→ ai-agents/agent-client-protocol
quiz intermediate

Agent vs. Workflow: which should you build?

The most important architectural decision in AI systems isn't which model to use — it's whether you need an agent at all.

flowchart TD
    Q1{"Task well-defined?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes| W["✅ Workflow\n(developer decides steps)\nbounded error · deterministic"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Needs improvisation?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| A["🤖 Agent\n(LLM decides steps)\nunbounded error · flexible"]
    Q2 -->|No| ST["Break into sub-tasks → Workflow"]
    ST --> W


| | Workflow | Agent | |--|----------|-------| | Steps | Predetermined by developer code | Chosen by the LLM from available tools | | Error compounding | Bounded (N steps is known) | Unbounded (LLM chooses chain length) | | Debuggability | Deterministic replay | Non-deterministic | | Parallelization | Easy — split input, merge output | Hard — stateful reasoning | | Best for | Specified, repeatable tasks | Open-ended improvisation |

> Key insight (Matt Pocock): "Workflows are often unfairly maligned because they're not as exciting as agents. You'll often get better results from a workflow as long as the task is clearly specified." Most coding agent tasks — run tests, apply a patch, format code — are workflows wearing an agent costume.

Matt Pocock's definition separates agents and workflows by one key dimension. Which is it?

→ ai-agents/agent-design-patterns
quiz intermediate

Conway's Law: what should you change before the architecture?

Conway's Law: organizations design systems that mirror their own communication structure. The corollary is that you can't sustainably change software architecture without changing the team structure that produces it.

Key insight: Microservices sprawl is often Conway's Law in action — team boundaries become service boundaries automatically. If you want a different architecture, change who talks to whom.

—

Conway's Law says software systems mirror team communication structures. What's the practical implication when you need to change your architecture?

→ productivity/software-engineering-laws
quiz intermediate

The MCP REST Wrapper Trap

MCP tools are products for agents, not APIs for humans. Agents should complete tasks — not piece together answers from parts.

| ❌ REST Wrapper | ✅ Workflow Tool | |----------------|----------------| | get_user + get_orders + get_products | track_latest_order_by_email | | 3 calls, agent must join results | 1 call, complete answer returned | | More failure points | Fewer turns = fewer mistakes |

Key insight: Design from the task, not from the data model. "What does an agent need to accomplish?" beats "What endpoints do I have?"

A developer exposes their e-commerce API via MCP as separate tools: get_user, get_orders, get_products, get_inventory. What's the core design mistake?

→ ai-agents/building-mcp-servers
insight beginner

AlphaFold: The Data Point That Shows AI's Scale Is Different

Every tech disruption wave follows the same pattern: chaos → practitioners keep building → good practices emerge. But Martin Fowler's caveat: while the pattern is the same, AI's *magnitude* is unprecedented — "nothing has hit with the magnitude of AI."

The concrete evidence: AlphaFold (DeepMind). Protein folding — predicting a protein's 3D structure from its amino acid sequence — was called "the most important unsolved problem in science." Biochemists worked on it for 50+ years. AlphaFold solved it in a single model training run.

![AlphaFold protein structure prediction — DeepMind](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/C0gErQtnNFE/maxresdefault.jpg)

In 2024, Demis Hassabis and John Jumper won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry — the first Nobel awarded specifically for AI-driven scientific discovery.

Key insight: No prior disruption wave solved a 50-year scientific problem as a side effect. The internet added a distribution channel. OO added an abstraction model. AI implicitly internalized the physics and chemistry of protein folding from data — with no explicit equations. This is the kind of emergent capability that has no analog in prior waves.

Prior waves were additive (new channel, new abstraction, new process). AI may be multiplicative — potentially changing who does knowledge work and at what scale, not just how it's done.

| |
→ ai-agents/tech-disruption-cycles
connection advanced

Markov chains → diffusion models: the Phase I math that unlocks Phase VIII generative AI

AI Engineering from Scratch teaches Markov chains in Phase I (Lesson 22: Stochastic Processes) — long before any deep learning. The reason is architectural: diffusion models (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, Sora) are Markov processes with learned transition kernels.

# Phase I: Markov chain — next state depends only on current state
transition = {
    "Sunny":  {"Sunny": 0.7, "Cloudy": 0.2, "Rainy": 0.1},
    "Cloudy": {"Sunny": 0.3, "Cloudy": 0.4, "Rainy": 0.3},
    "Rainy":  {"Sunny": 0.2, "Cloudy": 0.3, "Rainy": 0.5},
}

# Phase VIII: Diffusion model — same math, learned by a neural network
# Forward process: x_t = add_gaussian_noise(x_{t-1}, beta_t)   ← fixed Markov transitions
# Reverse process: x_{t-1} = neural_net(x_t, t)               ← learned Markov reverse


The forward process (adding noise at each timestep) is a fixed Markov chain. The reverse process (denoising) is a learned Markov chain — a neural network trained to approximate the reverse transitions.

Key insight: When you've built random walks → Markov chains → MCMC from scratch in Phase I, the diffusion model "forward/reverse process" stops being magic and becomes a special case you already understand. The curriculum's math-first principle pays off 7 phases later: the model is no longer a black box.

The same connection runs through Langevin dynamics (MCMC sampling technique in Phase I) → score-based generative models (Phase VIII). Curriculum Phase I is not background math — it's the prerequisite for understanding Phase VIII without hand-waving.

→ knowledge-management/ai-engineering-from-scratch
quiz intermediate

The Real Lock-In in Managed Agent Platforms

The managed agent market is splitting into two camps:

| Camp | Example | Differentiator | |------|---------|---------------| | Integrated platforms | Claude Managed Agents | Reliability, battle-tested, proprietary memory | | Open harnesses | Deep Agents Deploy (LangChain) | Self-hosted memory, any model, 30+ endpoints |

Both agree on the same abstractions: agents, sessions, memory, tools. The protocol converges; ownership of data diverges.

Key insight: An agent's value compounds over time through its memory. Who owns that memory determines who owns the compounding value.

|

According to LangChain's Deep Agents Deploy analysis, what is the actual lock-in concern when using a managed agent platform — not the model API itself?

→ ai-agents/managed-agents-code
code advanced

LLM-as-Judge: Scaling Evaluation Without Human Labelers

// LLM-as-judge: a cheap model evaluates expensive model outputs at scale
import Anthropic from "@anthropic-ai/sdk";

const client = new Anthropic();

async function llmJudge(task: string, output: string): Promise<{
  score: number; pass: boolean; reasoning: string;
}> {
  const { content } = await client.messages.create({
    model: "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",  // cheap model for high-volume evaluation
    max_tokens: 256,
    messages: [{
      role: "user",
      content: Task: ${task}\nOutput: ${output}\n\nJSON only: {"score":1-5,"pass":bool,"reasoning":"one sentence"},
    }],
  });
  const text = content[0].type === "text" ? content[0].text : "{}";
  return JSON.parse(text);
  // { score: 4, pass: true, reasoning: "Accurate but omits edge case handling." }
}

// Self-consistency hallucination detection: high variance = likely hallucinating
async function detectHallucination(question: string, attempts = 3): Promise {
  const answers = await Promise.all(
    Array.from({ length: attempts }, () =>
      client.messages.create({
        model: "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens: 64,
        messages: [{ role: "user", content: question }],
      }).then(r => (r.content[0].type === "text" ? r.content[0].text : "").slice(0, 40))
    )
  );
  return new Set(answers).size > 1; // true = inconsistent = likely hallucinating
}


Key insight: LLM-as-judge separates "demo AI" from "production AI." Without evaluation loops, quality degrades as the world changes. With them, each cycle improves: user interactions → implicit/explicit signals → curate examples → update model → A/B test → deploy.

| |

What does this LLM-as-judge function return when the judge model scores an output 3/5?

→ ai-agents/ai-engineering-framework
quiz intermediate

When Does LLM-Assisted Coding Actually Help?

Domain fit determines ROI on LLM coding assistance:

| Domain | Fit | Reason | |--------|-----|--------| | TypeScript, Python, popular frameworks | High | Large training corpus, documentation-rich | | Web APIs, React, Laravel, Django | High | Millions of examples in training data | | Embedded C with specific MCU variants | Low | Narrow corpus, datasheet-level specificity | | Formal languages (Ada, Haskell, Coq) | Low | Small community, correctness requirements | | Security-critical code | Medium | Patterns exist but subtle bugs evade |

The STM32 failure: Asked for CMSIS clock config → received ST HAL code (wrong API layer). Corrected → generated code with wrong macro (PWR_CR_VOS_SCALE1 from STM32F407, not the STM32F411 target). Non-functional and required the same datasheet research the human would have done anyway.

> Key insight (Sarkar et al. 2022): LLM-assisted coding is a *different activity* from both pair programming and search-engine-assisted research. It's better in documentation-rich ecosystems; worse in narrow specialist domains where the model's training distribution lacks specificity.

| |

Maya Posch tested GitHub Copilot on STM32 CMSIS C++ and Ada networking. What was the main finding about cognitive load?

→ ai-agents/building-coding-agents
insight intermediate

Oakley's focused/diffuse toggle — why spacing works neurologically

Barbara Oakley failed math repeatedly, then reverse-engineered how learning actually works and became an engineering professor. Her [Learning How to Learn](https://www.coursera.org/learn/learning-how-to-learn) reached 4 million students — the most popular online course ever. The core finding: the brain has two modes that must alternate deliberately.

flowchart LR
    F["🎯 Focused Mode\nDirect attention\nActive problem-solving"]
    D["🌊 Diffuse Mode\nBackground consolidation\nCreative connections during rest"]
    F <-->|"deliberate toggle"| D


| Common intuition | What research shows | |-----------------|---------------------| | Rereading consolidates memory | Rereading builds familiarity, not recall | | Study until you understand | Spaced retrieval practice cements retention | | Highlighting key passages works | Passive highlighting creates illusion of competence | | Focus until mastered | Interleaving + diffuse breaks outperform pure focus |

Key insight: Spaced repetition and the focused/diffuse toggle are *the same phenomenon at different timescales*. Each review session is focused mode; the space between reviews is diffuse-mode consolidation. The forgetting curve isn't a bug — it's the biological signal that diffuse processing has occurred, making the next retrieval effort more durable.

The practical implication: you don't learn by reviewing more. You learn by reviewing *and then stopping* — letting the diffuse network run during the interval.

| |
→ knowledge-management/spaced-repetition-science
quiz intermediate

What does tau_agent do in Tau's three-layer architecture?

graph TD
    TUI["tau_coding (TUI, tools, sessions)"]
    AGENT["tau_agent (reusable loop — your plug-in point)"]
    AI["tau_ai (provider-neutral streaming)"]
    CUSTOM["Your Custom Frontend"]
    TUI --> AGENT --> AI
    CUSTOM -.->|"same CodingSession API"| AGENT
    style AGENT fill:#0891b2,color:#fff
    style CUSTOM fill:#7c3aed,color:#fff


| Layer | Owns | Depends on | |-------|------|------------| | tau_ai | HuggingFace, Minimax, Kimi, local | Nothing | | tau_agent | Loop, tools, sessions, compaction | tau_ai | | tau_coding | TUI, keybindings, coding config | tau_agent |

Key insight: tau_agent has zero dependency on any terminal framework. A web UI, IDE plugin, or desktop app plugs into the same CodingSession primitives the TUI uses — making the harness teachable by design.

| |

Tau splits into tau_ai → tau_agent → tau_coding. What is tau_agent responsible for?

→ ai-agents/tau-coding-agent
quiz beginner

DESIGN.md: What Does It Add Beyond a CSS Variable?

![DESIGN.md — Google Stitch open-source spec for shared visual language between brands and AI agents](https://opengraph.githubassets.com/1/google-labs-code/design.md)

DESIGN.md is Google Stitch's open-source (Apache 2.0) draft spec for encoding visual design rules for AI agents — same principle as AGENTS.md for coding behavior, applied to design systems.

# DESIGN.md example
## Colors
- primary: #2563EB — primary actions, CTAs. WCAG AA on white. ← semantic meaning
- destructive: #DC2626 — delete, remove, irreversible actions only

## Accessibility
- Minimum contrast ratio: 4.5:1 (WCAG AA) — CLI validates automatically
- Interactive targets: minimum 44×44px


The AGENTS.md parallel:

| File | Domain | What agents learn | |------|--------|-------------------| | AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md | Code behavior | Conventions, testing, style | | DESIGN.md | Visual design | Color semantics, spacing, accessibility |

Key insight: DESIGN.md extends the AGENTS.md pattern to the design layer. Context as a persistent file — read at session start — is more reliable than agents guessing design intent from component names alone.

| |

A CSS variable says `--color-primary: #2563EB`. A DESIGN.md file says `primary: #2563EB — primary actions, CTAs. WCAG AA on white.` What does DESIGN.md add that the variable doesn't have?

→ ai-design/design-md-spec
quiz intermediate

Glasswing SHA-3 commitment: what does publishing the hash immediately prove?

The Glasswing responsible disclosure model adds a cryptographic layer to standard coordinated disclosure:

Discovery → SHA-3 hash published immediately (timestamped public record)
          → Maintainer notified (90-day window + 45-day extension if in progress)
          → Full technical details published
          → Anyone can verify: sha3(published_details) == committed_hash


Key insight: The SHA-3 commitment solves the trust problem in AI-reported vulnerabilities: Anthropic cannot retroactively inflate or deflate severity after seeing a maintainer's reaction. The hash is the proof — committed before the vendor conversation begins.

Glasswing open-source scan accuracy (May 2026): 23,019 findings → 1,587 confirmed true positives (90.6% precision).

|

Anthropic publishes a SHA-3 hash of each unpatched vulnerability's details at the moment of discovery — before notifying the maintainer. What does this mechanism prove?

→ ai-security/glasswing-project
insight intermediate

The Sorcerer's Apprentice Trap

LLMs are the enchanted broom from *Fantasia* — they can generate code far faster than you can understand it. The apprentice (you) gets carried away by the magic; the sorcerer (understanding) is absent.

Key insight: Code has gotten much cheaper to create. Understanding code has become more expensive. The bottleneck moved from production to comprehension.

The corollary: for repeatable, predictable work, use the LLM to write the code *once*, then run it free forever. Spend tokens once to build the tool — then run it deterministically with no LLM in the loop.

·
→ ai-agents/code-is-cheap
insight intermediate

WordPress CPT protection cascade: admin UI says protected, runtime says open

WishList Member (WLM) content protection configured on a parent page with "automatically apply to child content" enabled — but 919 AIOVG video posts remained publicly accessible.

The gap: WLM's cascade follows the WordPress page/post hierarchy. AIOVG videos are a separate custom post type (aiovg_videos) with their own taxonomy. They are not WordPress children of the parent page in any way WLM recognizes. The admin UI showed the parent as protected; none of the 919 CPT posts were ever touched.

Parent Page → marked protected in WLM admin ✅
  └── AIOVG video CPT posts → NOT WordPress children → never protected ❌


Diagnostic principle: Always check the inverse of the reported symptom. "Members are blocked" and "non-members get in" can be the same root cause seen from two sides. The unreported half (919 public videos) is usually the dangerous one.

Key insight: Protection UI in the admin and actual runtime protection are different systems. The cascade that works for pages/sub-pages never fires for a CPT with its own taxonomy. Always verify protection by testing with a logged-out browser — not by reading admin settings.

| |
→ web/wordpress-membership-access-control
code beginner

Manifest: drop-in integration — just change the baseURL

const client = new OpenAI({
  baseURL: 'https://app.manifest.build/v1', // ← only this changes
  apiKey: process.env.MANIFEST_API_KEY!,
})

const response = await client.chat.completions.create({
  model: 'auto', // Manifest picks the tier-appropriate model
  messages: [{ role: 'user', content: prompt }],
})


Key insight: One line changed, up to 70% potential cost reduction. The routing is transparent to the calling code.

What single value changes to route an OpenAI-compatible SDK through Manifest's tier-aware router?

→ ai-agents/manifest-llm-router
quiz intermediate

Gall's Law vs. Brooks's Law — Which Applies to Your Crisis?

56 laws shape how software systems, teams, and decisions behave. Each has survived decades of engineering practice.

Most commonly violated in crises:

| Law | Statement | When it bites you | |-----|-----------|-------------------| | Brooks's Law | Adding people to a late project makes it later | "We just need more engineers" | | Gall's Law | Complex systems evolve from simple working systems | "Let's rewrite it properly" | | Goodhart's Law | Measured targets stop being good measures | Velocity, story points, coverage % | | Ninety-Ninety Rule | First 90% = 90% of time; last 10% = another 90% | "We're 90% done!" | | Hofstadter's Law | Takes longer than expected, even accounting for this law | Any estimate ever |

> Key insight (Gall's Law): "A complex system that works is invariably found to have evolved from a simple system that worked." The strongest argument against big-bang rewrites — the working simple system is the foundation you cannot recreate from scratch.

These laws aren't guidelines — they're descriptions of how complex systems behave. Knowing them doesn't prevent failure; it makes failure legible so you can navigate it.

| |

Your team is 3 weeks late on a critical feature. Leadership wants to add 4 more engineers to close the gap. Which law most directly predicts the outcome — and what does it say?

→ productivity/software-engineering-laws
insight intermediate

Impeccable uses PRODUCT.md + DESIGN.md — the CLAUDE.md pattern applied to visual design

Impeccable captures design intent as two persistent files loaded on every command. PRODUCT.md records who the product serves, its register, brand voice, and anti-references (what *not* to look like). DESIGN.md specifies colors, typographic scale, spacing, and component rules.

This is the same "context-as-first-class-artifact" pattern that CLAUDE.md uses for engineering conventions — except applied to visual design. Without it, AI tools regenerate a generic hero section from scratch each time with no memory of brand intent.

## Anti-references
Purple gradients. Glassmorphism. "Boost your productivity."


Key insight: The anti-references field in PRODUCT.md is the highest-leverage entry — two explicit rejections beat a page of positive style direction.

→ ai-design/impeccable
connection intermediate

LLM Wiki Pattern vs. GraphRAG: same idea, different outputs

Microsoft's GraphRAG (Edge 2024) and Karpathy's LLM Wiki pattern both pre-compute knowledge structure from raw documents rather than retrieving raw chunks at query time. GraphRAG found "substantial improvements over conventional RAG" for global sensemaking questions.

The critical difference: GraphRAG's output is an intermediate representation — a pre-computed knowledge graph used for query-time synthesis. The wiki's output is the final artifact — human-readable pages you can read directly without further inference.

Naive RAG:   query → retrieve chunks → synthesize (every query)
GraphRAG:    precompute graph → query → graph-guided synthesis (faster)
LLM Wiki:    precompute wiki pages → read page (no inference at query time)


GraphRAG optimizes retrieval. The wiki pattern eliminates it. Both are right in different contexts: GraphRAG for large dynamic corpora; wiki for curated, stable knowledge domains.

Key insight: Every generation of RAG adds more pre-computation. The wiki pattern takes it furthest — the synthesis IS the product.

→ knowledge-management/wiki-research-foundations
quiz beginner

AGENTS.md: Which Rule Is Actually Effective?

AGENTS.md (or CLAUDE.md) is read before every task. It's the difference between Claude that knows your conventions and Claude that invents them.

| ❌ Too vague | ❌ Too specific | ✅ General + actionable | |---|---|---| | "Don't make mistakes" | "On UserMessageHandler, don't return early" | "Use Yoda conditions: if (null === $value)" | | "Check everything well" | "Use public constants on app:foo-bar" | "Use strict comparisons only (===, !==)" |

Always include verification commands: 
## Verification Commands
- php vendor/bin/phpstan analyse — type errors
- php vendor/bin/phpunit — run tests
- php-cs-fixer fix --quiet — enforce code style


Key insight: AGENTS.md is now a growing open standard supported by Codex, Gemini, GitHub Copilot, Cursor, and 60+ tools — but Claude Code uses CLAUDE.md natively. For max portability, maintain both with cross-references.

| |

Which of the following is an effective AGENTS.md rule?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-workflows
quiz intermediate

OXC vs SWC: which Rust JS tool for TypeScript compilation?

The Rust JS toolchain rewrite is not incremental — it's 20-100× faster than Node.js equivalents:

ESLint (Node)  → Oxlint (Rust, 50-100×)
Prettier (Node) → Oxfmt (Rust, 30×)
Babel (Node)    → SWC (Rust, 20-70×), oxc-transform (Rust, faster still)



# Drop-in ESLint replacement — no config needed
npx oxlint src/
# 8ms for 450 files vs ESLint's 400ms+

# SWC in Jest (4-10× faster than ts-jest)
# jest.config.js: transform: { "^.+\\.(t|j)sx?$": ["@swc/jest"] }


Key insight: SWC = proven compiler (Next.js chose it). OXC = full toolchain bet (parser, linter, formatter, transformer, minifier — all in one). Pick SWC for TypeScript compilation; reach for OXC when you want to retire the whole ESLint + Prettier + Babel stack.

Your team needs TypeScript compilation and is moving away from Babel. Next.js is your framework. Which Rust-based tool is the safer production bet today?

→ frontend/rust-js-toolchain
quiz intermediate

Which Google GEAR path covers enterprise security and governance?

GEAR (Gemini Enterprise Agent Ready) is Google's free developer program built on the ADK and Agents CLI toolchain. Members get 35 monthly Google Skills credits, no-cost access to the Get Certified program, and early access to Gemini Enterprise tooling.

flowchart LR
    P1["🧭 Intro"] --> P2["🛠️ ADK Dev"] --> P3["🚀 Deploy"] --> P4["🏢 Scale + Govern"]
    style P4 fill:#ea4335,color:#fff


Key insight: GEAR maps the "prototype → production → enterprise" journey onto structured learning paths, with real ADK tooling at each step — not abstract certification content.

Google GEAR's 4-path progression takes developers from intro to enterprise scale. Which path covers CISO-approved guardrails, data grounding, and org-wide scalability?

→ ai-agents/google-gear
insight intermediate

Tool Names Are the SEO of Dynamic Tool Search

When using Claude's Agent SDK with ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH=true, the agent searches for tools by semantic similarity. Tool names and descriptions determine what surfaces — making naming a performance-critical decision.

// ❌ Generic — won't surface for "search Slack" queries
{ name: "query_slack", description: "Query Slack" }

// ✅ Specific — surfaces for "search Slack messages" and similar
{ name: "search_slack_messages", description: "Search Slack messages by keyword, channel, or date range. Use for: finding conversations, locating decisions, message history." }


| Aspect | Bad | Good | |--------|-----|------| | Name | query_slack | search_slack_messages | | Description | "Query Slack" | "Search Slack messages by keyword, channel, or date range. Use for: finding conversations, locating decisions, message history." | | System prompt | No tool catalog | "Available categories: slack, github, confluence, jira" |

Key insight: Tool names and descriptions are the "SEO" of the agent's internal tool search. A well-named tool with a rich description is discoverable; a poorly named tool is effectively invisible — even if it does exactly what's needed.

The Agent SDK returns only 3-5 most relevant tools per task. If your tool doesn't rank, it doesn't load. Max 10,000 tools supported; Haiku models excluded.

| |
→ ai-agents/agent-design-patterns
insight intermediate

Firecrawl /monitor flips scraping from pull to push

Firecrawl's /monitor endpoint means agents don't poll for page changes — the page calls the agent via webhook. Combined with a self-configuring SKILL.md agents can fetch to onboard themselves, it's the only web data layer designed to configure itself for the agent using it.

| Pattern | How it works | |---------|-------------| | Traditional polling | Agent checks the page every N minutes | | /monitor (push) | Firecrawl sends a webhook the moment a page changes |

Key insight: For multi-day agents, polling burns tokens and wall-clock time. /monitor lets agents sleep until something actually changes — a critical efficiency for long-horizon autonomous tasks.

Also notable: 96% web coverage including JS-heavy SPAs, P95 scrape latency of 3.4s, no proxy headaches — Firecrawl handles JS rendering, smart waits, and anti-bot bypass internally.

→ ai-agents/firecrawl
insight intermediate

Orchestrators loop forever without explicit done criteria

In the Operator pattern, the orchestrator plans and delegates work to subagents. Without explicit done criteria defined before the orchestrator starts, it will loop indefinitely — producing progressively worse results as context fills.

graph TD
    DC["⚠️ Done Criteria\n(define before starting — prevents loops)"]
    O["🧠 Orchestrator\nPlans · Delegates · Assembles"]
    S1["🔧 Subagent 1\nTask A"]
    S2["🔧 Subagent 2\nTask B"]

    DC -->|"gates start"| O
    O --> S1 & S2

    style DC fill:#d97706,color:#fff
    style O fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff


Key insight: Define done criteria in the spawn prompt, not mid-execution. "Keep going until all tests pass" is done criteria. "Implement the auth module" is not — the orchestrator will invent more work to do.

Done criteria examples: - ✓ "All TypeScript errors are resolved and tests pass" - ✓ "Every TODO comment has a linked issue number" - ✗ "Improve the code" — unbounded; orchestrator loops - ✗ "Make it production-ready" — unbounded; orchestrator invents work

This is the operator-specific failure mode. Compare: explains why unbounded agent loops degrade exponentially.

→ ai-agents/claude-code-workflow-patterns
insight beginner

Fewer Tokens = More Accurate

A March 2026 paper found that constraining models to brief responses improved accuracy by 26 percentage points. Fewer tokens means fewer opportunities for hallucination. The "Caveman" technique achieves 65% average output reduction across diverse tasks.

This isn't just a cost optimization — it's a reliability technique. Verbose output gives the model more chances to drift off course.

Key insight: Compression is context-dependent. Never compress code generation or user-facing docs. Compress agent-to-developer communication aggressively.

|
→ ai-agents/token-efficiency
quiz intermediate

What does Cavekit's /ck:map command do?

Cavekit (by Julius Brussee, also author of Caveman) implements "spec is the product, code is the derivative." The four phases:

1. /ck:sketch — natural language → numbered requirements + acceptance criteria 2. /ck:map → dependency graph, tiered build plan (e.g., "34 tasks, 5 tiers") 3. /ck:make → parallel execution of independent tasks, validated at each tier gate 4. /ck:check → gap analysis, cross-model peer review (Codex optional)

/ck:sketch → "A REST API for tasks"
/ck:map   → "34 tasks, 5 tiers"  ← This step
/ck:make  → "CAVEKIT COMPLETE — 34 tasks, 18 iterations"


Key insight: The /ck:map step eliminates merge conflicts by grouping only truly independent tasks into each wave. This is a direct architectural solution to the compounding error problem — fewer sequential dependencies means shorter failure chains.

|

In Cavekit's four-phase workflow, what does /ck:map produce?

→ ai-agents/cavekit
insight beginner

The /grill-me Pattern: Interrogate the Brief Before You Plan

The /grill-me skill (Matt Pocock) inserts a clarification phase before planning. Instead of Claude immediately drafting an implementation plan, it systematically interrogates the brief for hidden assumptions.

User: /grill-me
      "The client brief is in client-brief.md"

Claude: (loads .claude/skills/grill-me/SKILL.md)
        "What's the target user? What counts as success?
         What happens if the user skips step 3?
         Should points be integers or allow decimals?"


Why it matters: The gap between the initial brief and what you actually need is always larger than it looks. Ambiguity found during grilling costs zero — ambiguity found mid-implementation costs a full context reset.

Key insight: Don't /clear during the grill session. The clarifications need to stay in context — they become the inputs to the planning session that immediately follows.

The sequence: 1. /grill-me — surfaces hidden assumptions 2. Answer all questions in the same conversation thread 3. Transition to plan mode — the cleared-up brief becomes the planning input

This is the requirements phase of the Agent Skills 6-phase lifecycle (/spec) applied as a conversational skill rather than a document template.

| |
→ ai-agents/claude-code-workflows
code advanced

Svelte custom renderer: implement the SvelteRenderer interface for a new target

Svelte's custom renderers API decouples the analysis pass (which state changed) from code generation (how to update the target). Any renderer implements SvelteRenderer:

interface SvelteRenderer {
  createElement(node: FragmentNode): TNode;
  createText(content: string): TNode;
  setAttribute(node: TNode, key: string, value: unknown): void;
  appendChild(parent: TNode, child: TNode): void;
  update(node: TNode, key: string, value: unknown): void; // fine-grained reactive update
  destroy(node: TNode): void;
}


Same .svelte component → DOM renderer (web), TerminalRenderer (TUI), LynxRenderer (iOS/Android). The IR (intermediate representation / fragment tree) is the shared currency.

Which method fires when fine-grained reactive state changes (not a full re-render)?

→ frontend/svelte-custom-renderers
quiz intermediate

What are the three top-level blocks in a LikeC4 architecture file?

// architecture.c4 — lives in the repo alongside code
specification {
  element person     // define once, use anywhere
  element system
  element container
}

model {
  customer = person "Customer" {}
  ecommerce = system "E-Commerce Platform" {
    webapp = container "Web App"  { technology "React" }
    api    = container "REST API" { technology "Node.js" }
    db     = container "Database" { technology "PostgreSQL" }
  }
  customer -> webapp "browses, places orders"
  webapp   -> api    "REST calls (JSON)"
  api      -> db     "SQL queries"
}

views {
  view context of ecommerce {     // stakeholder view
    include *, customer
    autoLayout TopBottom
  }
  view containers of ecommerce {  // engineer view
    include ecommerce.*, customer
    autoLayout LeftRight
  }
}


Key insight: The model is defined once; views are lenses onto it. Two views from the same model — a context diagram for stakeholders and a container diagram for engineers — stay in sync automatically because they're both derived from the same model {} block.

·

A LikeC4 `.c4` file has three required top-level blocks. Which set is correct?

→ tools/likec4
insight advanced

EmbeddingGemma gets world knowledge for free via T5Gemma adaptation

EmbeddingGemma starts as a Gemma 3 decoder (unidirectional attention), converts via T5Gemma adaptation to encoder-decoder, then initializes from the encoder half to get bidirectional attention. No additional knowledge-learning training is needed — the world knowledge already baked into Gemma 3 carries over.

graph LR
    G3["Gemma 3\n300M decoder-only"] -->|"T5Gemma adapt"| T5["Encoder-Decoder"]
    T5 -->|"take encoder half"| EG["EmbeddingGemma\nbidirectional attention\n768-dim vectors"]
    style G3 fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style EG fill:#166534,color:#fff


Key insight: The expensive part (learning what words mean) is inherited for free. The fine-tuning budget is spent entirely on learning *how to represent* that knowledge as useful vectors — not re-learning the knowledge itself.

→ ai-agents/embedding-gemma
quiz intermediate

Which browsers support same-document View Transitions?

View Transitions have two flavours: same-document (SPA-style, JS-triggered) and cross-document (MPA, native navigation). Firefox 144 closed the last gap for same-document support in January 2026.

| Scenario | Chrome | Firefox | Safari | |----------|--------|---------|--------| | Same-Document | 111+ | 144+ | 18+ | | Cross-Document | 126+ | Planned | 18.2+ |

Key insight: @view-transition { navigation: auto; } enables cross-document transitions — but only where the API is supported. Feature-detect with document.startViewTransition before wrapping JS-triggered DOM mutations.

As of mid-2026, same-document View Transitions (JS-triggered via `document.startViewTransition()`) are Baseline Newly Available. Which set of browsers supports them?

→ frontend/view-transitions
quiz intermediate

Why don't coding agents use vector RAG for code navigation?

A typical mid-sized codebase fits in a large context window (50K–150K tokens). At that scale, targeted file reads beat smart selection via embeddings — and file reads are always fresh, while vector indexes go stale on every edit.

RAG still wins for: large unstructured knowledge bases (PDFs, docs), cross-domain search (code + Jira + Slack), and natural-language queries like "find all places we validate user input."

Key insight: "RAG or file search?" is almost always "depends on what you're retrieving." Code → structural parsing. Prose → semantic search. Mixed sources → hybrid.

Claude Code and Aider don't spin up a vector database for codebase navigation. What is the primary reason coding agents prefer structural search (grep, AST parsing) over vector RAG?

→ ai-agents/rag-vs-file-search
quiz intermediate

Auto-Memory: Which Type Stores 'Avoid X' Rules With the Why?

The four auto-memory types each have a distinct lifespan and decay rate:

| Type | Stores | Decays? | |------|--------|---------| | User | Role, expertise, goals, mental models | Rarely | | Feedback | Rules from corrections + confirmations | Occasionally | | Project | In-flight work, deadlines, active migrations | Fast — update as project moves | | Reference | Pointers to dashboards, wikis, Slack channels | When systems change |

Key insight: Without the *why*, Claude can't generalize to edge cases — it follows the rule blindly. A feedback memory that says "don't use mocks for DB tests — prior incident where mock/prod divergence masked a broken migration" is far more powerful than just "don't use mocks."

| |

Auto-memory organizes persistent context into four types. Which type stores rules derived from corrections — with 'why' and 'how to apply' included?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-auto-memory
quiz intermediate

CodeRabbit's Three-Tier Model Routing: Which Model Does What?

Opus    → Orchestration, strategic direction, full-context problem understanding
Sonnet  → Sequences Opus output into structured planning steps (decomposition)
Haiku   → Context distillation, targeted tool use, narrow-scope operations (fast, cheap)


The routing principle: match model tier to task complexity. Opus gets the hardest job (understanding what's actually needed), Sonnet structures it, Haiku executes the narrow tasks that result.

Key insight: "If Haiku does as well as Sonnet on a given task, we use Haiku. If the evaluation harness tells us plan quality improves when we give Opus more room, we give it more room. We don't guess." — CodeRabbit

The eval harness is what makes routing a science instead of intuition. Model selection isn't based on team preference — it's empirically validated per task type. This same principle appears in : the meta-pattern is matching model capability to task complexity using measurement, not assumption.

| |

In CodeRabbit's planning pipeline, which model handles 'context distillation, targeted tool use, and narrow-scope operations' — and why?

→ ai-agents/coderabbit
insight beginner

One-line addition that lifts MCP tool accuracy by 25 points

Anthropic's engineering team measured the impact of adding a single input example to tool descriptions. The result: accuracy on complex parameter handling jumped from 72% → 90%.

server.tool(
  "get_weather",
  // Without example: "Get current weather for a location."           → 72% accuracy
  // With example:    "...Example: { location: 'San Francisco, CA' }" → 90% accuracy
  "Get current weather for a location. Example: { location: 'San Francisco, CA' }",
  { location: z.string().describe("City and state, e.g. 'Austin, TX'") },
  async ({ location }) => ({ content: [{ type: "text", text: ${location}: 72°F }] })
);


Key insight: Adding Example: { field: 'value' } to a tool description is the single highest-ROI improvement on any MCP server. One line, 18 percentage-point accuracy gain, zero code change. Source: [Anthropic Engineering – Advanced Tool Use](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/advanced-tool-use).

→ ai-agents/building-mcp-servers
quiz intermediate

Which AI role type maps to 'barrel' vs 'ammunition' in the Rabois framework?

The AI role landscape maps directly onto the barrel/ammunition distinction. Barrels are rare; they take end-to-end ownership before certainty. The most valued AI roles in 2026 are barrel roles — they define what gets built, not just how.

Key insight: AI doesn't change the barrel/ammunition dynamic — it amplifies it. A barrel directing 5 AI coding agents produces 10× the output of an ammunition engineer working without agents. This is why Rabois calls barrels "multiplicative" in the AI era.

In Rabois's barrel/ammunition framework, which AI role most clearly maps to 'barrel'?

→ ai-agents/ai-roles-career-paths
insight intermediate

Tool Output Sandboxing Extends Sessions from 30 Minutes to 3 Hours

Raw MCP tool output is the biggest context killer — not LLM responses. A 30-minute session burns 315 KB: one Playwright snapshot (56 KB), 20 GitHub issues (59 KB), a 500-row CSV (85 KB). [Context Mode](https://github.com/mksglu/context-mode) intercepts tool calls via PreToolUse hooks, processing raw data in a sandboxed subprocess and returning only a structured summary.

| Tool call | Raw | Sandboxed | Saved | |-----------|-----|-----------|-------| | playwright.snapshot() | 56.2 KB | 299 B | 99% | | GitHub Issues (20) | 58.9 KB | 1.1 KB | 98% | | Access log (500 req) | 45.1 KB | 155 B | 100% | | Full 30-min session | 315 KB | 5.4 KB | 98% |

// Before: 47 Read() calls → 700 KB in context
// After: ctx_execute processes in subprocess → 3.6 KB returned
ctx_execute("javascript", 
  const files = fs.readdirSync('src').filter(f => f.endsWith('.ts'));
  files.forEach(f => console.log(f + ': ' +
    fs.readFileSync('src/' + f, 'utf8').split('\\n').length + ' lines'));
);


Key insight: The biggest compression wins are on the INPUT side, not output. Caveman compresses what the agent says; Context Mode prevents the tool data explosion before it ever enters the context window. The result: session life goes from ~30 min to ~3 hours without hitting limits.

| |
→ ai-agents/compression-strategies
quiz intermediate

Sandcastle: bind-mount vs isolated sandbox

Sandcastle is provider-agnostic: swap docker() for vercel() without changing orchestration code.

import { run, claudeCode } from "@ai-hero/sandcastle";
import { docker } from "@ai-hero/sandcastle/sandboxes/docker";
import { vercel } from "@ai-hero/sandcastle/sandboxes/vercel";

// Bind-mount — fast, shares host repo
const result = await run({ agent: claudeCode("claude-opus-4-7"), sandbox: docker(), prompt: "..." });

// Isolated — Firecracker microVMs, clean boundaries
const result = await run({ agent: claudeCode("claude-opus-4-7"), sandbox: vercel(), prompt: "..." });


Key insight: Use bind-mount for speed in trusted CI pipelines; use isolated providers when running agent code you don't fully control.

What is the key behavioral difference between Docker (bind-mount) and Vercel (isolated) sandbox providers in Sandcastle?

→ ai-agents/sandcastle
insight intermediate

The Re-Soloing Trap: One Engineer + Many Agents Loses Team Intelligence

Kent Beck and Martin Fowler's joint observation at the Pragmatic Summit (February 2026): the "re-soloing" effect is a hidden cost of AI-augmented development.

When one engineer manages ten AI agents instead of working on a team, they lose the social complexity that historically catches errors no test suite covers — the architectural gut-check from a peer review, the "wait, does this actually make sense?" from a non-author reading it fresh.

Key insight: "AI doesn't replace teams — it amplifies them. The re-soloing trap (one engineer + many agents) loses the collaborative judgment that catches errors no test suite covers." — Beck & Fowler

Their finding: two humans plus AI agents outperform one human plus AI agents, even when the solo engineer manages more agents. The second human multiplies signal quality, not just throughput.

Beck's related insight: TDD becomes *more* important, not less, with AI-generated code. AI-generated code requires stronger verification practices because it's optimized for plausibility, not correctness.

Solo engineer + 10 agents:
  ✅ High throughput
  ❌ Single point of judgment failure
  ❌ No peer catch on architecture mistakes

2 engineers + 5 agents each:
  ✅ High throughput
  ✅ Cross-review catches subtle errors
  ✅ Team dynamics preserved


Fowler also flags that AI performs well on greenfield but poorly on "large and complex codebases" — context complexity is the problem at the codebase level.

| |
→ ai-agents/agent-quality-gates
quiz intermediate

Which n-dx component drives the autonomous task execution loop?

graph LR
    A["sourcevision\nanalyze codebase\n→ CONTEXT.md"] --> P["rex\nmanage PRD\nepics · tasks"]
    P --> E["hench\nautonomous loop\nClause Code · tests · commit"]
    style E fill:#166534,color:#fff


Key insight: Most AI dev tools operate at the file or prompt level. n-dx operates at the *project* level — a living PRD (rex) grounded in real codebase analysis (sourcevision) drives every execution cycle (hench). ndx self-heal 3 . re-runs the full loop three times autonomously.

· ·

n-dx has three core components: sourcevision, rex, and hench. Which one picks the next task from the PRD, builds context, and drives Claude Code to completion?

→ ai-agents/n-dx
quiz intermediate

What Does /pre-mortem Generate — Before Any Bug Appears?

The pre-mortem technique applied to code: before shipping, the agent imagines the system has already failed and writes the incident report. This bypasses optimism bias — imagining failure is cognitively easier than finding it.

| honnibal/claude-skills skill | What it targets | |------------------------------|-----------------| | /hypothesis-tests | Roundtrips, invariants, edge cases — function correctness | | /mutation-testing | Does the test suite catch deliberate mutations? | | /pre-mortem | Operational failure modes — what breaks in production at 3am? | | /try-except | Over-broad exception handling — what errors get silently swallowed? |

The skill can generate a PRE-MORTEM.md artifact. Because this output is large and undesirable mid-refactor, all honnibal skills ship with disable-model-invocation: true — it only fires when you explicitly call it.

What does the honnibal/claude-skills /pre-mortem skill produce?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-engineering-skills
quiz intermediate

Hyrum's Law — Why Every Observable Behavior Becomes a Contract

> "With a sufficient number of users of an API, it does not matter what you promise in the contract: all observable behaviors of your system will be depended on by somebody." — Hyrum Wright, Google engineer

This is why Google's [Abseil Compatibility Guidelines](https://abseil.io/about/compatibility) are stricter than most teams expect.

Real-world examples: - Linux kernel maintaining a syscall behavior for decades because userspace depended on the undocumented edge case - Python's dict insertion order (CPython 3.6 implementation detail → Python 3.7 language guarantee) because code already relied on it - Google's response payload field ordering becoming an implicit dependency

Key insight: The larger your user base, the smaller your "safe to change" surface becomes — eventually shrinking to zero. Every bug fix that changes observable behavior is a potential breaking change at scale. Hyrum's Law is why "this was never documented" is rarely a sufficient defense.

| |

Your API has an undocumented behavior: responses arrive in alphabetical order by accident. Users start relying on it. What does Hyrum's Law predict?

→ productivity/software-engineering-laws
insight advanced

EmbeddingGemma's GOR loss: why embeddings need to fill the space

EmbeddingGemma uses three simultaneous loss functions during training. Two are expected — NCE for similarity, GED for distillation — but GOR (Global Orthogonal Regularizer) is the hidden quality multiplier. Without GOR, a model might correctly rank "salmon" over "granite" for a seafood query, yet pack *all* embeddings into one corner of the 768-dim sphere. GOR penalizes clustering: it pushes embeddings to spread across the full space.

graph TD
    NCE["📐 NCE — Noise-Contrastive Estimation\nPull correct pairs together · Push wrong pairs apart\nHard negatives force fine-grained distinction"]
    GOR["📊 GOR — Global Orthogonal Regularizer\nPrevent cluster collapse\nForce spread across full 768-dim space"]
    GED["🎓 GED — Geometric Embedding Distillation\nMinimize L2 distance to Gemini Embedding teacher\n300M model inherits quality from much larger model"]
    NCE & GOR & GED -->|"combined loss"| OUT["🔢 High-quality 768-dim embedding\nSimilarity-aware · Space-filling · Teacher-calibrated"]
    style NCE fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style GOR fill:#0891b2,color:#fff
    style GED fill:#7c3aed,color:#fff
    style OUT fill:#166534,color:#fff


Key insight: Without GOR, similarity-correct embeddings could still cluster in a tiny region of the hypersphere — making quantization (8-bit, 4-bit storage) lossy and Approximate Nearest Neighbor search unreliable. GOR isn't about similarity; it's about exploiting the *full geometric capacity* of the embedding space.

The three losses together explain why EmbeddingGemma performs better than models trained only on NCE: you need the "fill the space" constraint to make the vectors robust to compression and fast retrieval in production vector databases.

· ·
→ ai-agents/embedding-gemma
code intermediate

Dynamic Tool Search Saves 90% Tokens

// Instead of loading ALL tools into context:
const allTools = getAllTools(); // 50 tools = 10-20K tokens

// Search for only relevant tools per task:
const relevant = searchTools(task.description, { limit: 5 });
// 3-5 tools = ~1-2K tokens — 90% savings


Loading fewer, more relevant tools doesn't just save tokens — it reduces confusion. The model makes better tool choices when it isn't overwhelmed with options.

|

What does this tool search approach achieve compared to loading all 50 tools?

→ ai-agents/token-efficiency
quiz intermediate

Why does hybrid search (BM25 + dense) outperform either alone?

// Reciprocal Rank Fusion: merge BM25 + dense ranked results
// k=60 is the standard constant — flattens the difference between rank 1 and rank 2
function reciprocalRankFusion(lists: string[][], k = 60): { id: string; score: number }[] {
  const scores = new Map();
  for (const list of lists) {
    list.forEach((id, rank) => {
      scores.set(id, (scores.get(id) ?? 0) + 1 / (k + rank + 1));
    });
  }
  return [...scores.entries()]
    .map(([id, score]) => ({ id, score }))
    .sort((a, b) => b.score - a.score);
}

const bm25  = ["doc-3", "doc-1", "doc-5"]; // exact keyword matches
const dense = ["doc-1", "doc-3", "doc-7"]; // semantic similarity
const fused = reciprocalRankFusion([bm25, dense]);
// doc-1 and doc-3 appear in both → highest fused scores


| Search type | Fails when | |-------------|-----------| | Lexical (BM25) | User uses different vocabulary | | Semantic (dense) | User uses exact technical strings | | Hybrid (RRF) | Rarely — failure modes cancel out |

Key insight: "Add hybrid search first — it's the biggest single lift for most corpora and costs almost nothing in extra latency." The pattern: retrieve 50 candidates → rerank with cross-encoder → pass top 5 to the model.

| |

A user searches for 'model v2.3.1 deployment timeout'. Which retrieval failure does each method handle?

→ ai-agents/rag-advanced
connection intermediate

Design Systems → Design Skills → Generative Protocols

Three layers of UI generation have emerged over a decade:

Layer 1 (2015-2023): Static Design Systems — Humans design upfront. Components are fixed. AI has no role.

Layer 2 (2024-2025): Design Skills — AI generates code, but design skills inject taste as correction layers. The AI builds, humans curate quality.

Layer 3 (2025-2026): Generative Protocols — AI outputs structured UI descriptions (A2UI, AG-UI) at runtime. Designers become taste architects encoding principles, not pushing pixels.

Key insight: Each layer doesn't replace the previous — it builds on it. Design systems are still needed; design skills still correct; protocols just add the runtime generation capability.

| |
→ ai-design/generative-ui
quiz intermediate

RAG vs. Finetune: Which Should You Reach For First?

flowchart TD
    Q1{"Knowledge is large,\ndynamic, or external?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes| RAG["📚 RAG\n(retrieve at query time)"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Behavior/style needs\npersistent change?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| FT["🔧 Finetune\n(PEFT, LoRA)"]
    Q2 -->|No| PE["✏️ Prompt Engineering\n(context + examples)"]


| Approach | Best for | Not for | |----------|----------|---------| | Prompt engineering | Behavior, tone, format, constraints | Large knowledge bases | | RAG | Dynamic, large, external knowledge | Style/persona changes | | Finetune | Persistent behavior shift, domain vocabulary | Frequently updated knowledge |

Key insight: Most teams reach for finetuning too early. Prompt engineering and RAG solve the majority of use cases with less complexity and cost. Finetune for behavior; RAG for knowledge.

| |

Your application needs an LLM to answer questions about your company's internal documentation (10,000 pages, updated weekly). It should maintain company tone. What's the right adaptation strategy?

→ ai-agents/ai-engineering-framework
insight intermediate

Build MCP in PHP to understand what the TypeScript SDK is hiding

PHP has no official MCP SDK. Writing an MCP client in PHP means speaking raw JSON-RPC over proc_open — which exposes every detail the TypeScript SDK silently handles for you.

// PHP must do the MCP handshake explicitly — TypeScript SDK hides this:
rpcRequest($serverIn, $serverOut, 'initialize', [
    'protocolVersion' => '2024-11-05',
    'clientInfo' => ['name' => 'php-mcp-client', 'version' => '1.0.0'],
    'capabilities' => [],
]);

// Required notification after initialize — or the server won't respond to tool calls
rpcSend($serverIn, ['jsonrpc' => '2.0', 'method' => 'notifications/initialized', 'params' => []]);

// Every request is wrapped in the JSON-RPC 2.0 envelope
rpcRequest($serverIn, $serverOut, 'tools/list');
// { "jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 1, "method": "tools/list", "params": {} }


The TypeScript new Client() + client.connect(transport) buries all of this. When you implement it in PHP you learn:

1. MCP uses a two-phase handshake: initialize request + notifications/initialized notification. Skip the notification and the server waits forever. 2. Every call is { jsonrpc: "2.0", id, method, params } — method names are tools/list, tools/call, resources/list, not camelCase identifiers. 3. Tool calls use inputSchema → input_schema remapping before calling the Anthropic API (same gotcha as Python).

Key insight: Reading the PHP client implementation teaches the protocol as well as any spec document — maybe better, because you see exactly which lines of TypeScript automate each step. The TypeScript SDK is a convenience wrapper over these 30 lines of JSON-RPC.

· ·
→ ai-agents/mcp-client-typescript
quiz intermediate

Netflix hybrid search: which technique catches what?

Netflix's query engine combines symbolic and semantic search — neither alone is reliable enough at 216M+ frame scale.

| Technique | Catches | Misses | |-----------|---------|--------| | Exact k-NN | Uncompromising precision | Slow at scale | | ANN (HNSW) | Near-instant at 216M frames | Approximate — can miss edge cases | | Phrase matching + slop | Exact names, specific quotes | Semantic variation | | N-gram search | Frame-accurate as-you-type | Only surface tokens, not meaning | | Levenshtein fuzzy | Transcription errors, phonetics | High-confidence exact match speed |

Key insight: Symbolic text matching catches what vector search misses (exact names, specific phrases), and vector search catches what text matching misses (semantic similarity, visual context). The combination is more reliable than either alone — the same logic as multimodal fusion applied to the query layer.

· ·

In Netflix's hybrid search, a user queries 'Freinds dont lie' (misspelled). Which technique handles this correctly that exact k-NN and phrase matching would miss?

→ ai-agents/multimodal-agent-search
quiz intermediate

Lightpanda vs Chrome: what advantage does it offer for AI agents?

graph LR
    subgraph Chrome["Chrome on 933 pages (AWS m5.large)"]
        C1["⏱ 46 seconds"]
        C2["💾 2 GB RAM"]
    end
    subgraph LP["⚡ Lightpanda — same workload"]
        L1["⏱ 5 seconds (9×)"]
        L2["💾 123 MB (16×)"]
    end


Drop-in replacement — no code changes needed:

// Before: puppeteer.launch()
// After: puppeteer.connect() — everything else unchanged
const browser = await puppeteer.connect({
  browserWSEndpoint: "wss://euwest.cloud.lightpanda.io/ws?token=TOKEN",
});


Key insight: At 100 parallel scraping workers, Chrome costs ~200 GB RAM; Lightpanda costs ~12 GB. The economics of agent-scale web browsing change entirely when you're not paying for a GUI renderer you never use.

· ·

What is the key architectural reason Lightpanda is 9× faster and uses 16× less memory than Chrome in agent workloads?

→ ai-agents/lightpanda
quiz intermediate

What's the success rate of a 50-step agent task at 95% per-step accuracy?

Each step multiplies the probability of failure. A simple formula: accuracy^steps.

const successRate = (p: number, n: number) => Math.pow(p, n);
successRate(0.95, 50) // → 0.077 (7.7%)
successRate(0.99, 50) // → 0.605 (60.5%)


Key insight: Reliability is a systems architecture problem, not a model accuracy problem. Even at 99% per-step accuracy, a 50-step task succeeds only 60% of the time.

|

At 95% per-step accuracy, what is the end-to-end success rate for a 50-step agent task?

→ ai-agents/compounding-errors
quiz intermediate

RAG Query Transformation: Which technique fixes which retrieval failure?

Each transformation fixes a specific retrieval failure mode:

| Transformation | Fixes | |---|---| | Rewrite | Poor phrasing — bad keywords or unclear intent | | Multiple queries | Narrow/ambiguous — one angle misses valid results | | Step-back | Too specific — broaden first, then narrow | | HyDE | Vocabulary mismatch — user terms differ from doc terms | | Decompose | Multi-hop — question has 3+ sub-requirements |

Key insight: Most production RAG runs multiple queries + reranking together. These transformations are not mutually exclusive — apply the one that matches the failure mode you observe.

You have a multi-part question: 'How do you deploy FastAPI on Kubernetes with auto-scaling?' Plain vector search misses half the relevant docs. Which query transformation is best?

→ ai-agents/rag-retrieval-techniques
code intermediate

Self-Correction Loop: What Does This Code Return When All Ratings Are ≥ 8?

async function withSelfCorrection(prompt: string): Promise {
  return ask(
    ${prompt}\n\n +
    "Reread your response. Rate it 1-10 on accuracy, specificity, and actionability. " +
    "For any dimension below 8, explain what's weak and fix it. Show only the improved version."
  );
  // One API call — model self-reviews and returns only the corrected draft
}


The self-correction loop is a single API call, not two. The model generates a draft, immediately reviews it, and shows only the final result — the caller never sees the intermediate steps.

graph LR
    D["First draft"] -->|"rate 1-10"| R{"Any dimension\nbelow 8?"}
    R -->|"No"| O["✅ Output as-is"]
    R -->|"Yes"| F["Explain weakness\n+ rewrite"]
    F --> O2["✅ Show only\nimproved version"]


Key insight: Adding a self-correction suffix to any prompt takes 15 seconds and produces a better result 85–90% of the time. *(single source — author estimate)*

| |

What does `withSelfCorrection()` show the caller when the model rates accuracy=9, specificity=8, actionability=7?

→ ai-agents/prompt-engineering-mastery
insight intermediate

The PRD Does Three Jobs: Gate, Artifact, and Onboarding Asset

CodeRabbit's planning system doesn't just produce a plan to help the AI — it produces a Product Requirements Document that serves three distinct purposes for the whole team:

| Job | What it does | |-----|-------------| | Quality gate | If plan quality is poor, catch it before touching the codebase — "late validation is very expensive" | | Shared artifact | Captures what was decided and why; team can review before code generation starts | | Onboarding asset | New engineers see the original intent, not just the output; the chronicle of *why* |

Key insight: "What we've built is a team-wide planning system. The plan itself becomes a quality gate. If the quality of that plan is really good upfront, the downstream effect is very pronounced." — David Loker, CodeRabbit VP of AI

This reframes what an AI coding tool is: not an individual developer's autocomplete, but a team-level process artifact that captures shared understanding before any key is typed.

The three-job PRD is the opposite of documentation-as-afterthought. It's documentation-as-prerequisite. This connects directly to (GitHub Spec Kit's 7-step workflow) and (spec-is-the-product for solo developers) — all solving the same "code written without shared intent" failure mode at different team sizes.

| | |
→ ai-agents/coderabbit
quiz intermediate

RAG reranking: why retrieve 50 but pass only 5 to the model?

Stage 1 — Hybrid retrieval (fast, high recall):
  BM25 + dense embeddings → RRF fusion → top 50 candidates

Stage 2 — Reranking (slower, high precision):
  cross-encoder scores each of the 50 (query, doc) pairs → top 5

Stage 3 — Generation:
  LLM receives 5 high-precision chunks → accurate, focused answer


| Reranker | Notes | |----------|-------| | Cohere Rerank | Hosted API | | Voyage Rerank | Strong general performance | | BGE rerankers | Self-hostable, open weights |

Key insight: Reranking provides a massive quality lift for a small latency cost. Add it after hybrid search — it's the second-biggest RAG improvement after switching from single-method retrieval to hybrid.

| |

In production RAG, why retrieve 50 candidates but only pass the top 5 to the LLM?

→ ai-agents/rag-advanced
insight advanced

ml-intern: From 10% to 32% on GPQA Diamond — Autonomously, in 10 Hours

HuggingFace's ml-intern is an open-source agent that runs the full ML post-training research loop without human checkpoints: literature search → citation graph traversal → dataset discovery → data quality inspection → training job launch → eval monitoring → failure diagnosis → retrain.

GPQA Diamond benchmark run (fully autonomous): 1. Studied citations from the benchmark paper 2. Found OpenScience and NemoTron-CrossThink datasets 3. Added 7 difficulty-filtered variants from ARC/SciQ/MMLU 4. Ran 12 SFT runs on Qwen3-1.7B 5. Result: 10% → 32% on GPQA in under 10 hours

For comparison: Claude Code's best on GPQA is 22.99%.

flowchart LR
    U["💬 User Prompt"] --> AR["arXiv + hf.co/papers"]
    AR --> CG["Citation graph traversal"]
    CG --> DQ["Dataset quality inspection"]
    DQ --> TJ["Training job launch"]
    TJ --> RM["Eval monitoring"]
    RM --> FD{Failure?}
    FD -->|Yes| RT["Diagnose + retrain"]
    RT --> TJ
    FD -->|No| FM["✅ Final model"]


Key insight: The bottleneck shifts from researcher time to GPU time. ml-intern doesn't just automate one step — it automates the *loop*: find → read → implement → train → evaluate → iterate. This is the feedback loop run fully autonomously at the research level.

The data quality inspection step is the critical quality gate — the agent rejected low-quality healthcare datasets and generated 1,100 synthetic data points instead, then upsampled 50× before training. Quality gates built into the loop prevent bad data from compounding through training.

| | |
→ ai-agents/ml-intern
quiz intermediate

Faithfulness vs Factuality Hallucination

Matt Pocock's Dictionary of AI Coding distinguishes two hallucination modes with different root causes and different fixes.

| Mode | What happened | Fix | |------|--------------|-----| | Faithfulness | Context IS loaded; model drifts from it (dumb zone) | Compact or clear the session | | Factuality | Model invents something never in context | Load the right docs |

Key insight: Compacting a session when the model hallucinated a never-loaded API does nothing. Diagnosing which type you have determines the correct intervention.

An agent starts ignoring the TypeScript schema file you loaded and invents incorrect field names that were never there. Which hallucination type is this, and what's the correct fix?

→ ai-agents/token-fundamentals
quiz intermediate

Which Anthropic interpretability technique finds *what changed* between model versions?

Anthropic's interpretability program builds in layers. Circuit Tracing watches computation flow end-to-end; Persona Vectors locate trait directions. But Crosscoder Model Diffing solves a different problem: when a model update changes behavior, pinpoint *which internal computations diverged* — not just that the output changed.

graph LR
    A["Model v1"] & B["Model v2"] --> C["Crosscoder\nSparse Autoencoder"]
    C --> D["Model-exclusive\nfeatures — what's new"]
    C --> E["Shared features\n— what's stable"]


Key insight: Diff tools move model evaluation from "test the outputs" to "inspect the internals." The public diff tool (March 2026) makes this accessible without mechanistic ML expertise.

Which Anthropic interpretability technique is designed to find *what changed* between two model versions?

→ ai-security/llm-interpretability
insight beginner

Scheduling Closes the Gap Between AI Assistant and AI Worker

Claude Routines (April 2026) adds cron scheduling and webhook triggers to Claude Code agent sessions. This one change is conceptually significant:

Key insight: "Routines turn Claude Code from a tool you invoke into an agent that runs autonomously. The gap between 'AI assistant' and 'AI worker' is scheduling. Routines close it."

The difference from N8N/Zapier/Make isn't the scheduling itself — it's what runs inside:

| Platform | Execution model | |---|---| | N8N / Zapier | API chain — call endpoints, pass data | | Claude Routines | Full agent session — planning, file I/O, iteration, judgment |

A routine can read your inbox, draft replies, flag urgency, and create calendar blocks — that's judgment, not data passing. A webhook fires when your meeting ends, and the transcript becomes a proposal before the client hangs up.

![Claude Code scheduled automation and webhook workflows](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/j3aXJNu9804/maxresdefault.jpg)

| |
→ ai-agents/claude-routines
code intermediate

HyDE: What do you embed when the query vocabulary doesn't match the docs?

// HyDE: embed a hypothetical answer, not the user's question
import Anthropic from "@anthropic-ai/sdk";
const client = new Anthropic();

async function hydeQuery(
  question: string,
  embedAndSearch: (text: string) => Promise
): Promise {
  const { content } = await client.messages.create({
    model: "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens: 128,
    messages: [{ role: "user", content: Answer briefly and factually: "${question}" }],
  });
  const hypothetical = content[0].type === "text" ? content[0].text : question;
  return embedAndSearch(hypothetical); // embed the answer, not the question
}
// "killing a process" → hypothetical uses "terminate the job" → matches doc vocabulary


Key insight: Vocabulary mismatch between user questions and documentation is a top RAG failure mode. HyDE fixes it by transforming "question-to-document" matching into "answer-to-document" matching — the same abstraction level.

| |

In HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embedding), what text gets embedded and sent to the vector store?

→ ai-agents/rag-advanced
insight beginner

Context Engineer: what the fastest-growing AI role actually does

Context engineering is the 2024-2025 evolution beyond prompt engineering. Where prompt engineers craft words, context engineers design systems.

Karpathy's definition: *"Context engineering is the delicate art and science of filling the context window with just the right information for the next step."*

Philipp Schmid's definition: *"Designing and building dynamic systems that provide the right information and tools, in the right format, at the right time."*

What context engineers actually build: - RAG pipelines (what the model retrieves and when) - Context window management across long-horizon tasks - Memory hierarchies: what agents remember vs. forget - Information layering: system prompts → conversation history → retrieved docs → tool definitions → safety constraints

Key insight: A prompt engineer optimizes a single prompt. A context engineer designs the information architecture that determines what the model knows at every step of every task. The scope jump is comparable to going from writing SQL queries to designing a database schema. Context Engineers work upstream of AI Engineers, downstream of domain experts.

→ ai-agents/ai-roles-career-paths
quiz intermediate

The Internal Knowledge Gap: What Developers Assume AI Already Knows

Developer's mental model          What was explicitly specified
─────────────────────────         ────────────────────────────
Users log in                      "the system requires users"
Login requires a front door       (not stated — assumed obvious)
Tokens require an auth page       (not stated)


CodeRabbit calls this the internal knowledge gap: the chasm between what a developer means and what they say. It's not a model capability problem — it's an assumption surfacing problem. Senior developers have the same gap when onboarding junior engineers; AI just never pushes back.

Key insight: "As we gain experience as developers, we internalize knowledge. We assume other developers know these things too. But then we make that assumption of the AI system — and we're not even aware we're assuming." — David Loker, CodeRabbit

The mitigation: insert a planning layer that asks *"What is missing? What is taken for granted?"* before a single line of code is written. Catching wrong assumptions at the planning stage is orders of magnitude cheaper than catching them at code review.

| |

A developer asks an AI to 'build a memory system with users.' The agent produces a system where users must provide an auth token on every API call — no login page exists. What is the root cause?

→ ai-agents/coderabbit
code intermediate

auth.md: how does an agent discover and use a registration flow?

// TypeScript: discover and register via agent-verified flow
async function agentRegisterUser(appDomain: string, agentToken: string): Promise {
  // Step 1: discover supported flows
  const authMd = await fetch(https://${appDomain}/auth.md).then(r => r.text());
  if (!authMd.includes("agent-verified")) {
    throw new Error("Agent-verified flow not supported — user-claimed flow requires human OTP");
  }
  // Step 2: present identity assertion from the agent's platform
  const res = await fetch(https://${appDomain}/auth/agent-register, {
    method: "POST",
    headers: {
      "Content-Type": "application/json",
      "Authorization": Bearer ${agentToken}, // identity from agent's platform
    },
    body: JSON.stringify({ flow: "agent-verified" }),
  });
  const { apiKey } = await res.json() as { apiKey: string };
  return apiKey; // scoped API key the agent uses in subsequent calls
}
// Output: 'sk_live_abc...' — agent continues the task autonomously, no hand-off


The app decides what to return: a scoped API key or access token using its existing auth methods. auth.md is the discovery layer, not a new auth mechanism.

| |

What is the first step an agent must take before it can register a user in a third-party app via auth.md?

→ ai-agents/auth-md
insight beginner

Knowledge Compilation Makes Errors Visible — RAG Keeps Them Hidden

The deepest advantage of knowledge compilation over RAG isn't speed or cost — it's error visibility.

| Approach | Where errors live | Can you fix them? | |----------|------------------|-------------------| | RAG (re-derive at query time) | Ephemeral chat responses | No — each hallucination is lost | | Knowledge compilation (pre-synthesize) | Persistent wiki pages | Yes — visible, reviewable, correctable |

A wiki page with a wrong claim can be caught, corrected, and the fix propagates to every future query. A chatbot hallucination in a one-off response is invisible — no one knows it happened, and the next query starts from zero.

Key insight: Compilation shifts errors from invisible (ephemeral chat responses) to visible (persistent artifacts that can be reviewed and corrected). The wiki doesn't just store knowledge — it externalizes the error surface.

This is why the Glasswing security scanner, this wiki's ingest pipeline, and Cavekit's spec-first builds all use the same pattern: pre-compile, persist, review — instead of re-derive, hallucinate, repeat.

RAG:  source → [LLM reasoning] → response (errors ephemeral, invisible)
Wiki: source → [LLM + human review] → wiki page → response (errors visible, correctable)


Each ingest session doesn't just add knowledge — it also verifies and corrects the existing page graph, making the whole system smarter over time.

| |
→ ai-agents/agent-design-patterns
quiz intermediate

MCP Python gotcha: why does the model never call any tools?

# BUG: passing MCP tool objects directly to Anthropic
available_tools = [
    {"name": t.name, "description": t.description, "inputSchema": t.inputSchema}
    # ↑ Claude API ignores any tool without a valid "input_schema" key
]

# FIX: bridge the field name
available_tools = [
    {
        "name": t.name,
        "description": t.description,
        "input_schema": t.inputSchema,  # MCP: inputSchema → Claude: input_schema
    }
    for t in (await self.session.list_tools()).tools
]


Key insight: The API silently ignores tools with a missing input_schema, making it look like the model simply chose not to call any tools. This is the #1 Python MCP integration gotcha — the TypeScript SDK avoids it by remapping automatically, but Python has no such magic. Always explicit-map in your comprehension.

The same bridge applies to any non-Python integration that calls the Anthropic API with MCP-sourced tool definitions (PHP via curl, Go, Rust — wherever you speak the API directly).

· ·

You build a Python MCP client. You list_tools() and pass them to claude.messages.create(). Claude always responds with plain text, never calling any tools. The tools are valid on the MCP server. What is the most likely cause?

→ ai-agents/mcp-client-python
connection intermediate

Skills vs Hooks: what's the difference in Claude Code?

Skills are workflows a developer invokes — /spec, /build, /review. The agent follows them as discipline: harder to skip than prose instructions, but the agent can still rationalize its way around them. Hooks are deterministic enforcement — a PostToolUse hook that runs php-cs-fixer fires automatically on every .php file Claude writes, no matter what the agent decides.

{
  "hooks": {
    "PostToolUse": [{
      "matcher": "Write|Edit",
      "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": "php-cs-fixer fix \"$CLAUDE_FILE_PATH\" 2>/dev/null"}]
    }]
  }
}


The two layers complement each other:

| Layer | What it is | Can agent skip it? | |-------|-----------|-------------------| | Skills | Structured workflows (/spec → /build → /test) | Yes — the agent can rationalize | | Hooks | Deterministic shell commands on tool events | No — exit code 2 blocks the next step |

Key insight: Skills are discipline; hooks are enforcement. A gate that runs as a pre-commit hook cannot be argued with. An anti-rationalization table can be.

→ ai-agents/claude-code-hooks-settings
quiz intermediate

Chrome Modern Web Guidance — Google's Fix for Outdated Agent Code

graph LR
    SK["Modern Web Guidance skills"] --> AG["Coding agent"]
    AG --> WP["Modern browser APIs\n(CSS Anchor · dialog · Popover\nWebAuthn · container queries)"]
    WP --> OUT["Accessible · Performant · Secure"]


| From (agent default) | To (with skills) | |---------------------|-----------------| | Custom modal + focus trap + ARIA | with native focus management | | position: absolute tooltips | Popover API + CSS Anchor Positioning | | Custom auth flow | WebAuthn passkeys | | Fixed layout | Container queries (@container) |

Key insight: This is Google Chrome's answer to the "agents write 2019-era code" problem — instead of hoping the model knows modern APIs, they ship authoritative skills that encode the *right* approach for every common web task.

# Install via Claude Code plugin marketplace
/plugin marketplace add GoogleChrome/modern-web-guidance


| |

What specific problem does Chrome Modern Web Guidance solve for AI coding agents?

→ frontend/chrome-modern-web-guidance
code advanced

Consecutive user messages bug in context compaction
