async function compactContext(messages: MessageParam[]): Promise {
  if (messages.length < 20) return messages;

  // BUG: rawRecent[0] may be a user message
  const rawRecent = messages.slice(-10);
  // Prepending a user summary → consecutive user-user turns → API error ❌

  // FIX: advance to the first assistant message in recent
  const firstAss = rawRecent.findIndex(m => m.role === "assistant");
  const skip     = firstAss > 0 ? firstAss : 0;
  const recent   = rawRecent.slice(skip);   // guaranteed to start at assistant
  const toSummarize = messages.slice(0, messages.length - 10 + skip);

  const summary = await summarize(toSummarize); // cheap haiku call

  return [
    { role: "user", content: [Earlier context]: ${summary} }, // user summary
    ...recent,    // starts with assistant turn — role alternation preserved ✅
  ];
}


Key insight: Conversations start with user at index 0. A 20-message conversation has indices 0–19. slice(-10) gives indices 10–19 — index 10 is even, so it's a user message half the time. The consecutive-role bug is silent: it produces a 400 error from the API with no other warning, easily confused with rate limits or network errors.

· ·

A context compactor takes the last 10 messages as `recent` and prepends a summary `{ role: 'user', content: '[Earlier context]: ...' }`. What bug can this introduce?

→ ai-agents/building-coding-agents
insight beginner

AI Found a 27-Year-Old Bug Humans Missed

Project Glasswing's Claude Mythos Preview discovered a 27-year-old vulnerability in OpenBSD and a 16-year-old FFmpeg bug that survived 5 million automated test runs. Anthropic invested $100M in model credits + $4M donated to open-source security.

This is AI at its best: pattern matching at scale across massive code search spaces. The same model that fails at multi-step math can find vulnerabilities that escaped decades of human review.

Key insight: The arms race is already happening. Suppressing offensive AI is futile — the winning move is defensive deployment at scale. AI findings still ALWAYS require human verification (confidence > 0.85 is not final).

|
→ ai-security/glasswing-project
quiz beginner

Managed Agent Abstractions

Claude's managed agents define four clean abstractions that separate concerns. Unlike DIY harnesses where you manage state, retries, and infrastructure yourself, managed agents handle this at the platform level — the same shift as cloud computing did for servers.

Key insight: Sessions are stateful — file system and conversation persist across interactions. No custom state management needed.

|

What are the four core abstractions of Claude's managed agents?

→ ai-agents/claude-managed-agents
quiz intermediate

The Minimal Coding Agent Loop

The agentic loop in pseudocode:

async function agentLoop(task: string): Promise {
  const context: Message[] = [{ role: "user", content: task }];
  while (true) {
    const response = await claude.messages.create({ messages: context, tools });
    context.push({ role: "assistant", content: response.content });
    if (response.stop_reason === "end_turn") break;
    const toolResults = await executeTools(response.content);
    context.push({ role: "user", content: toolResults });
  }
}


Key insight: Any developer can build this loop in an afternoon. The engineering challenge is everything around it — what goes in the context, how you prompt the model, and how you prevent the agent from deleting your database.

| |

Where does most of the complexity live in production coding agents like Claude Code or Cursor?

→ ai-agents/building-coding-agents
code intermediate

Write Code, Not Reads — The Think in Code Pattern

Instead of 47 file-read calls flooding 700 KB into context, write one analysis script:

// ❌ Before: 47 × Read() calls → ~700 KB in context
// ✅ After: 1 × ctx_execute() → ~3.6 KB (only console.log output enters)

ctx_execute("javascript", 
  const files = fs.readdirSync('src').filter(f => f.endsWith('.ts'));
  files.forEach(f => {
    const lines = fs.readFileSync('src/' + f, 'utf8').split('\\n').length;
    console.log(f + ': ' + lines + ' lines');
  });
);
// Output: auth.ts: 47 lines, utils.ts: 82 lines, ...
// Context cost: 3.6 KB instead of 700 KB


The LLM generates the analysis program; the sandbox runs it and returns only what was printed.

Key insight: The bottleneck isn't reading — it's raw data flooding context. One script that filters and summarizes returns 100–200× fewer tokens than sequential file reads.

|

What does replacing 47 Read() calls with one ctx_execute() script save in context tokens?

→ ai-agents/context-mode
insight intermediate

LLM Pair Programming: The Specification Drift Problem

Maya Posch asked GitHub Copilot for CMSIS code to configure an STM32F411. It returned HAL code. Asked again explicitly for CMSIS — it returned bloated inline structs with a constant (PWR_CR_VOS_SCALE1) that doesn't exist for the F411 MCU variant. The code compiled silently and would have misconfigured the hardware at runtime.

Specification drift failure pattern:
  1. Asked for CMSIS → received HAL (wrong API layer)
  2. Corrected to CMSIS → wrong MCU constants (F407 constants in F411 code)
  3. No compile error — hardware misconfiguration at runtime


Key insight: LLM coding tools don't eliminate the need to verify — they add an intermediate step that may give *false confidence*. Confident, authoritative wrong information is harder to catch than an obvious error.

The [Sarkar et al. 2022 paper](https://advait.org/publications-web/sarkar-2022-programming-ai/) frames this precisely: LLM-assisted programming is neither pair programming nor search-engine use — it's a distinct mode. In niche domains (embedded, specialty APIs), training data is sparse and errors aren't obvious to the model.

LLMs perform best where training data is dense (TypeScript, Python, React). Specification drift risk is highest in specialist domains where the model has seen few examples of the exact API surface.

| |
→ ai-agents/llm-pair-programming
insight intermediate

Hermes: The Agent That Grows Itself

Most AI coding agents reset when the session ends. Hermes Agent (Nous Research, MIT license) breaks this pattern: it runs on your server, accumulates persistent memory across sessions, and auto-generates new skills from its experiences — the capability set expands the longer it runs.

| | Claude Code | Hermes | |--|--|--| | Persistence | Session-based | Server-persistent | | Skill growth | Manual (you write skills) | Automatic (self-generates) | | Platform reach | Terminal/IDE | Telegram, Slack, CLI, 15+ platforms | | Scheduling | Via Claude Routines (separate) | Native natural language cron |

Key insight: Claude Code is a collaborator you direct in real time. Hermes is closer to an employee — it works asynchronously, reaches you on Telegram, and gets better at your projects without you doing anything.

curl -fsSL https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/install.sh | bash
hermes setup


→ ai-agents/hermes-agent
quiz intermediate

What went wrong with Claude Code's caching optimization (Bug 2)?

The clear_thinking_20251015 API header + keep:1 was intended to run once when a session resumed after >1 hour idle. A bug made it run on every subsequent turn for the rest of the session.

Intended: clears once → full reasoning history resumes
Bug:      clears EVERY turn → Claude forgets why it made earlier decisions


Key insight: Prior reasoning in conversation history is load-bearing. Claude uses it to remember *why* it made decisions — it's not just context, it's the memory of intent.

After a session went idle for >1 hour, the caching bug caused Claude Code to:

→ ai-agents/claude-code-april-2026-postmortem
quiz intermediate

TypeScript: `satisfies` vs `as` — Which Validates?

// as: skips validation, loses inference
const routes = {
  home: "/",
  homr: "/home", // typo compiles silently
} as Record;
routes.home; // → string (not "/")

// satisfies: validates + preserves inference
const routes = {
  home: "/",
  homr: "/home", // TS error: 'homr' is not in Record?
} satisfies Record;
routes.home; // → "/" (literal — better autocomplete, discriminated union–ready)


Key insight: as is a promise to the compiler: "trust me, I know the type." satisfies asks the compiler to verify that promise — and still keeps the narrower type it already knows.

| |

What is the key difference between `satisfies` and `as` in TypeScript?

→ frontend/typescript-patterns
insight beginner

The best tools for thought predate computers by 60,000 years

Maggie Appleton's essay argues that "tools for thought" has been colonized by software — but the phrase contains no word for "computer." The most transformative cognitive tools in human history are cultural practices, not software products:

| Tool | Age | What it enabled | |------|-----|----------------| | Aboriginal songlines | ~60,000 BCE | Spatial + cultural knowledge encoded into paths and song | | Written language | 3,200 BCE | Communicating ideas across time and space | | Zettelkasten | 1500s | Linked index cards: single beliefs, cross-references | | Scientific method | ~1000 CE | Hypothesis → empirical observation → cumulative facts | | Data visualization | 1785 | Mapping quantitative data onto spatial relationships |

None of these required computers. All extended human cognitive abilities beyond what was otherwise possible.

Key insight: Current TFT apps (Roam, Obsidian, Notion) claim the label by offering networked note-taking — one narrow pattern descended from Zettelkasten. The software industry captured the term, not the concept. The bottleneck is rarely better software; it's better habits.

· ·
→ knowledge-management/tools-for-thought-cultural
connection intermediate

MemSearch vs Auto Dream — Two Memory Strategies

MemSearch and Auto Dream both solve Claude Code's cross-session memory problem — but they use fundamentally different strategies that complement each other.

| Dimension | MemSearch | Auto Dream | |-----------|-----------|------------| | Storage | Vector index + daily .md logs | Curated topic .md files | | Retrieval | Semantic search (L1 → L2 → L3) | Startup injection via MEMORY.md | | Write model | Auto-summarize via Haiku per turn | Periodic consolidation run | | Source | Raw session transcripts (searchable) | Structured memory files | | Best for | "What did we discuss about X?" | "What are the current conventions?" |

The analogy: Auto Dream is like organized notes on a whiteboard — clean, current, always visible at startup. MemSearch is like a searchable archive of every meeting recording — find anything, anytime, but you have to query it.

Key insight: Use both. MemSearch for episodic recall ("what did we decide about auth last week?"), Auto Dream for semantic memory ("what are our current architecture conventions?"). They're different layers of the same memory system.

| |
→ ai-agents/memsearch
quiz intermediate

EmbeddingGemma: what does Matryoshka Representation Learning enable?

# Use 256-dim embeddings — same model, no retraining
embedding = model.encode("query text")
embedding_256 = embedding[:256]  # 67% less storage vs full 768-dim


| Dims | Storage savings | Use case | |------|----------------|---------| | 768 | — | Maximum accuracy | | 512 | 33% | Balanced production | | 256 | 67% | High-scale systems | | 128 | 83% | Speed/storage constrained |

EmbeddingGemma is trained with Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL). What practical benefit does this give users?

→ ai-agents/embedding-gemma
quiz intermediate

Auto Dream — What Happens in Each Phase?

Auto Dream runs between Claude Code sessions when two gates are both true: 24 hours have passed since the last consolidation AND more than 5 sessions have occurred. One observed run consolidated 913 sessions in ~8–9 minutes, running in the background.

flowchart LR
    P1["🗺️ Phase 1\nOrientation\nRead memory dir"]
    P2["🔍 Phase 2\nGather Signal\nGrep transcripts"]
    P3["🔄 Phase 3\nConsolidate\nFix dates, purge stale"]
    P4["✂️ Phase 4\nPrune & Index\nKeep under 200 lines"]
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4


Key insight: Phase 2 does NOT read transcripts end-to-end — that would be prohibitively expensive for projects with 500+ sessions. It uses targeted grep-style searches: user corrections, "remember this" moments, recurring themes.

| |

Which Auto Dream phase is responsible for converting relative dates to absolute dates and deleting contradicted facts?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-auto-dream
insight intermediate

The mnemonic medium: what if spaced repetition was embedded in reading?

Nielsen and Matuschak built [Quantum Country](https://quantum.country/) (2019) as an experiment: a quantum computing primer where 112 spaced repetition review questions are embedded directly in the text. You read, you get quizzed, the reviews continue over weeks via email.

The insight: card-writing is the bottleneck to spaced repetition adoption. Most people don't start because making good cards is hard. The mnemonic medium offloads card creation to the author.

Nielsen's 10-year Anki finding: Memory enables creativity. When facts are fluent (immediate, no cognitive load), they combine in new ways. He prepared for an AlphaGo article by making 70 cards on Go strategy — the fluency let him hold the whole game in mind while writing.

> Key insight (Nielsen): "Spaced repetition is a *programmable* memory." Once you've written a card, you can *choose* to remember that fact — and the system makes it happen. Most people treat memory as passive; spaced repetition makes it deliberate.

Writing good cards (Matuschak): Atomic (one fact), precise (not vague), connected (fact + why it matters), personal (your use case, not abstractions).

| |
→ knowledge-management/spaced-repetition-science
code intermediate

One-line provider switch with Vercel AI SDK

The same generateText call works with any provider — just swap the model import:

import { generateText } from "ai";
import { anthropic } from "@ai-sdk/anthropic";
// import { openai }  from "@ai-sdk/openai";
// import { google }  from "@ai-sdk/google";

// Change this one line to switch providers:
const model = anthropic("claude-sonnet-4-6");
// const model = openai("gpt-4o");
// const model = google("gemini-2.5-flash");

const { text } = await generateText({
  model,
  prompt: "Summarize this document: ...",
});
// Output: same structure regardless of active provider


Key insight: Provider lock-in is fragile — pricing changes, rate limits shift, new models emerge. Separating provider choice from agent logic (via any-llm or Vercel AI SDK) lets you benchmark and switch without touching business logic.

|

In the Vercel AI SDK, what is the minimum change needed to switch from Anthropic Claude to OpenAI GPT-4o?

→ ai-agents/mozilla-ai
insight beginner

Stale Docs Are Annoying for Humans — But Poison for AI Agents

Human developers are skeptical of wrong instructions — they mentally filter stale docs and double-check before acting. AI agents read AGENTS.md as authoritative ground truth: the agent follows a stale file path confidently into the wrong directory, runs the renamed command without hesitation, looks in the deleted file.

| ❌ Dangerous — changes constantly | ✅ Safe — changes slowly | |----------------------------------|------------------------| | src/auth/handlers.ts — file paths | "authentication logic" — capabilities | | org.users_table — schema names | "user identity management" — domain concepts | | run npm test:unit — script names | "run the unit test suite" — intent |

The rule: Describe capabilities and concepts, not paths and commands. Let the agent discover structure just-in-time during planning.

Key insight: For humans, stale docs are a speed bump. For AI agents operating inside an agentic loop, stale documentation is actively authoritative — the agent doesn't question it, it executes on it. A wrong file path in AGENTS.md is a guaranteed failure on every task that touches that path.

Fixing a bloated, stale AGENTS.md — paste this into your coding agent: 
Refactor my AGENTS.md: remove specific file paths (replace with capability descriptions),
consolidate duplicate rules, move TypeScript/CSS/test conventions to docs/ subdirectory files,
leave only project description + package manager + build command in root AGENTS.md.


| |
→ ai-agents/agents-md-best-practices
insight intermediate

When should product teams reject customer feedback?

Keith Rabois's counterintuitive rule: direct customer feedback is actively harmful for genuinely novel consumer products — customers describe the product they already know, not the product you could build.

| Product type | Use customer feedback? | Why | |---|---|---| | Consumer (novel) | No — trust product intuition | Customers constrained by what exists | | B2B / enterprise | Yes — actively seek it | Real operational pain → actionable improvements | | Existing products | Yes — structured research | Improving known things → feedback reliably narrows the gap |

The distinction is inventing vs. improving. Steve Jobs rejected customer research for the iPhone; Apple runs structured research for iOS usability improvements.

Key insight: The same information (customer feedback) is a liability when innovating and an asset when optimizing. The variable is whether the customer can imagine the product space you're building toward. If they can't, their feedback points backward.

·
→ productivity/barrels-vs-ammunition
quiz intermediate

WooCommerce MCP: Why Is There a Local Proxy?

flowchart TD
    CD["🤖 Claude Desktop"] -->|MCP protocol| LP["Local Proxy\n(Node.js)"]
    LP -->|HTTPS + auth| WP["WordPress site\n/wp-json/mcp"]
    WP -->|Abilities API| WC["WooCommerce + Plugins"]


Two MCP patterns, one protocol:

| Pattern | Example | What's on the other side | |---------|---------|--------------------------| | Hosted API MCP | WooCommerce MCP | Remote API (live store data) | | Local CLI MCP | WordPress Studio MCP | Local CLI → filesystem + DB |

From Claude Desktop you can ask: "Show me my low-stock products" or "Apply a 20% discount to the Summer collection" — the proxy translates these into WooCommerce Abilities API calls.

Key insight: The local proxy is also the security layer. Your WooCommerce credentials never leave your machine — they're handled by the proxy, not passed through Claude's infrastructure.

| |

In the WooCommerce MCP architecture, what is the local proxy's role?

→ ai-agents/mcp-ecosystem
code intermediate

PHP + Hyperbrowser: what does BrowserFactory receive to connect?

PHP has no official Hyperbrowser SDK, so you use two separate libraries: curl for session creation (REST API) and chrome-php/chrome for browser control (CDP). The session lifecycle is the same across all languages: create → connect → use → stop.

// 1. Create session via REST API
$wsEndpoint = hyperbrowserCreateSession(getenv('HYPERBROWSER_API_KEY'));
// wsEndpoint = "wss://cdn.hyperbrowser.ai/ws?token=..."

// 2. BrowserFactory accepts WS URL just like a local binary path
$browser = (new BrowserFactory($wsEndpoint))->createBrowser();
$page    = $browser->createPage();
$page->navigate('https://example.com')->waitForNavigation();
echo $page->evaluate('document.title')->getReturnValue(); // "Example Domain"
$browser->close();


Key insight: BrowserFactory is CDP-agnostic — it doesn't care whether the endpoint is a local process or a cloud container. The wsEndpoint from Hyperbrowser's REST API slots in where the binary path would normally go.

PHP has no official Hyperbrowser SDK. What does `BrowserFactory` receive as its argument to connect to a cloud session?

→ ai-agents/hyperbrowser
code beginner

<dialog> replaces ~40 lines of focus-trap + ARIA + backdrop boilerplate




  

  

    
Confirm delete

    
    
  






  
Confirm delete

  

    
    
  






const dialog = document.querySelector("#confirm-dialog")!;
dialog.addEventListener("close", () => {
  if (dialog.returnValue === "confirm") handleDelete();
});
// dialog.showModal() → native backdrop · traps focus · blocks scroll
// form method="dialog" → sets returnValue to clicked button's value attribute


Key insight: is the clearest browser-primitive win in Chrome's Modern Web Guidance. It's not just shorter — it's more accessible by default, handles edge cases (Escape key, focus restoration) that most custom implementations miss.

| |

What does the native <dialog> element provide automatically that a custom modal div requires manual implementation for?

→ frontend/chrome-modern-web-guidance
quiz intermediate

HTML vs Markdown for AI Output — Why Format Choices Should Be Revisited

| Era | Context window | Why Markdown made sense | |-----|----------------|------------------------| | GPT-4 era (2022–2024) | 8K–32K tokens | Terseness; every token counted | | Modern agents (2025+) | 200K–1M tokens | That constraint is gone |

Use HTML output when the response renders in a browser or web app. Use Markdown output when the output goes to a terminal, .md file, or text logger.

Key insight: Markdown-as-default is a historical accident of small context windows, not a principled design decision. Constraints from one era shouldn't dictate conventions after the hardware changes.

## Output Format (in CLAUDE.md for web-rendering contexts)

For responses rendered in the browser:
- Output HTML, not Markdown
- Use semantic elements: 
, 
, 
/
- Use 
 for code blocks
- Do NOT use Markdown syntax (**, ##, -, etc.)



 |  |

Markdown became the default AI output format in the GPT-4 era. What constraint justified it then — and why has that argument weakened?

→ ai-agents/html-vs-markdown-output
quiz intermediate

What makes `roborev refine` different from `roborev fix`?

flowchart LR
    FIX["roborev fix\n(one round)"] --> COMMIT["new commit"]
    COMMIT --> REVIEW["re-reviewed by hook"]
    
    REFINE["roborev refine\n(isolated worktree)"] --> LOOP["fix → commit → review\n↺ until all pass"]
    
    style REFINE fill:#166534,color:#fff
    style LOOP fill:#0891b2,color:#fff


Key insight: refine is the agentic quality loop with a terminal state — it doesn't stop until the codebase is clean or budget is exhausted. The isolated worktree ensures your working tree stays untouched during the loop.

· ·

What does `roborev refine` do that `roborev fix` does not?

→ ai-agents/roborev
insight beginner

'50 AI Agents Running My Company' Is a Slop Machine

Max Brodeur-Urbas (CEO, Gumloop, $50M Series A) on the viral "I run my company with 50 AI agents" narrative: "That's not automation. That's a slop machine."

The math backs him up: if each of 50 agents works at 95% accuracy, the compound success probability is 0.95^50 ≈ 7.7%. You get garbage.

The real playbook: - Identify your 2-3 highest-ROI workflows - Build those with deep validation and human oversight at critical steps - Measure actual outcomes, not agent count

Key insight: Agent count is a vanity metric. Automation reliability is an engineering metric. The companies that successfully automate with AI have fewer, better-validated flows — not more agents.

The same insight applies to : reliability requires shorter chains, not longer ones. Each agent you add is another step where errors can compound.

|
→ ai-agents/agent-quality-gates
insight intermediate

Operant flips the standard Slack bot identity model

Most Slack bots run under a single shared identity — every tool call lands in the audit log as "the workspace did it." Operant flips this: each Slack user connects their own accounts via Pipedream Connect, and every action runs under *that person's* credentials.

graph TD
    subgraph Standard["Standard Slack Bot"]
        BOT["Bot identity\n(shared)"] --> TOOLS1["Gmail · GitHub · Linear\n(shared credentials)"]
        TOOLS1 --> LOG1["Audit: 'workspace did it'"]
    end
    subgraph Operant["Operant"]
        U1["Alice's OAuth"] --> TOOLS2["Alice's Gmail · Alice's GitHub"]
        U2["Bob's OAuth"] --> TOOLS2
        TOOLS2 --> LOG2["Audit: 'Alice ran this tool call'"]
    end
    style LOG2 fill:#166534,color:#fff
    style LOG1 fill:#991b1b,color:#fff


Key insight: Per-user OAuth transforms the audit log from "the bot did something" to "Alice ran gmail.send on behalf of Bob's task." In regulated environments, this distinction is the difference between passing and failing a compliance review.

Operant is self-hosted (Docker Compose → Helm/Fly), MIT licensed, and pairs with as the agent runtime. Six built-in roles (owner → viewer) plus custom (action, resource) permissions.

· ·
→ ai-agents/operant
connection intermediate

A2A and MCP: Complementary, Not Competing

Two protocols that often get conflated — but operate at different layers:

| Protocol | Who talks to whom | What it enables | |----------|------------------|-----------------| | MCP (Anthropic) | Agent ↔ Tools/Data | Agent accesses databases, APIs, filesystem, external context | | A2A (Google, 50+ partners) | Agent ↔ Agent | Agents discover, delegate to, and coordinate with *other agents* |

User → [Orchestrating Agent]
            ↓ MCP: read CRM database
            ↓ A2A: delegate to CandidateSourcingAgent (Salesforce)
                       ↓ MCP: read Salesforce records
            ↓ A2A: delegate to CalendarAgent (enterprise calendar)


Key insight: A2A to multi-agent systems is what HTTP was to websites — the interoperability layer that lets a heterogeneous ecosystem function as one coherent network. MCP gives agents their tools; A2A lets them coordinate work.

The same "open protocol prevents vendor lock-in" logic appears in AT Protocol for social data — open standards enabling portability across providers.

| |
→ ai-agents/agent-interoperability
code intermediate

What does this OpenTUI React code render?

import { createCliRenderer } from "@opentui/core"
import { createRoot } from "@opentui/react"

function App() {
  return Hello, OpenTUI!
}

const renderer = await createCliRenderer()
createRoot(renderer).render()


Point jsxImportSource at @opentui/react in tsconfig.json and your React components render into the terminal instead of the DOM. OpenTUI is not a wrapper around ncurses — it's a Zig-native core with Yoga-powered flexbox layout and tree-sitter syntax highlighting.

Key insight: The same React mental model — components, hooks, JSX — works in the terminal. UI frameworks are decoupled from their rendering targets.

What does this OpenTUI code render, and where?

→ frontend/opentui
quiz intermediate

SkillToolset: How Much Does Progressive Disclosure Save?

L1: list_skills → name + description (~100 tokens each) — always loaded
L2: load_skill  → full instructions (<5,000 tokens) — loaded on demand
L3: load_skill_resource → style guides, API specs — loaded on demand


The three-tool contract is framework-agnostic (ADK, Claude Code, Cursor, 40+ tools). The agent always starts at L1, scans descriptions to decide relevance, then drills down only when needed — the same pattern as applied to knowledge rather than tools.

Key insight: 10 skills × ~100 token L1 ≈ 1,000 tokens baseline. vs. concatenated ≈ 10,000 tokens. The skill factory extends this further: an agent that generates new SKILL.md files at runtime without human intervention.

| |

A SkillToolset has 10 skills, each with ~1,000 tokens of full instructions. Using L1/L2/L3 progressive disclosure instead of concatenating all instructions, approximately how much is the baseline context reduced?

→ ai-agents/agent-quality-gates
quiz intermediate

Claude Code hook load order: which settings win?

graph LR
    PER["🔷 Personal hooks\n~/.claude/settings.json\nhighest priority"]
    PRJ["🔶 Project hooks\n.claude/settings.json\nsecond"]
    PLG["🔴 Plugin hooks\nalphabetical by name\nlowest priority"]

    PER -->|"then"| PRJ -->|"then"| PLG

    style PER fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style PRJ fill:#7c3aed,color:#fff
    style PLG fill:#dc2626,color:#fff


Key insight: Fixing cascade in personal or project settings overrides plugin behavior — you don't need to modify or uninstall plugins. A single-formatter lock-file hook in ~/.claude/settings.json prevents any plugin's PostToolUse formatter from cascading, because personal hooks run first and establish the lock.

| |

When multiple hooks are registered for the same PostToolUse event — one in ~/.claude/settings.json, one in .claude/settings.json, and one from an installed plugin — which order do they run in?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-hook-conflicts
code intermediate

Checkpoint granularity: where to call saveCheckpoint()

The checkpoint-and-resume pattern is the most foundational long-running agent pattern. Granularity is the critical design decision:

for (const item of remaining) {
  await processItem(item);
  cp.processedIds.push(item.id);
  saveCheckpoint(cp); // ← after each item, not at the end
  // crash here → next run resumes from the NEXT unprocessed item
}
// Output on resume after crash at item 48: "Resuming: 47 done, 153 remaining"


Key insight: Too fine-grained (every step) wastes I/O. Too coarse (end-only) means reprocessing everything on failure. Right granularity: after each meaningful unit of work.

In a checkpoint-and-resume pattern, where should saveCheckpoint() be called?

→ ai-agents/long-running-agents
insight intermediate

Code First, Spec Second: The Prototypes > Plans Reversal

Matt Pocock (2026): "The more I replace plans with prototypes, the better the outputs. Who'd have thought that low fidelity prototypes were better than walls of spec. Oh yeah, the entire industry for 20 years."

The inverted workflow — code IS the research phase, not the product:

/grill-with-docs     → surface the question you can't answer without seeing it
→ /prototype         → rough working version (burn tokens freely)
→ iterate on prototype until it feels right
→ /rewind            → return to the open question with prototype as evidence
→ select "summarize" → compress learnings into spec


Spec written *after* touching prototype code is dramatically better than spec from imagination, because some constraints and interactions are invisible until you see the interaction working.

Key insight: /grill-with-docs and /domain-model work better when paired with a prototype step — you can't fully specify before prototyping because some questions only become answerable by seeing the system move.

→ ai-agents/claude-code-grilling-skills
quiz intermediate

METR time horizon: how fast is the doubling rate accelerating?

The METR time horizon metric measures how long an AI agent can autonomously work on a task before needing human help. It has been doubling steadily since 2019.

All-time trend  → 6.3 months / 188 days per doubling (2019–2026)
Post-2024 pace  → ~3 months / ~89 days per doubling (acceleration)

If extrapolated:
  2028 → day-scale autonomous tasks (24h+)
  2034 → year-scale autonomous tasks


Key insight: The acceleration post-2024 is the key finding. The trend wasn't just continuing — it was compressing. The long-running agent engineering patterns documented today are what makes these timelines plausible.

This is why production harnesses (checkpoint-resume, brain/hands/session split, test ratchets) matter now — not as research curiosities but as the infrastructure that enables the next doubling.

According to METR Time Horizon 1.1, what is the post-2024 doubling rate for AI agent task horizons — and how does it compare to the all-time historical average?

→ ai-agents/long-running-agents
insight intermediate

Agent Experience (AX): Building Tools for Two Audiences

Fowler and Beck surface a new UX dimension: when you build APIs, CLIs, and tools, you now have two audiences — human developers and AI agents that use those tools autonomously.

| Human developer needs | Agent needs | |----------------------|-------------| | Readable documentation | Machine-readable output (JSON, structured data) | | Memorable CLI flags | Deterministic behavior (same input → same output) | | Helpful interactive prompts | No interactive prompts — blocks agent workflows | | Friendly error messages | Actionable errors with context ("file not found at /path/x") | | Visual feedback | Parseable stdout/stderr |

The interactive prompt issue is particularly consequential: any tool that asks for human confirmation mid-execution blocks an autonomous agent. This is why Claude Code has --dangerously-skip-permissions — and why security sandboxing matters when you use it.

Key insight: "Agent experience" (AX) is a new design constraint. A CLI that prompts Are you sure? [y/N] is AX-hostile. A CLI with --yes and structured JSON output is AX-friendly. The AI transition is making this a first-class API design requirement.

| |
→ ai-agents/tech-disruption-cycles
connection intermediate

Cohere's three models map onto three RAG pipeline stages

Cohere's model lineup maps directly onto the retrieval-augmented generation pipeline — one model per stage:

User Query
  → Embed        dense vector representation for similarity search
  → Rerank       cross-encoder re-scores top-50 candidates → top-5
  → Command      generates answer from the 5 most relevant docs


All three run inside the same private deployment — no document content crosses a vendor boundary between retrieval and generation.

// Stage 2: rerank after initial vector search
const { results } = await fetch("https://api.cohere.com/v2/rerank", {
  method: "POST",
  headers: { "Authorization": Bearer ${process.env.COHERE_API_KEY} },
  body: JSON.stringify({
    model: "rerank-v3.5",
    query: userQuery,
    documents: vectorSearchCandidates, // top-50 from Embed search
    top_n: 5,
  }),
}).then(r => r.json());
// results[0].relevance_score → 0.0–1.0; pass top-5 to Command


Key insight: Buying Embed + Rerank + Command from one vendor means the full RAG pipeline runs under one private deployment contract. No mixing providers, no data crossing boundaries between retrieval and generation.

| |
→ ai-agents/cohere
quiz intermediate

ADR Templates — When to Use MADR Over Nygard?

The WICSA 2015 paper *Architectural Decision Guidance Across Projects* compared seven ADR templates. The key axis: decision complexity vs. documentation overhead.

| Template | Best for | |----------|----------| | Nygard | Most decisions — minimal, widely known | | MADR | 3+ serious alternatives, explicit trade-off docs | | Y-statements | Simple decisions, fits in code comments | | RFC | Cross-team, high-stakes architectural pivots |

Key insight: The Azure Well-Architected Framework, AWS Prescriptive Guidance, and the Open Practice Library all recommend ADRs — but they're still underused because teams skip them under time pressure, then re-argue settled decisions six months later when the team changes.

| |

You're facing a decision with four serious alternatives and need to document the trade-offs between them. Which ADR template is best suited for this?

→ productivity/architectural-decision-records
code intermediate

LLMs sometimes wrap JSON in code fences — how do you reliably parse it?

LLMs reliably produce valid JSON *content* but don't always honor "no fences" instructions. The extractJson helper is the standard fix in production pipelines:

// TypeScript
function extractJson(text: string): string {
  const fenced = text.match(/
(?:json)?\s*([\s\S]*?)``/); if (fenced) return fenced[1].trim(); const start = text.search(/[{\[]/); return start >= 0 ? text.slice(start) : text; }

// Usage const meta = JSON.parse(extractJson(llmResponse));
php // PHP function extractJson(string $text): string { if (preg_match('/`(?:json)?\s*([\s\S]*?)`/', $text, $m)) return trim($m[1]); if (preg_match('/[{\[]/', $text, $m, PREG_OFFSET_CAPTURE)) return substr($text, $m[0][1]); return $text; } 

Key insight: Three failure modes, one function: (1)
json ... ` fence — extract inner content; (2) prose preamble before {` — slice from first brace; (3) raw JSON — return unchanged. Never assume the model omits fences just because you asked nicely.

· ·

You ask Claude to return raw JSON. It returns ```json\n{...}\n``` instead. What's the minimal fix to handle both cases?

→ knowledge-management/wiki-ingest-pipeline
insight beginner

Barrels vs. Ammunition — The AI Era Multiplier

Keith Rabois' talent framework: two types of people in every organization.

Barrels take end-to-end ownership of outcomes. Given a goal, they find the path. Rare.

Ammunition execute with precision given clear direction. Essential, but impact is linear — they need a barrel to point them.

Barrel:     Goal → [independent path-finding] → shipped outcome
Ammunition: Clear task → [skilled execution] → completed task


Why this matters with AI:

- A barrel directing 5 AI agents outputs what previously required 10 people → multiplicative - High-skill implementation tasks (boilerplate, queries, specs-to-code) are now AI-accessible → ammunition is partially automated

Key insight: AI makes barrels more powerful and ammunition more interchangeable. The competitive advantage shifts to barrel density — teams where most people can independently own scope end-to-end. AI provides the leverage; barrels direct it.

The dying PM role: The traditional PM-as-translator disappears when both business and engineering speak the same language (natural language + AI). What survives is ownership, judgment, and taste — the barrel qualities.

| |
→ productivity/barrels-vs-ammunition
quiz beginner

When should the agentic loop exit?

The two stop states in every agentic loop:

| stop_reason | Meaning | What to do | |--------------|---------|------------| | "tool_use" | LLM requested ≥1 tool | Execute each tool, push tool_result, call LLM again | | "end_turn" | LLM finished | Extract text block, print result, break |

if (response.stop_reason === "end_turn") {
  const text = response.content.find(b => b.type === "text");
  console.log(text?.text);
  break; // ← only exit point
}
// otherwise: execute tools, push tool_results, continue loop


When does the while(true) agentic loop exit?

→ ai-agents/agentic-loop-typescript
code intermediate

WordPress REST API — fetch posts with embedded featured image (TypeScript + PHP)

The WordPress REST API is built into every WordPress installation since v4.7. No plugins. Works from any frontend, React block, Node.js script, or PHP cron.

// TypeScript: fetch recent posts with embedded featured image
interface WPPost {
  id: number;
  title: { rendered: string };
  _embedded?: {
    "wp:featuredmedia"?: Array<{ source_url: string }>;
  };
}

async function getRecentPosts(siteUrl: string, perPage = 10): Promise {
  const url = new URL(${siteUrl}/wp-json/wp/v2/posts);
  url.searchParams.set("per_page", String(perPage));
  url.searchParams.set("_embed", "1"); // embeds featured image + author inline
  const res = await fetch(url.toString());
  if (!res.ok) throw new Error(WP REST error: ${res.status});
  return res.json() as Promise;
}

const posts = await getRecentPosts("https://example.com");
posts.forEach(p => {
  const img = p._embedded?.["wp:featuredmedia"]?.[0]?.source_url ?? "(no image)";
  console.log(${p.id}: ${p.title.rendered} — ${img});
});
// Output:
// 42: My Latest Post — https://example.com/wp-content/uploads/featured.jpg
// 41: Another Post Title — (no image)



 10,
    '_embed'   => '1',
]);
$ch = curl_init($url);
curl_setopt_array($ch, [CURLOPT_RETURNTRANSFER => true, CURLOPT_USERAGENT => 'wp-client/1.0']);
$posts = json_decode((string) curl_exec($ch), true);
curl_close($ch);
foreach ($posts as $p) {
    $img = $p['_embedded']['wp:featuredmedia'][0]['source_url'] ?? '(no image)';
    printf("%d: %s — %s\n", $p['id'], $p['title']['rendered'], $img);
}


Key insight: ?_embed=1 is the single most useful REST API parameter — it collapses featured image + author + terms into one request. Without it, each post object requires 2–3 additional requests to resolve related data.

The WordPress REST API is at /wp-json/wp/v2/posts. Which query parameter embeds the featured image inline to avoid a second round-trip?

→ web/wordpress-custom-themes
insight beginner

Mobbin: Search 619K+ Real App Screens Instead of Guessing

Mobbin is a searchable library of 619,200+ screens and 141,500+ flows from 1,150+ production iOS and web apps. Instead of asking "how do other apps handle subscription paywalls?", you can filter by pattern and see 20+ real implementations in seconds.

| Resource | Count | |----------|-------| | iOS & Web apps | 1,150+ | | Screens (searchable by text, pattern, element) | 619,200+ | | Flows (video + prototype mode) | 141,500+ |

Key insight: "I finally deleted that folder of 1,866 unorganized screenshots and haven't looked back." (John Bai, Plaid) — The value isn't any single screenshot. It's asking "how do 20 different apps solve this?" and getting production-quality answers in seconds.

Flows show micro-interactions and animations in context — not static screenshots. Prototype mode lets you step through flows at your own pace, revealing transitions that screenshots miss entirely.

For AI-assisted design, Mobbin builds the taste vocabulary that prevents LLM defaults: seeing how production apps actually implement patterns gives you concrete reference points to inject into prompts and design skills.

| |
→ ai-design/mobbin
insight intermediate

agentmemory + agentbrain + akbp — three repos, one production agent stack

The AI Engineering from Scratch curriculum teaches eight agent primitives across Phase XIV. The same author ships three open-source repos that productionize them:

graph TD
    LOOP["🔄 Agent Loop\n(Phase XIV primitives)"]
    MEM["agentmemory ★17.7K\nPersistent state across sessions"]
    BRAIN["agentbrain ★21\nEvidence-first reasoning loop"]
    KB["akbp ★58\nKnowledge queries between sessions"]

    LOOP --> MEM
    LOOP --> BRAIN
    LOOP --> KB


| Repo | Role in the stack | Primitive it ships | |------|------------------|-------------------| | agentmemory | Writes durable state across sessions | Session persistence | | agentbrain | Evidence-first operating system: reason → verify → act | Runtime loop | | akbp | Agent Knowledge Base Protocol — answers knowledge queries between sessions and across agents | HTTP/RPC |

Key insight: The curriculum teaches the primitives bottom-up. The three repos are those same primitives productionized — study the curriculum to understand why each exists, use the repos to ship without reimplementing from scratch.

· ·
→ knowledge-management/ai-engineering-from-scratch
quiz beginner

Skills vs Plugins: When to Build vs Install

| Dimension | Skills | Plugins (MCP Servers) | |-----------|--------|-----------------------| | Format | .md files in .claude/commands/ | Separate server processes | | Ownership | You write and maintain it | Package author maintains it | | Best for | Team conventions, repeatable workflows | External APIs, databases, web search | | Setup time | Slower (writing) | Faster (install + configure) |

Key insight: Most mature setups use both — plugins for infrastructure, skills for workflows. A plugin connects Claude to your database; a skill tells Claude how your team thinks about database migrations.

| |

Your team has a custom PR review convention unique to your codebase — multi-step steps only your team knows. Should you build a skill or install a plugin?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-skills
insight beginner

Claude as learning system architect — not just an information source

Oakley's methods — focused/diffuse toggle, retrieval practice, interleaving — are well-documented but hard to apply. Most learners know the theory and still re-read out of habit. The emerging pattern: use Claude as a learning system architect, not an information source.

The practical loop: 
1. Describe the topic + your current knowledge level to Claude
2. Ask it to structure a study plan using Oakley's focused/diffuse model
3. Request retrieval-practice questions (not re-reading prompts)
4. After each session, report what felt hard → Claude adjusts the schedule
5. Combine with spaced repetition cards for long-term retention


Key insight: This is the mnemonic medium applied to any subject. Quantum Country embedded spaced repetition in a quantum computing text. Claude does this for *any* topic — and adapts in real time as you report difficulty. The 10-prompt pattern (Jaynit Makwana, May 2026, 171K views) puts Oakley's science into a reusable workflow.

| |
→ knowledge-management/spaced-repetition-science
quiz beginner

Nygard ADR Format — Which Section Is Missing?

# {N}. {Title — short noun phrase}

Date: YYYY-MM-DD
Status: Proposed | Accepted | Deprecated | Superseded by ADR-NNN

## Context
What situation forced this decision? What are the forces at play?

## Decision
What did we decide? Active voice: "We will..."

## Consequences
What becomes easier or harder? What technical debt is accepted?


Key insight: Code tells you *what*. Tests tell you *what it's supposed to do*. ADRs tell you *why* — the only layer that prevents future engineers from undoing hard-won choices because the context is gone.

| |

Nygard's 2011 ADR template is the most widely used format. Which of the following is NOT one of its sections?

→ productivity/architectural-decision-records
code intermediate

scheduler.yield() — breaking long tasks for better INP

// ❌ Synchronous — 50ms+ main thread block, browser cannot paint or handle input
function processItems(items: unknown[]) {
  for (const item of items) doWork(item);
}

// ✅ Modern — yields between items; browser processes input and repaints on each yield
async function processItems(items: unknown[]) {
  for (const item of items) {
    doWork(item);
    await scheduler.yield(); // returns control; loop resumes on next task slot
  }
}
// Polyfill: const yieldToMain = () => new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 0));


INP (Interaction to Next Paint) measures the latency from user input to the next frame. Long tasks (>50ms) on the main thread directly cause high INP — the browser can't respond to input until the task finishes. scheduler.yield() surgically splits the task without setTimeout scheduling complexity.

Key insight: The synchronous version and the async version produce identical output — the only difference is *when the browser gets to breathe*. One line (await scheduler.yield()) converts a janky block into a responsive stream.

|

What problem does this code fix, and why does it work?

→ frontend/chrome-modern-web-guidance
quiz beginner

Expert Prompts: Which of the 6 Elements Is Most Commonly Missing?

The 6 elements of every expert prompt — explicit or implied:

| Element | Example | |---------|---------| | Role | "A senior product strategist with 15 years of B2B SaaS experience" | | Context | Industry, audience, constraints, goals | | Task | "Write a competitive analysis across pricing, features, and messaging" | | Format | "A comparison table, followed by a 2-paragraph recommendation" | | Constraints | "No jargon. No generic advice. Under 500 words." | | Quality standard | "Specific enough for a prioritization decision in under 5 minutes" |

Before: "Write a blog post about AI"
→ Generic, average, sounds like everything else

After: All 6 elements applied →
→ Specific, differentiated, actionable


Key insight: The model is not the bottleneck — the prompt is. Two people using the same model on the same task get outputs miles apart in quality. The gap is always the prompt.

| |

An expert prompt contains 6 elements. Which element defines what 'done' looks like and is most often absent from beginner prompts?

→ ai-agents/prompt-engineering-mastery
quiz intermediate

MCP description quality: how bad is it, really?

Description quality is the highest-leverage improvement on any MCP server. The five most common smells:

| Smell | Instead | |-------|---------| | REST wrapper — GET /user + GET /orders | Workflow tool — track_latest_order_by_email | | 50+ tools (10K+ token discovery cost) | 5-10 curated tools | | Developer naming — fetch_v2_data_internal | "Search customer orders by date range, status, or total" | | Nested schemas | Flat primitives | | Silent errors — null | Actionable messages — "Date format wrong; use YYYY-MM-DD" |

Key insight: In a head-to-head study, the server with better descriptions won 72% of the time vs. 20% for the baseline — a 260% lift. The underlying code was identical.

A 2026 study of 856 MCP tools across 103 servers found that what percentage have at least one 'description smell' (vague, missing examples, developer-centric naming)?

→ ai-agents/building-mcp-servers
code intermediate

Zustand v4 TypeScript: why `create<State>()((set) => ...)` not `create<State>((set) => ...)`?

// ❌ Single call — TypeScript widens setter params to unknown (TS2555)
const useStore = create((set) => ({
  transcript: "",
  setTranscript: (transcript) => set({ transcript }), // Error: transcript is unknown
}));

// ✅ Curried double-call — State generic propagates correctly
const useRecognitionStore = create()((set) => ({
  transcript: "",
  isUserSpeaking: false,
  setTranscript: (transcript) => set({ transcript }),    // correctly typed: string
  setSpeaking: (isUserSpeaking) => set({ isUserSpeaking }), // correctly typed: boolean
}));


Key insight: The create()((set) => ...) pattern is required for TypeScript to correctly propagate the State generic through Zustand's curried factory — without it, every setter parameter becomes unknown, defeating type safety across the store.

·

What TypeScript error occurs when you omit the extra `()` in `create<State>()(...)` and write `create<State>(...)`?

→ frontend/zustand-when-hooks-dont-scale
insight advanced

Multi-model parallel code review: use different AI vendors, not just multiple instances

Nolan Lawson's production quality workflow runs three different AI code reviewers in parallel — Claude sub-agent, Codex, and Cursor Bugbot — then collates their findings and rules out false positives before producing a final report.

The key is using different vendors, not just multiple instances of one model. Different training data and RLHF procedures produce different blind spots. A bug that Claude misses, Codex may catch.

Claude sub-agent ──────────┐
Codex           ──────────→ Collate → Rule out false positives → Final report
Cursor Bugbot   ──────────┘


Key insight: Model diversity in review panels works like expert diversity in human review — each reviewer brings different failure modes to the surface. Running the same model N times amplifies its existing blind spots instead of compensating for them.

This extends 's spec-before-code principle: tests verify the contract, multi-model review verifies the implementation quality from orthogonal angles.

Compare with single-vendor parallel review (Split-and-Merge voting): voting across N runs of the same model reduces variance but not systematic blind spots. Vendor diversity reduces both.

→ ai-agents/agent-quality-gates
quiz beginner

Strategic Optimism: Belief as a Skill

Self-doubt is a cognitive filter — your brain actively confirms negative beliefs by directing attention toward failure evidence. Strategic optimism isn't the opposite of realism; it's realism applied to a longer time horizon.

Key insight: Duckworth's grit research found only perseverance (not passion) explains performance. The ceiling on any complex project isn't set by present capability — it's set by willingness to keep building.

|

According to Bandura's research across 114 studies, when does optimal performance occur?

→ productivity/self-belief-strategic-optimism
insight beginner

Code graphs show structure; Understand Anything shows meaning

Most code graph tools output "23 nodes, 34 edges" — structure without a legend. Understand Anything maps code to *business domains*: authentication flows, payment pipelines, user lifecycles. It answers "what does this codebase *do*?" not just "what is connected to what?"

| Tool | Output | |------|--------| | Typical code graph | "23 nodes, 34 edges…" | | Understand Anything | "This is the auth flow. This is the payment pipeline." | | Graphify | 71.5× token reduction for agent context compression |

Install as a Claude Code plugin: /plugin marketplace add Lum1104/Understand-Anything

Key insight: Understand Anything is the *onboarding and comprehension* tool; Graphify and claude-context are *runtime context* tools. Same domain (codebase understanding), completely different jobs.

→ ai-agents/understand-anything
quiz beginner

OpenClaw: AI That Lives in Your Inbox, Not a Separate Tool

OpenClaw connects Telegram, WhatsApp, and Discord to AI workflows with memory, tools, and multi-agent coordination baked in.

flowchart TD
    U1["📱 Telegram"] & U2["💬 WhatsApp"] & U3["🎮 Discord"] -->|trigger / receive| CORE["OpenClaw Core\n(Memory · Tools · Agents)"]
    CORE --> LLM["LLM Backend"]


| Use Case | What the AI Does | |----------|-----------------| | Finance bots | Monitor market news, send Telegram updates | | Remote coding | Control dev workflows from your phone | | Daily briefings | Scheduled summaries, auto-delivered | | Second brain | Capture notes via chat, retrieve via NLP | | Research pipelines | Gather → summarize → organize → report | | Multi-agent systems | Planner + executor + reviewer + reporter |

Key insight: The messaging channel is the interface. No new app to learn — just your existing inbox.

What is OpenClaw's core architectural value proposition compared to traditional AI tools?

→ ai-agents/openclaw
quiz intermediate

MCP vs CLI: when does the simpler option win?

The decision tree is simple:

| Use CLI when… | Use MCP when… | |---------------|---------------| | Local tool, agent has shell access | Remote execution required | | Agent already knows the command | Tool discovery needed (self-describing) | | Simple one-off operation | Per-tool security boundaries needed | | Token cost matters (94% cheaper) | Cross-platform consistency required |

graph TD
    Q1{Is it local + simple +\nagent has trusted shell?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| CLI["✅ Use CLI\n94% cheaper, no overhead"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Need security\nboundaries or remote\nexecution?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| MCP["✅ Use MCP\nTyped, auditable, cross-platform"]
    Q2 -->|No| EITHER["Either works\nPick what's easier to maintain"]
    style CLI fill:#064e3b,color:#fff
    style MCP fill:#1e3a5f,color:#fff


Key insight: MCP servers are products for agents, not APIs for humans. If the agent already navigates your system naturally, adding MCP adds ceremony without value.

One analysis found CLI can be 94% cheaper than MCP (fewer tokens, faster execution). Which scenario is the strongest argument to choose MCP over CLI instead?

→ ai-agents/building-mcp-servers
code intermediate

What does tabIndex={-1} on a dialog enable?

function Modal({ isOpen, onClose }: { isOpen: boolean; onClose: () => void }) {
  const dialogRef = useRef(null)

  useEffect(() => {
    if (isOpen) dialogRef.current?.focus()  // only works because of tabIndex={-1}
  }, [isOpen])

  return isOpen ? (
    

      
    

  ) : null
}


Key insight: The dialog *container* gets tabIndex={-1} to catch programmatic focus. The interactive children (buttons, inputs) are natively focusable and form the tab ring inside the dialog.

|

In a React modal, what does tabIndex={-1} combined with dialogRef.current?.focus() accomplish?

→ frontend/react-accessibility
quiz intermediate

Post-training: what does SFT teach vs. what does DPO teach?

flowchart LR
    PT["🔢 Pretrained Model\nnext-token predictor"] --> SFT["📋 SFT\n(prompt → ideal response)\nteaches instruction-following"]
    SFT --> DPO["⚖️ DPO\n(prompt + chosen + rejected)\nteaches human preferences"]
    DPO --> PROD["🚀 Production Model\nfollows instructions\nrefuses harmful requests"]


| Stage | Training data | Teaches | |-------|--------------|---------| | Pretraining | Trillion tokens of text | Next-token prediction | | SFT | (prompt, ideal response) pairs | Instruction-following, style | | DPO/RLHF | (prompt, chosen, rejected) triples | Human preferences, helpfulness |

Key insight: DPO failures look like outputs that are technically responsive but verbose, sycophantic, or subtly wrong in ways that track the biases of whoever labeled the preference pairs. Knowing the failure mode helps distinguish a prompting issue from something deeper in the training pipeline.

| |

A pretrained model follows instructions inconsistently and sometimes produces sycophantic, verbose responses even when wrong. Which post-training stage addresses each problem?

→ ai-agents/ai-engineering-framework
quiz intermediate

Faithfulness vs. Factuality Hallucination

Matt Pocock's dictionary makes this distinction clinically precise:

| Mode | What it is | Fix | |------|-----------|-----| | Faithfulness hallucination | Output drifts from loaded context | Clear / compact session | | Factuality hallucination | Invents facts never in any source | Load correct contextual knowledge | | Sycophancy | Confidently agreeable output | Phrase prompts neutrally |

Key insight: "The model hallucinated" is vague. "Faithfulness hallucination — I'm in the dumb zone" is actionable. Precision vocabulary turns random debugging into a root-cause workflow.

A model references a function from documentation you pasted earlier — but gets the API signature completely wrong. The documentation is definitely in context. Which hallucination type is this?

→ ai-agents/dictionary-of-ai-coding
insight intermediate

The security lethal trifecta for AI agents

Simon Willison's "lethal trifecta" — the three conditions that make AI agents exploitable:

1. Access to private data — source code, API keys, secrets 2. Exposure to untrusted content — files the agent reads (prompt injection vectors) 3. Ability to externally communicate — network access for data exfiltration

Key insight: "Anyone who gets text into your LLM has full control over what tools it runs next." Breaking any ONE leg of the trifecta neutralizes the attack.

Most practical mitigation: Network isolation (HTTP proxy allowlisting, container sandboxing) — filesystem access and untrusted content are often unavoidable for useful work, but exfiltration channels can be cut.

Managed platforms () provide container sandboxing by default, making this an infrastructure concern rather than a developer burden.

|
→ ai-agents/agent-quality-gates
insight intermediate

Why did Anthropic's ≤25-word limit cause a 3% quality drop in Claude Code?

In April 2026, Anthropic added "keep text between tool calls to ≤25 words" to Claude Code's system prompt. Internal evals looked fine — but a broader ablation suite showed a 3% coding quality drop. The constraint wasn't just trimming filler — it was suppressing intermediate reasoning.

// ❌ Fragile — word budget suppresses reasoning chains
"Keep text between tool calls to ≤25 words."

// ✅ Robust — structural guidance without a token budget
"Be concise. Skip preamble and filler. Focus on the task."


Key insight: Verbosity constraints interact with reasoning quality in non-obvious ways. A formatting preference that looks harmless can prevent the model from articulating the intermediate steps it needs to produce correct output.

Prefer structural verbosity guidance (be concise, skip preamble) over raw word counts in system prompts. The "≤N words" form is fragile — it trades quality for brevity without you noticing until evals catch it.

→ ai-agents/claude-code-april-2026-postmortem
insight advanced

Temporal bucket fusion = knowledge compilation for video

Netflix's video search synchronizes billions of AI model outputs (character recognition, scene detection, dialogue analysis) into sub-second queries by pre-computing intersections across one-second time buckets — rather than cross-referencing raw model outputs at query time.

flowchart LR
    CH["Character\n'Joey'"] & SC["Scene\n'kitchen'"] -->|second 4| Fused["Fused bucket\n'Joey in kitchen'"]
    Fused -->|query| Result["✅ Search < 1s"]


Key insight: This is applied to perception — the same architectural principle that makes RAG faster than runtime LLM reasoning. Pre-compute the hard part so queries just look up answers.

Multimodal reliability comes from uncorrelated failure modes: when a character recognition model fails, the scene detection model often doesn't, so the ensemble is more reliable than any single modality.

· ·
→ ai-agents/multimodal-agent-search
quiz intermediate

Agent Team Hooks: How do you block a task from completing?

{
  "hooks": {
    "TeammateIdle":  [{ "type": "command", "command": "npm run lint" }],
    "TaskCreated":   [{ "type": "command", "command": "./validate-scope.sh" }],
    "TaskCompleted": [{ "type": "command", "command": "./run-tests.sh" }]
  }
}


| Hook | Fires when | Exit 2 effect | |---|---|---| | TeammateIdle | Teammate between tasks | Keeps teammate working with feedback | | TaskCreated | Lead creates a task | Prevents task entering the queue | | TaskCompleted | Teammate marks complete | Blocks completion; teammate revises |

Key insight: These hooks configure in the same settings.json as standard PostToolUse hooks. Together they give quality enforcement at both the tool level and the team coordination level.

In Claude Code agent teams, what exit code should a quality-gate hook return to block a state transition and send corrective feedback to the teammate?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-agent-teams
quiz intermediate

Claude Code: what makes agent teams different from subagents?

| Dimension        | Subagents              | Agent Teams                         |
|------------------|------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Communication    | Report to caller only  | Teammates message each other        |
| Context          | Own; results summarize | Own; fully independent              |
| Coordination     | Main agent manages all | Shared task list, self-coordinating |
| Token cost       | Lower                  | Higher (each = full Claude)         |


Key insight: Use subagents when only the result matters. Use teams when teammates need to share findings, challenge each other, and iterate autonomously.

In Claude Code, what is the key difference between subagents and agent teammates?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-agent-teams
insight intermediate

Pretext: prepare() once, layout() many times — text measurement without DOM reflow

Pretext separates text measurement into two phases. prepare() does the expensive work once: Unicode segmentation (Intl.Segmenter), bidi analysis, and Canvas 2D measureText for font metrics — stored in an opaque handle. Every subsequent layout(width, lineHeight) is pure arithmetic over cached widths — no DOM, no reflow.

// Phase 1: once — segmentation + canvas measureText
const prepared = prepare('AGI 春天到了. بدأت 🚀', '16px Inter')

// Phase 2: pure arithmetic — on every resize
const { height, lineCount } = layout(prepared, containerWidth, 20)


getBoundingClientRect and offsetHeight both trigger full layout reflow. A masonry grid measuring 200 text boxes without Pretext triggers 200 reflows; with Pretext, zero.

Key insight: On resize, only layout() reruns — prepare() was already done. This makes Pretext especially efficient for responsive layouts that re-measure constantly.

→ frontend/pretext-text-layout
code advanced

Ripple Check: PostToolUse Hook for Domain-Specific Consistency

From rjs/shaping-skills — a Shape Up methodology skill set for Claude Code:

{
  "hooks": {
    "PostToolUse": [{
      "matcher": "Write|Edit",
      "hooks": [{
        "type": "command",
        "command": "grep -q 'shaping: true' \"$CLAUDE_FILE_PATH\" 2>/dev/null && echo 'Ripple Check: update affordance tables, fit checks, and work streams.' || true",
        "timeout": 5
      }]
    }]
  }
}


How it works: 1. Claude edits any file → hook fires 2. grep -q 'shaping: true' — checks if the file is a shaping document 3. Shaping doc: prints "Ripple Check: update affordance tables, fit checks, and work streams." 4. Any other file: || true → exits cleanly, no output, no interruption

Key insight: This is the PostToolUse pattern for domain-specific enforcement. Hooks don't have to fire for every file — they can read the file that was just written and respond conditionally. "Hooks are process discipline made automatic."

The general pattern: grep -q 'marker: true' "$CLAUDE_FILE_PATH" && echo 'Do X' || true — any frontmatter marker can trigger domain-specific reminders.

| |

What does the Ripple Check hook do when Claude edits a file WITHOUT 'shaping: true' in its frontmatter?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-skills
insight intermediate

Context Mode: intercept tool output before it fills the context window

The highest-impact compression technique works on the input side — preventing raw tool output from entering the context window at all.

The problem in numbers: | Tool call | Raw output | After Context Mode | |-----------|-----------|-------------------| | Playwright snapshot | 56.2 KB | 299 B | | 20 GitHub issues | 58.9 KB | 1.1 KB | | 500-row access log | 45.1 KB | 155 B | | Full 30-min session | 315 KB | 5.4 KB |

Context Mode intercepts MCP tool calls via PreToolUse hooks, processes raw data in a sandboxed subprocess, and returns only the structured summary. The LLM never sees the raw DOM, raw JSON, or raw log lines.

/plugin marketplace add mksglu/context-mode
/plugin install context-mode@context-mode


Result: Session life extends from ~30 minutes to ~3 hours without hitting the context limit (98% input reduction).

Key insight: Caveman compresses what Claude *says* (output). Context Mode prevents Claude from ever *seeing* bloated data (input). The biggest wins come from stacking both sides simultaneously.

| |
→ ai-agents/compression-strategies
insight intermediate

FSRS adapts to your personal forgetting curve — SM-2 (Anki) treats everyone identically

Anki's SM-2 algorithm (1987) uses a fixed ease-factor multiplier. Everyone studying the same card gets the same interval schedule. FSRS v6 (2022, ACM SIGKDD) replaces ease factors with machine-learned weights trained on your review history — it adapts to your personal forgetting curve.

| Property | SM-2 (Anki) | FSRS v6 (ts-fsrs) | |----------|-------------|-------------------| | Memory model | Interval multiplier | Stability + Retrievability | | Personalization | None — same schedule for all | Trainable on your review log | | Research backing | 1987 (SuperMemo) | [Ye et al. 2022, ACM SIGKDD](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3534678.3539081) |

import { fsrs } from 'ts-fsrs'

// FSRS uses 17 learnable weights (w[0]–w[16]).
// Default: population average. After training on your reviews:
// → Slow learner: longer early intervals for consolidation
// → Fast learner: skip to multi-day intervals sooner
const scheduler = fsrs({ w: yourPersonalizedWeights })


Key insight: SM-2's ease factor adjusts per-card, but the underlying model is the same for everyone. FSRS's machine-learned weights, trained on millions of review logs, schedule your 90% retention threshold — not a population average. The result: difficult material gets the spaced attention it needs instead of being over-scheduled by a generic ease multiplier.

→ knowledge-management/spaced-repetition-code
quiz beginner

When should you build a custom MCP client?

graph LR
    subgraph Standard["Standard — use existing client"]
        CC["Claude Code"] -->|"MCP"| S1["Any MCP server"]
        CU["Cursor"] -->|"MCP"| S1
    end
    subgraph Custom["Custom client — build your own"]
        APP["Your app\n(embedded loop)"] -->|"MCP"| S2["Your server"]
        APP -->|"MCP"| S3["Another server"]
    end
    style Standard fill:#1f2937,color:#9ca3af
    style Custom fill:#064e3b,color:#fff


Key insight: The Python client uses AsyncExitStack and the TypeScript client uses mcp.close() — both patterns ensure the server subprocess is killed when the client exits.

| |

Most developers don't need a custom MCP client — Claude Code, Cursor, and similar tools already implement the protocol. Which scenario DOES warrant building one?

→ ai-agents/mcp-client-development
insight beginner

Sandcastle decouples orchestration from agent execution

Sandcastle's core value is not the sandbox — it's the decoupling. You write the prompt and pick a provider; Sandcastle handles isolation, branch management, and structured result collection. Results come back as { commits, branch, iterations }.

// AFK pipeline: run an agent unattended for every open issue
for (const issue of issues) {
  const result = await run({
    agent: claudeCode("claude-opus-4-7"),
    sandbox: docker(),
    branchStrategy: { type: "branch", branch: agent/${issue.number} },
    prompt: issue.body,
  });
  console.log(Fixed in: ${result.commits.map(c => c.sha).join(", ")});
}


Key insight: Agent changes land on a named branch, not directly on main — quality gate by architecture. The human reviews and merges; the agent does not self-merge.

This is the complement to Claude Code's manual AFK ralph pattern — Sandcastle adds structure, provider abstraction, and structured result collection.

→ ai-agents/sandcastle
quiz beginner

scroll() vs view() — which CSS timeline for which effect?

/* scroll() — progress bar tracks page scroll position */
.reading-progress {
  animation: progress linear;
  animation-timeline: scroll(root);
}

/* view() — fade in as element enters viewport */
.card {
  animation: fade-in linear both;
  animation-timeline: view();
  animation-range: entry 0% entry 30%;
}


| Effect | Timeline | |--------|----------| | Reading progress bar | scroll(root) | | Shrinking sticky header | scroll(root) | | Fade-in on element enter | view() | | Stacking cards | view() |

Key insight: Both run off the main thread — no JavaScript, no scroll listeners. The browser handles geometry asynchronously.

|

You want a reading progress bar that fills as the user scrolls the full page. Which CSS Scroll-driven Animation timeline do you use?

→ frontend/scroll-driven-animations
quiz intermediate

When Should a Coding Agent Call the Advisor?

The Advisor Tool (beta, April 2026) pairs a cheap executor (Sonnet/Haiku) with a smarter advisor (Opus) inside a single API call. The executor does all generation; advisor only plans and course-corrects.

Key insight: Make the deliverable durable *before* the final advisor call. If the session ends during advisor inference, a written file persists — an unwritten one doesn't.

// The three right moments to call the advisor (mentally, not in code):
// 1. After reading files, BEFORE writing — "what's the best approach?"
// 2. When stuck — "I've tried X and Y, suggest a different angle"
// 3. After committing — "review what I built, anything missing?"


| |

According to Anthropic's Advisor Tool best practices, the executor should call the advisor at three key moments. Which option correctly lists all three?

→ ai-agents/advisor-tool
quiz beginner

AGENTS.md vs CLAUDE.md — Why the Filename Matters

AGENTS.md is read before every task — it's the difference between an AI that knows your conventions and one that invents them. The filename choice determines whether that investment is locked to one tool or portable across your entire team's toolchain.

| File | Supported by | |------|-------------| | CLAUDE.md | Claude Code only | | AGENTS.md | 55+ tools — Claude, Codex, Gemini, Copilot, Cursor, Windsurf… |

Effective AGENTS.md content is general and actionable:

## Code Style
- Use Yoda conditions: if (null === $value)
- Use strict comparisons only (===, !==)

## Verification Commands
- php vendor/bin/phpstan analyse — type errors
- php vendor/bin/phpunit — test suite


Key insight: The file is only as good as what's in it. Vague rules ("check everything well") are useless; specific, runnable verification commands are the highest-value entry.

| |

Why does the Claude Code for Symfony guide recommend naming your project context file AGENTS.md instead of CLAUDE.md?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-workflows
insight intermediate

RAG chunking: recursive 512-token wins at 69% — semantic chunking underperforms

FloTorch benchmark (Feb 2026): 7 chunking strategies, 50 academic papers, 905k tokens.

| Strategy | Accuracy | Why | |----------|----------|-----| | Recursive 512 tokens, 50–100 overlap | 69% | Default — best overall balance | | Fixed-size 256 tokens | ~65% | Lower context per chunk | | Semantic chunking | 54% | Produced 43-token fragments — too small for meaningful retrieval | | HTML/Markdown-aware | Better than recursive for structured docs | Preserves heading context | | Late chunking | Good for intra-document context | Embed full doc, derive chunk vectors via mean pooling |

Key insight: Bigger is not always better — there's a context cliff at ~2.5k tokens (Bennani & Moslonka, arXiv:2601.14123). Quality degrades beyond this threshold even if the model's context window is larger. Aim for 512 tokens with overlap.

Special case: Splitting tables as plain text destroys column-header relationships. Extract tables as Markdown or JSON and embed a description alongside the structured content.

Default strategy: recursive 512-token chunks with 50–100 token overlap. Only deviate for structured content (code, tables, HTML).

| |
→ ai-agents/rag-advanced
insight intermediate

Parasocial Trust in AI — why conversational AI makes users comply with requests they'd refuse from a faceless system

Human brains evolved to build trust through conversation. When AI speaks naturally, users unconsciously apply the same trust heuristics they use for people — without the social context that normally calibrates that trust.

The result: users overshare personal information and comply with requests from a conversational AI that they would refuse from a web form or a faceless system.

| Cue | What triggers parasocial trust | Why it's dangerous | |-----|-------------------------------|-------------------| | Natural language responses | Feels like talking to a person | Users lower guard, share more than needed | | Empathy / warmth signals | "I understand this is difficult…" | Compliance with high-stakes requests | | Persona continuity | Remembered preferences, named identity | Users attribute relationship + loyalty | | Confident tone | No hedging, direct answers | Automation bias compounds parasocial trust |

Design mitigations (The Decision Lab / NN/g): - Avoid false intimacy cues — no warmth theater when the AI has no actual relationship with the user - Be explicit about AI identity at points of escalating trust (before data collection, before high-stakes confirmations) - Design friction at high-stakes decisions — slow down the moment, break the conversational flow, make the stakes legible

Key insight: The most capable conversational AI systems carry the highest parasocial trust risk. The design challenge is not to make AI seem less human — it's to be honest about what the relationship actually is.

→ ai-design/ai-ux-psychology
quiz beginner

Matuschak's four principles for writing good spaced repetition cards

Poor spaced repetition cards create an illusion of learning without the substance. Matuschak's four principles:

| Principle | Bad | Good | |-----------|-----|------| | Atomic | "What is FSRS?" | "What does the S in FSRS stand for?" | | Precise | "How does spaced repetition work?" | "Why does reviewing 1 day after learning beat re-reading on day 0?" | | Connected | Isolated fact | Fact + why it matters + what it connects to | | Personal | Generic question | Question framed around your actual use case |

Key insight: The goal of spaced repetition is not to memorize facts — it's to build *working knowledge* where facts combine fluidly. Poorly written cards create pattern matching, not understanding.

Which card demonstrates the 'atomic' principle in Matuschak's card-writing framework?

→ knowledge-management/spaced-repetition-science
quiz intermediate

GPS: Why does your phone need 4 satellites, not 3?

c = 299,792,458 m/s
1 ns of clock drift → 0.3 m of position error
100 ns of quartz drift → 30 m of error

With 4 satellites, solve: √[(x − xᵢ)² + (y − yᵢ)² + (z − zᵢ)²] = ρᵢ + c · δt
Four unknowns: (x, y, z, δt) — position + clock correction.


Key insight: GPS gives your phone a free atomic clock. The fourth satellite isn't for redundancy — it's what makes cheap quartz receivers accurate to meters instead of kilometers.

The same Gauss-Newton linearization that solves the position also recovers the exact clock offset δt, synchronizing your phone to nanosecond precision as a mathematical side effect of computing your location.

|

Three satellites are mathematically sufficient to compute a 3D position via trilateration. Why does GPS require a fourth satellite?

→ tech/how-gps-works
insight intermediate

/domain-model challenges fuzzy language against your actual codebase

The /domain-model skill reads CONTEXT.md (glossary), CONTEXT-MAP.md (bounded contexts), and docs/adr/ before engaging with any plan. When you use a vague term like "account," it immediately asks: "Do you mean Customer, User, Organization, or Workspace?" — then updates CONTEXT.md inline as terms resolve.

ADRs are written only when a decision meets all three criteria: hard to reverse, surprising without context, and came from a real trade-off. This keeps the ADR log signal-rich rather than exhaustive.

/domain-model     ← default front-of-chain; reads codebase, aligns language
/grill-me         ← targeted pressure-test; adversarial questions, no domain modeling
/grill-with-docs  ← both: grilling + builds CONTEXT.md + writes ADRs


> Key insight (2026 update): Start with /domain-model by default — not /grill-me. Use grill-me only when you specifically want adversarial questioning without full domain modeling. Once CONTEXT.md exists, every future session is faster because the agent has a more concise language to think in.

· ·
→ ai-agents/claude-code-skills
insight advanced

Brevity sometimes improves LLM accuracy — not just saves tokens

Conventional wisdom: more tokens → better reasoning. Research ([arXiv:2604.00025, 2026](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.00025)) complicates this.

On 7.7% of benchmark problems, larger models underperformed smaller ones due to scale-dependent verbosity. Constraining those models to brief responses improved their accuracy by 26 percentage points on those problems — while also cutting token cost.

| Scenario | Brevity effect | |----------|---------------| | Status updates, progress reports | ✅ Compress — reduces hallucination surface, no loss | | Complex reasoning chains | ❌ Don't compress — models need token budget to think | | Code generation output | ❌ Don't compress — completeness matters | | Code review comments | ✅ Compress — verbosity adds noise, not signal |

The mechanism: verbose responses on straightforward tasks create *hallucination surface area* — the model fills space with plausible-sounding but incorrect elaboration. Brevity constraints cut the elaboration path before it diverges.

Key insight: Caveman-style compression is not purely a cost optimization. On the right task types, it's also a quality improvement — verbose models sometimes talk themselves into wrong answers.

| |
→ ai-agents/compression-strategies
quiz beginner

Working memory holds 4±1 chunks — not 7±2

![Working memory limits — humans hold 4±1 meaningful chunks before cognitive overload](https://picsum.photos/seed/memory-cognitive-load-chunks-ux/800/400)

Miller (1956): 7 ± 2 items
Cowan (2001):  4 ± 1 chunks  ← design target


| Principle | Application | |-----------|------------| | Reduce cognitive load | Show one thing at a time; hide complexity behind progressive disclosure | | Recognition over recall | Show options rather than requiring freeform input | | Chunking | Group related items so the group reads as one unit, not five |
