Key insight: The 7±2 myth persists in design guidelines everywhere. Cowan's revision — 4±1 — is the number that actually matters for UI design. When a form has 8 fields visible at once, or a menu has 12 options, users don't fail because of the 7±2 limit; they fail because of the 4±1 limit. Respect cognitive load by keeping the active decision space to 4 meaningful items.

Miller (1956) proposed 7±2 items in working memory. Cowan (2001) revised this. What is the current consensus, and what is the primary UX design response?

→ ai-design/ai-ux-psychology
insight intermediate

The Chain Method: Why 5 Focused Prompts Beat 1 Complex Prompt

Never ask an LLM to do 5 things in one prompt. Chain them — each focused step compounds quality, and you can review and correct between stages.

Prompt 1: "Research the top 5 competitors in construction project management."

Prompt 2: "Using this analysis: [paste]
           Identify the 3 biggest positioning gaps no competitor fills."

Prompt 3: "Based on these gaps: [paste]
           Write a positioning statement claiming one gap explicitly."

Prompt 4: "Based on this positioning: [paste]
           Write the homepage headline, subheadline, and three supporting points."


Why it works: Each step has a shorter failure chain. Quality compounds because each input is already validated. Correction is possible at each stage — impossible with a monolithic prompt.

This mirrors the insight in reverse: the p^n reliability problem applies in both directions. Fewer sequential dependent steps = higher probability of quality output at each stage.

Key insight: Monolithic prompts ask for 5 things → the weakest step contaminates all downstream steps. The chain method isolates failures so they can be fixed before they compound.

The meta-prompt extension: When you don't know how to write the first prompt, ask Claude to write it: "I want to accomplish [goal]. Write me the most effective prompt to achieve this result." The generated prompt almost always catches your blindspots.

| |
→ ai-agents/prompt-engineering-mastery
quiz beginner

What is the core lesson loop in AI Engineering from Scratch?

| Typical AI tutorials | AI Engineering from Scratch | |---|---| | Framework-first | Math-first | | 5-min copy-paste | 75-min derive → code → test | | "This works" | "Here's *why* this works" |

Key insight: By the time PyTorch shows up in this curriculum, you already know what it's doing under the hood — you've derived the loss function, the backprop algorithm, and the attention mechanism from raw math.

· ·

AI Engineering from Scratch follows a 4-step lesson loop. What is it?

→ knowledge-management/ai-engineering-from-scratch
insight intermediate

Almost no production system should use just one LLM tier

Five practical LLM tiers define the 2026 model landscape. The key principle: route cheap requests to cheap models. Using a frontier model for every request is like using a surgeon to change a lightbulb.

| Tier | Examples (mid-2026) | When to use | |------|---------------------|-------------| | Closed frontier | Claude Opus 4.8, GPT-5.5, Gemini 3.1 Pro | Hard reasoning, planning, complex evals | | Closed mid-tier | Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Pro | Most production tasks — the workhorse tier | | Closed cheap | Claude Haiku 4.5, Gemini Flash-Lite | High-volume: classification, routing, short summaries | | Open weights | Llama 4, Gemma, Mistral, DeepSeek | Data residency, cost at scale, fine-tuning | | Specialized | Voyage embeddings, Cohere rerankers | Embedding, reranking, domain-specific tasks |

Key insight: Benchmarks lie — models are trained on them. The only reliable signal is your own eval set. Pick by task fit, cost at projected volume, latency, context window, JSON mode support, and data residency.

The routing pattern: classify request complexity first (Haiku, ~0.001 cost) → route hard tasks to frontier, easy tasks to cheap tier. A 10× cost difference between tiers makes routing worth the engineering investment at scale.

| |
→ ai-agents/ai-engineering-framework
connection intermediate

Markov chains appear in three core AI architectures

The Markov chain — a process where the next state depends only on the current state — is the mathematical skeleton underlying three major AI paradigms:

Language models   →  Markov chain: each token sampled from P(next | context)
Diffusion models  →  forward pass = Brownian motion (adding noise)
                     reverse pass = learned Markov chain (removing noise)
RL agents         →  Markov Decision Processes (state, action, reward, next state)


Key insight: You don't need to wait until Phase XIV (Agent Engineering) to understand agents. The math is all in Phase I. The stationary distribution of a Markov chain (via eigendecomposition) is the same concept that underlies MCMC, Bayesian inference, and diffusion model training.

# Stationary distribution via eigendecomposition — eigenvector for λ=1 is the long-run state
eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eig(transition.T)
idx = np.argmin(np.abs(eigenvalues - 1))
stationary = np.real(eigenvectors[:, idx]) / np.real(eigenvectors[:, idx]).sum()


·
→ knowledge-management/ai-engineering-from-scratch
code intermediate

Read anyone's public data from AT Protocol without API keys

AT Protocol's key innovation: your data lives on a Personal Data Server (PDS) you control, addressable via at:// URIs. Any app can read your public records without platform permission.

import { BskyAgent } from "@atproto/api";
const agent = new BskyAgent({ service: "https://bsky.social" });

// Read raw records by Lexicon namespace — cross-app activity
const posts = await agent.com.atproto.repo.listRecords({
  repo: "alice.com",           // domain handle = DID resolver
  collection: "app.bsky.feed.post",
});
// Output: last posts from alice.com's PDS — no OAuth, no API key


The same PDS holds records from multiple apps (app.bsky.*, h.tangled.*, pub.leaflet.*) — one repository, many clients.

What method reads posts directly from Alice's Personal Data Server?

→ web/open-social
code intermediate

MCP path traversal: what's the missing check?

The most common MCP implementation vulnerability. Schema validation rejects traversal chars at the input layer; startsWith(BASE_DIR + sep) enforces the allow-list boundary after resolution.

const BASE_DIR = path.resolve("/app/data");

server.tool("read_report",
  "Read a report. Example: { filename: 'Q1-2026.md' }",
  { filename: z.string().regex(/^[\w-]+\.(?:md|txt)$/) },
  async ({ filename }) => {
    const resolved = path.resolve(BASE_DIR, filename);
    // MISSING CHECK HERE
    const content = await fs.readFile(resolved, "utf-8");
    return { content: [{ type: "text", text: content }] };
  }
);


Key insight: Fail closed — anything that can't be verified as safe gets blocked. Actionable errors ("access outside allowed directory") help the agent understand exactly what went wrong.

This MCP tool handler resolves a filename inside BASE_DIR. What critical check is missing before reading the file?

→ ai-agents/mcp-server-security
code advanced

SDK-level nested orchestrator: what does the root receive after fan-out resolves?

Claude Code's Agent tool automates nested spawning. The underlying SDK pattern is the same: child contexts do noisy work, only distilled verdicts flow upward.

async function investigate(hypothesis: string): Promise {
  const res = await client.messages.create({
    model: "claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens: 256,
    system: "Respond with CONFIRMED, REFUTED, or INCONCLUSIVE — then one sentence of evidence.",
    messages: [{ role: "user", content: hypothesis }],
  });
  return res.content[0].type === "text" ? res.content[0].text : "INCONCLUSIVE";
}

// Root: concurrent fan-out, distilled verdicts only
const verdicts = await Promise.all(hypotheses.map(investigate));
// ["CONFIRMED — DB index dropped.", "INCONCLUSIVE — Cache stable.", "REFUTED — API baseline unchanged."]


Key insight: The root context sees only three one-line strings — never the raw data inside each child. Claude Code's 5-level hard cap limits how many times this isolation can recurse.

| |

After Promise.all resolves across three child investigators, what does the root orchestrator receive?

→ ai-agents/nested-subagents
quiz intermediate

What are the three pillars of Harness Engineering?

Without a harness, AI agents produce code that individually looks fine but collectively drifts away from your architecture. The Harness Engineer is the role the industry needs now that agents genuinely act autonomously.

Harness = Context engineering
        + Architectural constraints (linters, watchdogs, hooks)
        + Entropy management (drift detection agents)


Key insight: Currently absorbed by platform engineers and staff/principal engineers. Will emerge as an explicit role at companies running autonomous coding agents at scale.

Birgitta Böckeler's Harness Engineer role keeps autonomous AI agents under architectural control. Which three pillars define it?

→ ai-agents/ai-roles-career-paths
quiz intermediate

Agent Protocol Stack — Which Protocol for Which Problem?

The 6-protocol agent stack is additive — each protocol has exactly one job:

| You need to… | Add | |---|---| | Connect to databases, APIs, files | MCP | | Delegate to remote specialist agents | A2A | | Transact with suppliers or marketplaces | UCP | | Add authorization + audit trail to payments | AP2 | | Render dynamic, interactive UI | A2UI | | Stream agent events to a frontend | AG-UI |

Key insight: Most agents start with just MCP, then add protocols as requirements grow. Don't add all 6 upfront — the stack is designed for incremental adoption.

| |

Your agent needs to place an order with an external supplier AND ensure there's a cryptographic audit trail of who approved the purchase. Which protocol combination handles this?

→ ai-agents/agent-protocol-stack
quiz intermediate

The four pillars of Agent Experience (AX)

graph TD
    AX["Agent Experience (AX)"]
    A["1. ACCESS\nAuth, onboarding, anonymous deploy"]
    C["2. CONTEXT\nDocs, MCP, CLI design, skills, LLMS.txt"]
    T["3. TOOLS\nCLI, API, SDK, MCP servers"]
    O["4. ORCHESTRATION\nBidirectional: product→agent AND agent→product"]
    AX --> A & C & T & O
    style AX fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style O fill:#d97706,color:#fff


Key insight: AX is the successor to DX. "Developer Experience was our North Star for 10 years. Agent Experience will be our North Star for the next decade." — Matt Biilmann, Netlify

Netlify's bet on AX (Jan 2025): 10× daily signups, 500× new paying customers. Products not optimized for agents become invisible — there's now an agent between the user and the product.

| |

Matt Biilmann (Netlify) defines AX across four pillars. Which pillar covers 'the bidirectional loop — your product kicking off agents AND agents working with the product'?

→ ai-agents/agent-experience-ax
quiz intermediate

A/B Testing Agent Versions Before Deploy

Most agent workflows test in production — deploy, observe, iterate. agents-cli eval introduces a pre-deploy quality gate: run structured evals, compare results, decide.

agents-cli eval run --output v1-results.json   # eval current version
# ... make changes ...
agents-cli eval run --output v2-results.json   # eval new version
agents-cli eval compare v1-results.json v2-results.json  # diff before deploy


Key insight: LLM-as-judge + trajectory scoring means you evaluate the full reasoning path — not just the final answer. A change that produces the right answer via a flawed path still fails the eval.

|

What does `agents-cli eval compare` let you do that most agent dev workflows don't?

→ ai-agents/google-agents-cli
insight intermediate

AI Vulnerability Discovery: the Bottleneck Shifted

One month after launching Project Glasswing, Anthropic reported 10,000+ high/critical vulnerabilities across 50 partner organizations. Cloudflare alone received 2,000 bugs (400 high/critical) — and some open-source maintainers asked Anthropic to slow down disclosures because they couldn't process findings fast enough.

> "Progress on software security used to be limited by how quickly we could find new vulnerabilities. Now it's limited by how quickly we can verify, disclose, and patch the large numbers of vulnerabilities found by AI." — Anthropic, May 2026

This represents a structural shift: for decades, finding was the bottleneck. Claude Mythos moved the bottleneck to human triage and remediation capacity. The UK AI Security Institute found Mythos Preview is the first model to solve *both* of their multistep cyberattack simulations end to end.

The Glasswing open-source scan produced 23,019 total findings; 1,587 confirmed true positives (90.6% precision); 75 publicly patched with CVE advisories.

Key insight: The most significant implication isn't the number of bugs found — it's that human review capacity has become the new rate-limiting step in software security. AI can find vulnerabilities faster than humans can triage, verify, and patch them.

| |
→ ai-security/glasswing-project
insight intermediate

Spec-Kit's [P] Markers Collapse Build Time by Running Agents in Parallel

After spec + plan, /speckit.tasks produces a tasks.md with every task in dependency order. Tasks safe to run concurrently are marked [P] — "parallel." An agent running /speckit.implement uses these markers to fire independent tasks simultaneously rather than waiting for each to finish.

# tasks.md (excerpt)
## Tier 1 (no dependencies)
- [P] Create User model (src/models/user.ts)
- [P] Create Post model (src/models/post.ts)
- [P] Write User model tests (src/models/user.test.ts)

## Tier 2 (depends on Tier 1)
- [ ] Create UserService (src/services/user.ts)
- [ ] Create PostService (src/services/post.ts)


Key insight: The [P] markers are a structural solution to wall-clock time, not just a hint. Agents that can execute in parallel worktrees (spec-kit-worktree-parallel extension) use these markers to collapse multi-hour sequential builds into minutes — each tier gate also catches errors before they cascade into dependent tasks.

The dependency ordering (models before services, services before endpoints, tests before implementation) mirrors the same tier-based approach Cavekit uses with /ck:map. Both treat task ordering as an architectural artifact, not an afterthought.

| |
→ ai-agents/spec-driven-development
quiz intermediate

A2UI: Safe Like Data, Expressive Like Code

A2UI defines 17 standard UI components (Text, Card, Form, Tabs, etc.) that agents can compose via JSON. The Emit-Render-Signal-Reason loop: agent emits UI → client renders → user interacts → client signals back → agent reasons about the interaction.

Key insight: Google ships both A2UI (open protocol for any agent) and Gemini's native generative UI (their own product) simultaneously. Dual investment validates that structured agent UI is the future.

|

What is A2UI's core security principle?

→ ai-design/a2ui-protocol
code intermediate

What does the scorer function return in an eval harness?

The eval harness pattern: each test case carries its own scorer function; the loop collects numeric scores.

interface EvalCase {
  id: string;
  input: string;
  score: (output: string) => number; // 0.0–1.0
}

async function runEvals(evalSet: EvalCase[]) {
  let total = 0;
  for (const ec of evalSet) {
    const output = await callLLM(ec.input);
    const score  = ec.score(output);
    total += score;
  }
  // "87% pass rate" — not "case 3 failed"
  console.log(Pass rate: ${((total / evalSet.length) * 100).toFixed(1)}%);
}


Key insight: The scorer *is* the eval. For model regression use exact-match; for safety use regex against a toxicity list; for quality use an LLM-as-judge. The harness stays the same — only the scorer changes.

·

What should `score` return — and what does the harness aggregate it into?

→ ai-agents/ai-evals-llm-testing
quiz intermediate

RCT Crowd Simulation — Same Outcome, Fraction of the Cost

Naive approach:  O(n²) collision checks per frame
                 1,000 guests = 1,000,000 checks per frame (infeasible in 1999)

RCT's approach: Each guest counts nearby guests (proximity count)
                If crowded → happiness penalty → guest complaint
                Player still manages crowd flow — via the same signals


Key insight: This is the "approximate algorithm" principle: choose a cheaper model that produces the same *observable outcome* for the player/user, not the same *internal simulation*. The player never sees collision geometry — they see guest happiness. Optimize the output, not the mechanism.

Modern analog: approximate nearest-neighbor search over exact k-NN; aggregate stats over per-agent state; cached results over recomputed values.

| |

How did RollerCoaster Tycoon simulate 2,000+ guests on 1999 hardware without O(n²) collision detection?

→ productivity/game-design-for-performance
quiz beginner

UI Skills as Design Taste Correction Layers

LLMs produce generic AI aesthetics by default. The [UI Skills directory](https://www.ui-skills.com/) is a set of Claude Code skills that layer specific forms of craft on top:

flowchart TD
    A["🤖 LLM default\n(generic AI aesthetics)"] --> B["+ baseline-ui\nTypography & animation guardrails"]
    B --> C["+ fixing-accessibility\nWCAG correction"]
    C --> D["+ emil-design-eng\nCraft & polish"]
    D --> E["✅ Production-grade\nIntentional interface"]


Key insight: These skills don't make UI generation faster — they make it intentional. Pablo Stanley's framing: design taste is deliberate rejection of defaults. UI Skills is the toolkit for that rejection.

What is the core philosophy behind the UI Skills directory for Claude Code?

→ ai-design/ui-skills
quiz intermediate

AGENTS.md Instruction Budget: Bloated vs. Minimal Token Cost

flowchart LR
    subgraph Bloated["❌ Bloated AGENTS.md"]
        B1["~500 lines · ~4,000 tokens\nloaded on every request"]
        B2["~2,000 tokens remain\nfor actual task work"]
    end
    subgraph Minimal["✅ Minimal AGENTS.md"]
        M1["~3 lines · ~50 tokens\nloaded on every request"]
        M2["~6,000 tokens remain\nfor actual task work"]
    end
    style Bloated fill:#7f1d1d,color:#fff
    style Minimal fill:#064e3b,color:#fff


The minimum viable root AGENTS.md has exactly three things: 1. One-sentence project description 2. Package manager (only if not npm) 3. Non-standard build/typecheck command

Everything else belongs in a docs/ subdirectory tree, loaded on demand when the agent needs it.

Key insight: Never auto-generate AGENTS.md. Initialization scripts prioritize comprehensiveness; your project needs restraint.

| |

A bloated AGENTS.md is ~500 lines (~4,000 tokens). A minimal one is ~3 lines (~50 tokens). Beyond the token math, why does a bloated AGENTS.md actually hurt agent performance?

→ ai-agents/agents-md-best-practices
quiz intermediate

Which integration gap does ACP fill that MCP, A2A, and AG-UI don't?

graph BT
    DATA["🗄️ Databases · APIs · Files"] -->|"MCP"| AGENT["🤖 Your Agent"]
    AGENTS["🤝 Remote Agents"] -->|"A2A"| AGENT
    SUPPLIER["🛒 Suppliers"] -->|"UCP + AP2"| AGENT
    AGENT -->|"A2UI + AG-UI"| FRONTEND["💻 Frontend"]
    EDITOR["🖊️ Code Editor / IDE"] -->|"ACP ← 7th"| AGENT

    style AGENT fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style EDITOR fill:#0e7490,color:#fff


The stack is additive: start with MCP for data, add A2A when you need delegation, add UCP+AP2 for commerce, add A2UI+AG-UI for UI, add ACP when you need your agent in editors.

· ·

The 6-protocol stack (MCP, A2A, UCP, AP2, A2UI, AG-UI) leaves one integration gap unsolved. ACP fills it. What is that gap?

→ ai-agents/agent-protocol-stack
quiz intermediate

Three Conditions for Writing an ADR — Which One Is NOT Required?

From /grill-with-docs: during a domain modeling session, ADRs are created automatically — but only when all three conditions hold. Example: choosing ON DELETE RESTRICT instead of SET NULL qualifies — hard to change once foreign keys exist, surprises developers who expect SET NULL, deliberate trade-off against convenience.

# docs/adr/0012-pitch-fk-delete-restrict.md
## Decision
Use ON DELETE RESTRICT for pitchId foreign key.

## Context
SET NULL would silently detach videos from pitches during cascade deletes.
RESTRICT forces conscious detachment, matching our archive-over-delete philosophy.


Key insight: The three-condition gate prevents documentation debt. Teams that write ADRs for every choice end up with hundreds of irrelevant records — and ignore them all.

Matt Pocock's /grill-with-docs creates an ADR only when all three conditions are met. Which of these is NOT one of the three required conditions?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-grilling-skills
code beginner

How do you call Gemma 4 E4B locally from an existing OpenAI SDK integration?

import OpenAI from "openai";

const client = new OpenAI({
  baseURL: "http://localhost:8080/v1",  // ← only change needed
  apiKey: "local",                       // llama.cpp ignores this
});

const response = await client.chat.completions.create({
  model: "gemma-4-E4B-it-Q4_K_M",      // llama.cpp ignores this too
  messages: [{ role: "user", content: "Explain the Mandelbrot set." }],
  max_tokens: 256,
});
// Output: local inference — zero cloud dependency, zero per-token cost


Key insight: Apache 2.0 weights + llama.cpp inference + OpenAI-compatible API = full local stack. Any privacy-sensitive app can swap in Gemma 4 with one line change.

·

What single change plugs Gemma 4 running on llama.cpp into an existing OpenAI SDK client?

→ ai-agents/gemma-4
quiz beginner

What single change lifts MCP tool accuracy from 72% to 90%?

A 2026 study of 856 tools across 103 MCP servers found 97.1% had at least one description smell. Separately, Wang et al. analyzed 10,831 servers: standard-compliant descriptions were chosen +260% more often in head-to-head competition (72% vs 20% baseline) — same code, better description wins.

// Before: vague description
server.tool("get_weather", "Get weather for a location", { location: z.string() }, handler);

// After: +18% accuracy just from the example
server.tool("get_weather",
  "Get current weather for a city. Example: { location: 'San Francisco, CA' }",
  { location: z.string().describe("City and state, e.g. 'Austin, TX'") },
  handler
);


Anthropic Engineering found one addition to MCP tool descriptions that lifted complex parameter handling accuracy from 72% to 90%. What was it?

→ ai-agents/mcp-server-design
quiz intermediate

What does Spec-Kit's specify phase intentionally exclude?

Spec-Driven Development inverts the traditional workflow: the specification *is* the product, code is its derivative.

flowchart LR
    S["/speckit.specify\nWhat + Why\n(no tech stack)"] --> CL["/speckit.clarify\nFill gaps"] --> P["/speckit.plan\nNow: tech stack\n+ architecture"]
    style S fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style CL fill:#0891b2,color:#fff
    style P fill:#059669,color:#fff


The separation: Steps 1-3 answer *what* to build. Steps 4-5 decide *how* to build it. This order prevents a classic failure mode: the model picks a tech stack based on the first sentence of the prompt, then builds confidently toward the wrong target.

Key insight: The bottleneck in AI-assisted development isn't generating code — it's generating the *right* code the first time. Spec → clarify → plan surfaces wrong assumptions before any implementation begins.

| |

In GitHub's Spec-Kit 7-step workflow, what does the /speckit.specify phase deliberately exclude?

→ ai-agents/spec-driven-development
code intermediate

ml-intern quality gate: what happens when the agent finds low-quality data?

type DataVerdict = "proceed" | "generate_synthetic" | "reject";

// Step 1: inspect before committing GPU resources
const report = JSON.parse(await askAgent(history,
  Inspect dataset "${dataset}". Return JSON:  +
  {verdict:"proceed"|"generate_synthetic"|"reject", issues:string[], syntheticConfig?:{count:number,upsample:number}}
)) as DatasetReport;

if (report.verdict === "reject") { continue; }              // skip entirely
if (report.verdict === "generate_synthetic") {
  const cfg = report.syntheticConfig ?? { count: 1000, upsample: 10 };
  // HealthBench: 1,100 pts upsampled 50× → beat Codex by 60%
}

// Step 2: train → eval → diagnose → retry (up to 3 attempts)
for (let attempt = 1; attempt <= 3; attempt++) {
  const run = JSON.parse(await askAgent(history,
    Train on ${dataset}, return: {status:"success"|"failed", metric:number, failureAnalysis?:string}
  )) as RunResult;
  if (run.status === "success") return;
  // "✗ Attempt 1: reward collapse — re-diagnosing..."
}


Key insight: The quality gate is what makes ml-intern more than a naive training loop. Bad data rejected early saves hours of GPU time and prevents compounding errors downstream. The HealthBench win didn't come from a better model — it came from refusing to train on bad data and generating better data instead.

| |

ml-intern's quality gate inspects each dataset before committing GPU resources. Which verdict triggers synthetic data generation, and what did it produce for HealthBench?

→ ai-agents/ml-intern
insight intermediate

Source-Driven Development: Agents Invent APIs They Don't Know

AI coding agents frequently invent API parameters, use deprecated methods, or pattern-complete from stale training data. The failure is invisible until runtime: the code looks correct, passes type checks, and then crashes on a parameter that never existed.

Source-Driven Development (an Agent Skills principle) addresses this by forcing the agent to fetch current official documentation and cite it before writing any code that uses that API.

// ❌ What agents do by default:
// Pattern-complete from training data (may be 12+ months stale)
const result = await anthropic.messages.create({
  model: "claude-3-opus-20240229",  // deprecated model ID
  max_tokens_to_sample: 1024,       // old parameter name (was renamed)
});

// ✅ Source-driven development:
// 1. Fetch current docs: GET https://docs.anthropic.com/en/api/messages
// 2. Cite the source in a comment
// 3. Use only parameters confirmed in today's docs
const result = await anthropic.messages.create({
  model: "claude-opus-4-6",   // verified in docs fetched 2026-04-18
  max_tokens: 4096,           // current parameter name
});
// Output: { id: "msg_...", content: [...] }


Key insight: Source-Driven Development is the same principle as knowledge compilation applied to individual coding decisions — ground every framework claim in a verified, current source. The agent can't write code that uses an API it hasn't fetched documentation for.

This connects directly to why wikis beat RAG: pre-compiled, reviewed knowledge prevents the hallucination surface area that grows when models invent from stale training.

| |
→ ai-agents/agent-quality-gates
insight intermediate

Wiki cards are generated once at ingest — zero LLM inference at review time

The wiki's /learn/ page is a compile-once-serve-cheaply pattern applied to knowledge. Cards are generated by an LLM once during ingest (1-3 cards per significantly updated wiki page), then stored as markdown files. At review time, ts-fsrs schedules them using only localStorage — no LLM, no API calls, no inference cost.

graph LR
    INGEST["📥 Ingest\nnew source"] -->|"LLM writes\n1-3 cards"| CARDS["🃏 Cards\n(markdown files)"]
    CARDS -->|"loaded by\n/learn/ page"| FSRS["ts-fsrs\nscheduler"]
    FSRS -->|"due cards"| USER["👤 User rates\nAgain / Hard / Good / Easy"]
    USER -->|"updated card.due\n→ localStorage"| FSRS

    style INGEST fill:#1e3a5f,color:#fff
    style CARDS fill:#7c3aed,color:#fff
    style FSRS fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style USER fill:#059669,color:#fff


This mirrors how the wiki itself works vs. RAG: pre-compiled knowledge artifacts (one LLM call at write time) serve reads at zero marginal inference cost, while live RAG queries pay inference cost on every lookup.

Key insight: The same generate-once principle that makes pre-compiled wiki pages more token-efficient than live RAG queries applies to spaced repetition cards. The LLM does expensive synthesis once; every subsequent review session is free. The cost amortizes over all future review sessions.

→ knowledge-management/spaced-repetition-code
code beginner

A Tool Call Is Just an Instruction — Nothing Executes Until You Do It

Matt Pocock ([aihero.dev](https://www.aihero.dev/what-are-tools)) makes this explicit:

> "This tool call is just an instruction for which tool should be called. Nothing has happened yet — the assistant has just produced a message."

// Step 3: LLM returns tool_use — nothing executed yet
// { type: "tool_use", id: "tu_01", name: "bash", input: { command: "grep -r TODO src/" } }

// Step 4: YOUR code executes the tool
const output = execSync(input.command, { encoding: "utf8" });

// Step 5: Send result back — errors are valid inputs that enable recovery
messages.push({
  role: "user",
  content: [{
    type: "tool_result",
    tool_use_id: "tu_01",
    content: success ? output : Error: ${errorMessage},
    // LLM sees the error and adapts — silent failures break the loop
  }]
});


Key insight: Silent tool failures are the most common loop bug. If your tool crashes and you don't send the error back as a tool_result, the LLM assumes it succeeded and proceeds on false premises — compounding errors through every subsequent step.

| |

After the LLM returns a tool_use block, what has happened?

→ ai-agents/agentic-loop-typescript
quiz intermediate

Claude Code: Which of the 5 Agentic Patterns Fits Your Task?

graph TD
    Q1{Task structure?}
    Q1 -->|"Linear, predictable"| SEQ["1. Sequential\nLow tokens · High debuggability"]
    Q1 -->|"Needs tool coordination"| ORC["2. Operator\nOrchestrator + subagents"]
    Q1 -->|"Many independent subtasks"| PAR["3. Split-and-Merge\nN× tokens · Fast wall-clock"]
    Q1 -->|"Multi-phase expertise"| TEAM["4. Agent Teams\nPeer review · Hardest to debug"]
    Q1 -->|"Well-validated, repeatable"| HEAD["5. Headless\n--print · No human in loop"]

    style SEQ fill:#0891b2,color:#fff
    style ORC fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style PAR fill:#7c3aed,color:#fff
    style TEAM fill:#d97706,color:#fff
    style HEAD fill:#166534,color:#fff


> Key insight (Headless): Headless is the *last* pattern you adopt, not the first. Validate interactively → confirm all failure modes → only then automate. Mistakes in headless compound without correction.

| |

You need to generate 200 independent test case summaries — each is a self-contained document with no dependency on the others. Which pattern minimizes wall-clock time?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-workflows
code intermediate

PHP 8 match: What Happens With an Unhandled Case?

match enforces strict comparison (===) and fails loudly on unhandled cases:

// ❌ switch: fall-through + loose comparison = silent bugs
switch ($status) {
    case "draft":   $label = "Draft";        // no break → falls through!
    case "review":  $label = "Under Review"; // overwrites silently
}

// ✅ match: strict ===, no fall-through, UnhandledMatchError if no arm matches
$label = match($status) {
    'draft'     => 'Draft',
    'review'    => 'Under Review',
    'published' => 'Published',
    // no default → UnhandledMatchError on any other value: loud and immediate
};

// match(true) for range conditions — replaces nested if/elseif chains
$tier = match(true) {
    $score >= 90 => 'A',
    $score >= 75 => 'B',
    $score >= 60 => 'C',
    default      => 'F',
};


Key insight: Writing match without a default is intentional: you're asserting that these are the only valid states. If a new state appears and crashes, that's the feature — it surfaces the bug immediately rather than computing wrong answers silently for months.

What does PHP throw when a match expression has no arm that matches and no default?

→ frontend/modern-php-8
quiz intermediate

Browser tool selection: when does Hyperbrowser win over Lightpanda or Firecrawl?

flowchart TD
    Q1{"Need live browser\n(JS rendering)?"}
    Q1 -->|No| STATIC["fetch() + Cheerio\nno browser needed"]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{"Control vs abstraction?"}
    Q2 -->|"Full Playwright control"| Q3{"Self-hosted OK?"}
    Q2 -->|"Describe intent, not steps"| FC["Firecrawl\nManaged API"]
    Q3 -->|Yes| LP["Lightpanda\n9× faster · 16× less memory\nlocal binary"]
    Q3 -->|No| HB["✅ Hyperbrowser\nCloud CDP · 10,000+ sessions\nZero infrastructure"]
    style HB fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style LP fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style FC fill:#0891b2,color:#fff
    style STATIC fill:#374151,color:#d1d5db


| Tool | Model | Code changes? | |------|-------|--------------| | fetch() + Cheerio | HTTP + HTML parser | None — already minimal | | Firecrawl | Managed API — describe what, it decides how | Large (different API entirely) | | Lightpanda | Local headless browser, CDP-compatible | Drop-in swap (connect vs launch) | | Hyperbrowser | Cloud CDP — Playwright/Puppeteer native | Minimal (swap launch → connect) |

Key insight: Hyperbrowser vs Lightpanda is an infrastructure question, not a code question — the Playwright API is identical. Hyperbrowser vs Firecrawl is a control question: do you want to script interactions yourself, or describe what you want?

An AI agent must scrape a JavaScript-heavy SPA. The team needs full Playwright control but cannot maintain browser infrastructure. Which tool fits best?

→ ai-agents/hyperbrowser
insight intermediate

Code Mode: swap 40 tools for search() + execute() and cut tokens 99.9%

Cloudflare's MCP server exposed 2,500 API endpoints as individual tools. Discovery alone consumed tens of thousands of tokens on every query. Their solution: Code Mode — collapse every tool into two meta-tools.

// Before Code Mode: one tool per endpoint
server.tool("create_dns_record", ...);     // 2,500 tools × description tokens = 💸
server.tool("delete_dns_record", ...);
server.tool("list_zones", ...);
// … 2,497 more tools

// After Code Mode: two meta-tools — the LLM writes code against them
server.tool("search_api",
  "Search available Cloudflare API endpoints. Example: { query: 'DNS records' }",
  { query: z.string() },
  async ({ query }) => { /* return relevant endpoints + schemas */ }
);
server.tool("execute_api",
  "Execute a Cloudflare API call. Example: { endpoint: 'POST /zones/{id}/dns_records', args: {...} }",
  { endpoint: z.string(), args: z.record(z.unknown()) },
  async ({ endpoint, args }) => { /* call the real API */ }
);


Result: 99.9% token reduction — from exposing full schemas of 2,500 tools to returning only the handful the LLM searched for.

Key insight: Code Mode inverts the MCP design: instead of describing what tools *do*, describe how to *find and invoke* any tool programmatically. The LLM writes the integration code on demand. This is the natural endpoint of "fewer, broader tools" — taken to its logical extreme.

The pattern also applies beyond APIs: search_docs(query) + run_query(sql) covers any document database. One afternoon to replace 50 narrow tools.

→ ai-agents/building-mcp-servers
quiz intermediate

What three variables does FSRS model?

import { createEmptyCard, fsrs, Rating } from 'ts-fsrs'

const scheduler = fsrs()
const card = createEmptyCard()
const preview = scheduler.repeat(card, new Date())

// Rating.Good → { due: +1day, stability: 1.3, difficulty: 5, state: 'Learning' }
// Rating.Easy → { due: +4days, stability: 4.0, difficulty: 3, state: 'Review' }


Key insight: FSRS is *trainable* — it learns your personal forgetting curve. After enough reviews, it schedules your personal 90% retention threshold, not a population average.

| |

FSRS (the modern spaced repetition algorithm used by ts-fsrs) models memory through three variables. Which set is correct?

→ knowledge-management/spaced-repetition-code
code intermediate

What does GROUP BY ALL do in DuckDB?

SELECT station_name, count(*) AS num_services
FROM 'train_services.parquet'
GROUP BY ALL
ORDER BY num_services DESC LIMIT 10;
-- equivalent to: GROUP BY station_name


Works across joins with many GROUP BY columns too — DuckDB infers which columns need grouping. The FROM 'file.parquet' syntax is also notable: DuckDB reads files directly in SQL, no import step.

What does `GROUP BY ALL` do in this DuckDB query?

→ tools/duckdb
quiz intermediate

Kapa.ai: what happens when a question falls outside indexed docs?

Most RAG systems still let the LLM fill gaps with training data. Kapa enforces strict grounding: if it can't cite a source, it doesn't answer.

Key insight: 'I don't know' is a feature, not a failure. The grounded-only guarantee is the reliability property that unlocks enterprise deployment.

When a user asks Kapa.ai something outside its indexed documentation, what does the answer engine do?

→ ai-agents/kapa-ai
code intermediate

Configuring the Claude API for HTML Output

const client = new Anthropic();
const response = await client.messages.create({
  model: "claude-sonnet-4-6",
  max_tokens: 4096,
  system: Output HTML, not Markdown. Use semantic elements:

, 
, 
–
, 
    , 
      , 
      .
Use 
      / for collapsible sections.
Use 
       for code blocks.
Do NOT use Markdown syntax (**, ##, -, etc.).,
  messages: [{ role: "user", content: "Explain how React hooks work" }],
});

      const html = (response.content[0] as Anthropic.TextBlock).text;
// One step: LLM output → innerHTML — no Markdown parser required
document.getElementById("output")!.innerHTML = html;



      Key insight: The system prompt is the only change. The entire Markdown-parser dependency drops out of your stack — no marked, no remark, no MDX. The pipeline shrinks from 3 steps (LLM → parser → browser) to 1 step (LLM → browser).

       |  |

To make Claude return HTML instead of Markdown in an API call, where do you put the HTML formatting instruction, and what does the inject step look like?

→ ai-agents/html-vs-markdown-output
insight intermediate

claude-context cuts token usage ~40% through hybrid semantic code search

Without semantic search, AI agents discover code through multi-round exploration: read a file, follow imports, read another file. Each round burns tokens and latency. claude-context collapses this into a single hybrid search query.

// Instead of multi-round file traversal:
const results = await context.semanticSearch('./project', 'database connection pooling', 5);
// Returns: [{ relativePath, startLine, endLine, score }]
// BM25 (keyword) + dense vector (semantic) fire simultaneously


Key insight: The ~40% token reduction means retrieving *more* useful code under the same token budget — not just fewer tokens for the same result. BM25 catches exact identifier matches; vector search catches semantic intent where variable names differ from the query.

→ ai-agents/claude-context-mcp
quiz beginner

Which Gestalt principle explains why items inside a card border feel like one group?

graph TD
    CR["📦 Common Region\nBorder/card groups items\nCards, panels, containers"]
    PR["⬛⬛ Proximity\nClose items group\nNav clusters, form fields"]
    SIM["🔵🔵🔵 Similarity\nAlike items group\nRepeating icons, matched colors"]
    CLO["◐ Closure\nGaps get mentally filled\nLogos with missing pieces"]
    CON["——→ Continuity\nEye follows smoothest path\nProgress bars, carousels"]
    FG["● Figure/Ground\nOne object pops, rest recedes\nModals, tooltips, overlays"]
    CR & PR & SIM & CLO & CON & FG --> OUT["🧠 Perceived structure before reading a word"]
    style OUT fill:#166534,color:#fff


Design implication: Card containers, whitespace, alignment, and color communicate structure instantly — before the user has read a single label. Gestalt violations (logically grouped items placed far apart, unrelated items sharing a border) create confusion that users experience as "the UI feels off" without being able to articulate why.

> Key insight (NN/g): Don Norman calls himself a "cognitive designer" because regardless of the product type, what matters is designing for how people *actually* think — not who we wish they were.

A UI places related content inside a bordered card. Before the user reads a word, their brain groups the card's contents together. Which Gestalt principle is doing this work?

→ ai-design/ai-ux-psychology
insight intermediate

View Transition snapshots are flat images — not live DOM

During a View Transition the browser captures flat image snapshots of all named elements — ::view-transition-old (leaving state) and ::view-transition-new (entering state). This has a surprising consequence: CSS properties like font-size, color, and border-radius do not interpolate during the morph. The snapshot is a PNG, not a DOM node.

/* Both old and new states must share the exact aspect ratio for seamless morphing */
/* If text reflows change line count, the aspect ratio shifts — breaking the morph */
.post-title {
  view-transition-name: post-title;
  /* Fix max-width or line clamp to stabilise aspect ratio */
}

/* Debug in Chrome: DevTools → Cmd+Shift+P → "animations" panel
   Slow down, pause, replay — inspect ::view-transition-group pseudo-elements */


Key insight: Three common breakages share one root cause — flat snapshots: text reflows change aspect ratio, clip-path on parent containers doesn't clip the snapshot, and border-radius distorts when source/target ratios differ.

→ frontend/view-transitions
quiz beginner

Agent or Workflow? The Decision That Matters Most

flowchart TD
    Q1{"Task well-defined?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes| W["✅ Workflow\n(predictable, debuggable, bounded error)"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Needs improvisation?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| A["🤖 Agent\n(LLM decides steps)"]
    Q2 -->|No| ST["Break into sub-tasks → Workflow"]


| Dimension | Workflow | Agent | |-----------|----------|-------| | Steps | Predetermined by developer code | Chosen by LLM | | Error | Bounded (known N steps) | Unbounded (LLM chooses chain length) | | Best for | Repeatable, specified tasks | Open-ended, improvisation required |

Key insight: Workflows are often unfairly maligned because they're not as exciting as agents. But for clearly-specified tasks, they're more reliable, debuggable, and cheaper — every time.

Anthropic's own recommendation: "Avoid agentic systems if simple single-call optimization suffices."

| |

Matt Pocock argues that choosing between agent and workflow is the most important architectural decision. What is the key criterion for preferring a workflow over an agent?

→ ai-agents/agent-design-patterns
insight intermediate

Netflix's Temporal Buckets: Reliability Through Diversity

Netflix's video search architecture achieves agent reliability through an unexpected strategy: intentionally diversifying failure modes across modalities. Rather than one model that might fail in one systematic way, three parallel search paths (visual, audio, text) fail independently.

The temporal bucket fusion approach: - Divide video timeline into overlapping 30s buckets - Run visual/audio/semantic search on each bucket independently - Fuse results with reciprocal rank fusion — the highest agreement wins

// Reciprocal rank fusion: consensus beats any single signal
function fuseBuckets(results: BucketResult[][]): RankedResult[] {
  const scores = new Map();
  for (const rankedList of results) {
    rankedList.forEach((r, rank) => {
      scores.set(r.id, (scores.get(r.id) ?? 0) + 1 / (60 + rank));
    });
  }
  return [...scores.entries()]
    .sort(([, a], [, b]) => b - a)
    .map(([id]) => ({ id }));
}


Key insight: Reliability doesn't require a single perfect model — it requires that independent failure modes cancel each other out.

|
→ ai-agents/multimodal-agent-search
quiz intermediate

Fitts's Law: where should a destructive button go relative to the primary action?

T = a + b × log₂(2D/W)
    D = distance to target
    W = target width

Large W (big button) → log term shrinks → T drops → faster
Small D (near current cursor) → log term shrinks → T drops → faster
Screen edge → W = ∞ → log₂(2D/∞) = 0 → fastest possible



graph TD
    subgraph FAST["✅ Fast & Low-Error"]
        F1["Large button\n(big W)"]
        F2["Near cursor\n(small D)"]
        F3["Screen edge/corner\n(infinite effective size)"]
    end
    subgraph RISKY["⚠️ Mis-click Zone"]
        S1["Destructive action\nnext to frequent action\n← same size + distance"]
    end
    FAST --> GOOD["Primary actions: large, contextually near\nDestructive: separated, visually secondary"]
    RISKY --> BAD["Accidental delete, frustration,\nerror rate spikes"]
    style FAST fill:#166534,color:#fff
    style RISKY fill:#dc2626,color:#fff


Key insight: Fitts's Law makes big, nearby targets easy to hit — which is great for primary actions and dangerous for destructive ones next to them. The design response is asymmetric: make primary actions fast (big, close) and destructive actions slow (small, distant, separated).

A UI has a primary 'Save' button and a destructive 'Delete' button. According to Fitts's Law, what is the highest-risk placement — and what should you do instead?

→ ai-design/ai-ux-psychology
insight beginner

AI Coding Assistants Default to Pre-PHP 8.0 Syntax

AI coding assistants (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) default to legacy PHP syntax even when they know better. They "admitted they knew the better way, but defaulted to the traditional way out of habit." Training data is dominated by legacy PHP 7 code.

What this means in practice: Ask for PHP code and you'll likely get: - switch instead of match (fall-through bugs possible) - String constants instead of enum (typos compile silently) - getFullName()/setFullName() methods instead of property hooks - Manual $this->prop = $arg constructors instead of property promotion

The fix: Explicitly prompt for modern features: *"Use PHP 8.1+ features: enums, readonly, match expressions, constructor property promotion."*

// What AI gives you by default:
if ($status === 'draft') { ... } // magic string

// What you should ask for explicitly:
enum ArticleStatus: string { case Draft = 'draft'; ... }
$status = ArticleStatus::tryFrom($_POST['status'] ?? ''); // null if invalid


Key insight: The theme of modern PHP is turning silent failures into loud compile-time errors. Enums catch typos at parse time. match throws UnhandledMatchError. #[Override] fails on misspelled method names. You have to ask for this defense layer — it won't appear by default.

|
→ frontend/modern-php-8
insight intermediate

Companion Agent Pattern: Cross-Model Peer Review Without Cavekit Config

Running a second AI model alongside Claude Code creates automatic peer review at zero config overhead. The pattern: Claude proposes, Codex challenges.

- Where they agree: high confidence — proceed - Where they disagree: flag for human review — the disagreement itself is signal

This is the Cavekit adversarial review pattern without the Cavekit configuration overhead. It works because models trained on different datasets and optimized with different objectives have uncorrelated blind spots.

# Terminal 1: Claude Code working on a feature
claude code "refactor auth module to use JWTs"

# Terminal 2: Codex reviewing the output
codex "review the auth changes in the diff — what's wrong or missing?"

# Where both agree: ship it
# Where they disagree: the disagreement IS the review


Key insight: You don't need the models to agree on the solution — you need them to disagree on the *problems*. Disagreement surfaces what one model would have approved without the other's challenge.

The same pattern at the platform level is the Cavekit adversarial build review and the — all variations on multi-model verification. The companion pattern is the open-source, no-config analog.

| |
→ ai-agents/building-coding-agents
insight intermediate

Make agent teammates adversarial to defeat anchoring bias

Sequential investigation anchors on the first plausible explanation and stops exploring. Claude Code agent teams fix this by making teammates explicitly adversarial — like a scientific debate where investigators try to disprove each other's theories.

Users report the app exits after one message. Spawn 5 agent teammates
to investigate different hypotheses. Have them talk to each other to try
to disprove each other's theories, like a scientific debate.


Key insight: The debate structure is the mechanism. Teammates that must actively refute each other's theories before a winner can stand means the surviving explanation has survived challenge — much more likely to be the actual root cause.

This is distinct from parallel code review (different lenses on the same artifact) — competing hypotheses require adversarial interaction, not just separate analysis.

Requires CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1 in settings.json. v2.1.32+.

→ ai-agents/claude-code-agent-teams
insight intermediate

Skill Files Are Maximum-Privilege Remote Code Execution

Skill files (.md in .claude/commands/) have full access to your filesystem, shell, and git. Installing an external skill is trusting its author with everything Claude Code can do.

graph LR
    R["External .md skill\n(GitHub source)"] -->|"GitHub renders"| V["Visible text\n(what you see)"]
    R -->|"Model reads"| H["Hidden HTML comments\n"]
    H -->|"executed by agent"| X["🔓 Credential theft\nFile exfiltration"]
    V -->|"ship as .md.txt"| S["✅ Plain text\nAll content visible"]
    style H fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style X fill:#7f1d1d,color:#fff
    style S fill:#166534,color:#fff


Markdown can contain invisible HTML comments GitHub hides in its rendered preview. A malicious skill can embed instructions invisible in the UI but executed by the model.

Before installing any externally sourced skill: 1. Read the raw file, not the GitHub-rendered preview 2. Check for and invisible Unicode 3. Prefer repos that ship skills as .md.txt (plain text, fully visible)

> Key insight (honnibal): "A skill that says 'helpful refactoring assistant' in its visible text could contain a hidden HTML comment telling the model to exfiltrate files." Skills are the attack surface because they're maximum-privilege by design.

|
→ ai-agents/claude-code-skills
insight advanced

Code Mode: 2 tools replace 2,500 individual API wrappers

Cloudflare exposed all 2,500 of their API endpoints through just two MCP meta-tools — search() and execute() — instead of 2,500 individual wrappers. This is a 99.9% reduction in schema tokens (2 tools × ~200 tokens = 400 tokens vs ~500K for the full schema).

// Two meta-tools replace 2,500 endpoint wrappers
// search() → LLM discovers the right endpoint schema
// execute() → LLM calls it with the parameters it composed
server.tool("search", "Find endpoints by intent. Example: { query: 'list DNS records' }", ...);
server.tool("execute", "Call an endpoint. Example: { method: 'GET', path: '/zones/{id}/dns_records' }", ...);


The tradeoff: Code Mode requires the LLM to generate correct API calls on the fly. It works best with consistent, well-documented APIs. Inconsistent APIs with edge cases still benefit from explicit tool wrappers that handle edge cases server-side.

Key insight: Code Mode is the logical endpoint of "curate ruthlessly." If 5–10 tools beat 50, then 2 meta-tools beat 2,500 — but only when the LLM can write reliable code against them.

→ ai-agents/mcp-server-patterns
quiz intermediate

Pretext: which problem does it NOT solve?

A surprising use case: CI label overflow detection. Because Pretext needs no browser, you can verify at build time that button labels fit their containers — catching truncation bugs before deployment.

// No DOM, no browser — works in Node/CI
const prepared = prepare(buttonLabel, '14px Inter')
const { lineCount } = layout(prepared, buttonWidth, 20)
if (lineCount > 1) throw new Error(Button label overflows: "${buttonLabel}")


Key insight: The "no DOM required" property isn't just a performance win — it enables a whole class of build-time and server-side text layout that was previously impossible.

Pretext measures and lays out text without DOM reflow. Which of these is NOT a use case it addresses?

→ frontend/pretext-text-layout
insight beginner

The Anti-Slop Principle: Agent Count is a Vanity Metric

Max Brodeur-Urbas (Gumloop, $17M Series A): "'50 AI agents running my company' — that's not automation. That's a slop machine."

| Approach | Failure mode | Reliability | |----------|-------------|-------------| | 50 loosely connected agents | Cascading errors, hard to debug | p^50 compounding | | 5 tightly scoped agents | Isolated failures, clear responsibility | p^5, debuggable | | 1 well-designed human-AI workflow | Predictable, auditable | Human catches what AI misses |

Real automation means fewer, well-designed agents with: - Tight scope (each agent does one thing reliably) - Hard validation gates (not just soft confidence thresholds) - Designed failure modes (explicit fallback, not silent drift) - Human-in-the-loop for high-stakes decisions

Key insight: Agent count is a vanity metric. 5 tightly scoped agents reliably beat 50 loosely orchestrated ones. The compounding math is brutal — at 95% per-step reliability, 50 steps gives you 8% end-to-end success.

| |
→ ai-agents/agent-quality-gates
connection intermediate

Computers as meta-medium: why they matter for thought

Alan Turing framed computers as "universal reasoning machines." Alan Kay extended this: computers are the meta-medium — they can simulate any other medium. This is *why* computation matters for tools for thought:

Computers can simulate:
  paper and pencil → canvas → library card catalog → Zettelkasten
  → and compose them in novel combinations

They don't add new cognitive capabilities.
They instantiate all previous ones + amplify them at scale.


Andy Clark's extended mind hypothesis (1998) adds the other half: cognitive processes don't stop at the skull. A notebook, a calendar, a wiki aren't just aids — they're *constitutive parts of the cognitive system*. Remove them and you lose cognitive capacity, not just convenience.

Key insight: Computational tools for thought (LLM wikis, spaced repetition apps) sit at the intersection of both ideas: they're the meta-medium instantiating a 60,000-year-old practice of cognitive extension. The computer is the most powerful TFT in history — not because it invented anything new, but because it can simulate everything that came before and compose it without limits.

· ·
→ knowledge-management/tools-for-thought-cultural
quiz intermediate

Four Patterns That Make Agents Reliable

Four architectural patterns systematically improve agent reliability: knowledge compilation, dynamic tool search, persistent memory, and managed infrastructure. Each moves work OUT of the unreliable real-time LLM call and INTO reliable pre-computed systems.

Key insight: The common thread — reduce what the LLM must figure out at runtime. The less it improvises, the less it fails.

|

Which pattern moves knowledge from real-time LLM inference to pre-compiled artifacts?

→ ai-agents/agent-design-patterns
quiz intermediate

TypeScript: Discriminated Unions — Why Optional Blobs Fail

// Optional blob: allows impossible states
type BadState = {
  status: "loading" | "success" | "error";
  data?: User;
  error?: Error;
};

// Discriminated union: makes illegal states unrepresentable
type State =
  | { status: "loading" }
  | { status: "success"; data: User }
  | { status: "error"; error: Error };

// Now TypeScript enforces correctness in switch:
function render(s: State) {
  switch (s.status) {
    case "loading": return "Loading...";
    case "success": return s.data.name;   // s.data guaranteed to exist
    case "error":   return s.error.message; // s.error guaranteed to exist
    default: {
      const _exhaustive: never = s; // compile error if new status added without handling
      return _exhaustive;
    }
  }
}


Key insight: The meta-pattern across all 15 TypeScript patterns points one direction: encode more constraints in types, annotate less prose. Every discriminated union replaces an optional blob. Every unknown replaces an any. The cumulative effect: bugs that used to be runtime surprises become compiler errors.

| |

You have a loading state type: `{ status: 'loading' | 'success' | 'error'; data?: User; error?: Error }`. What illegal state does this type allow?

→ frontend/typescript-patterns
quiz intermediate

Svelte Custom Renderers: What the IR Unlocks

flowchart LR
    SV[".svelte file"] --> C["Svelte Compiler"]
    C --> IR["IR (fragment tree)"]
    IR --> DOM["🌐 Web DOM"]
    IR --> Native["📱 iOS/Android (Lynx.js)"]
    IR --> Term["⌨️ Terminal (Ink-style)"]
    IR --> Three["🎮 Threlte (Three.js)"]


Key insight: Svelte's compiler-first approach gives it a structural advantage over runtime-based cross-platform frameworks. React Native carries ~45KB of runtime everywhere; a Svelte-based native framework could deliver the same reactivity with near-zero runtime overhead.

Bonus unlock: The IR sidesteps Svelte's CSP complication — the DOM template element trick blocked in strict Content Security Policy environments. No DOM dependency → no CSP constraint.

|

The Svelte custom renderers API introduces an Intermediate Representation (IR). What does this enable that current Svelte cannot do?

→ frontend/svelte-custom-renderers
code intermediate

MCP Client: Multi-Turn Tool Loop — What's the Bug?

// BUG: sends one user message per tool result
while (response.stop_reason === "tool_use") {
  for (const block of response.content) {
    if (block.type !== "tool_use") continue;
    const result = await this.mcp.callTool({ name: block.name, arguments: block.input });
    messages.push({ role: "assistant", content: [block] });  // ← BUG: one per tool
    messages.push({ role: "user", content: [{ type: "tool_result", ... }] }); // ← BUG
  }
  response = await this.anthropic.messages.create({ messages, tools: this.tools });
}


The fix — batch ALL results into ONE user message:

// CORRECT: batch all tool results into one user turn
while (response.stop_reason === "tool_use") {
  const toolResults = [];
  for (const block of response.content) {
    if (block.type !== "tool_use") continue;
    const result = await this.mcp.callTool({ name: block.name, arguments: block.input });
    toolResults.push({ type: "tool_result", tool_use_id: block.id, content: text });
  }
  messages.push({ role: "assistant", content: response.content }); // once
  messages.push({ role: "user", content: toolResults });            // once, all batched
  response = await this.anthropic.messages.create({ messages, tools: this.tools });
}


Key insight: The loop is driven by stop_reason, not content iteration. A single response can contain multiple tool_use blocks — all must execute and their results must land in ONE user message.

| |

This TypeScript MCP client loop has a critical bug. What is it?

→ ai-agents/mcp-client-development
connection intermediate

Barrels vs. Ammunition: How Fowler & Beck's 'engineer as agent manager' maps

Keith Rabois calls them "barrels": people who take end-to-end ownership and can direct others. Martin Fowler and Kent Beck call them "engineers as managers of multiple agents": the industrial-revolution shift where craftsmen become orchestrators of machinery.

Both frameworks arrive at the same conclusion from different directions. Rabois identifies the pattern as a hiring filter — look for ownership history, not technical depth. Fowler/Beck identify it as a craft evolution — the AI shift transforms the *role*, not just the *tools*.

The key divergence: Rabois frames it as a talent-density problem (get more barrels). Fowler/Beck frame it as a transition problem (teach ammunition to think like barrels). Both are right in different organizations.

Key insight: High barrel-density teams amplify with AI; ammunition-heavy teams get automated or downsized. The outcome depends on whether the team learned to direct, not just execute.

→ productivity/barrels-vs-ammunition
insight advanced

The Skill Factory: An Agent That Writes Its Own Skills

The most powerful ADK skill pattern: a meta-skill whose sole purpose is generating new SKILL.md files at runtime — extending the agent's capabilities without human intervention.

# The factory skill embeds the spec + example as L3 references
skill_creator = models.Skill(
    frontmatter=models.Frontmatter(
        name="skill-creator",
        description="Creates new ADK-compatible skill definitions from requirements. Generates SKILL.md following agentskills.io.",
    ),
    instructions=(
        "When asked to create a new skill, generate a complete SKILL.md.\n"
        "Read references/skill-spec.md for the format specification.\n"
        "Read references/example-skill.md for a working example.\n"
        "Rules: kebab-case name ≤64 chars, description ≤1024 chars, SKILL.md ≤500 lines."
    ),
    resources={"skill-spec.md": "# agentskills.io spec...", "example-skill.md": "..."},
)


How the factory works: 1. Agent receives: "I need a skill for Python security review" 2. Agent loads skill-creator (L2 instructions) 3. Agent reads skill-spec.md via load_skill_resource (L3) 4. Agent generates a spec-compliant SKILL.md — works in ADK, Claude Code, Cursor, and 40+ tools

Key insight: A skill factory doesn't generate text — it generates *deployable capabilities*. Save the output to skills/python-security-review/SKILL.md and any agentskills.io-compatible tool can use it. Treat generated skills like code dependencies: review before deploying.

| |
→ ai-agents/adk-skill-patterns
quiz beginner

Impeccable: which AI design anti-patterns does the catalog name?

These patterns emerge when AI has no design direction — they're the default output distribution of unconstrained generation. Naming them lets you reject them explicitly in PRODUCT.md's anti-references field.

| Anti-Pattern | What it looks like | |---|---| | Purple gradients | Lavender-to-indigo fills signaling "AI product" | | Cardocalypse | Everything in a card; cards inside cards | | Layout templates | Hero + 3 columns + CTA — the wireframe that became the final design | | Inter everywhere | No typographic contrast across weights or sizes |

Key insight: This isn't a style preference list — it's a precision rejection filter for AI's default output distribution.

Impeccable catalogs AI design anti-patterns that emerge without design direction. Which of these is NOT in its named catalog?

→ ai-design/impeccable
quiz intermediate

Karpathy's Four CLAUDE.md Principles — Which Fixes Over-Engineering?

Andrej Karpathy (co-founder OpenAI) turned frustrating AI coding patterns into four CLAUDE.md correction rules. Forrest Chang's implementation earned 44k+ GitHub stars in 7 days (3M views on X).

| Principle | Default behavior fixed | |-----------|----------------------| | Think Before Coding | Guesses wrong thing → implements it | | Simplicity First | 1000-line abstraction when 100 would suffice | | Surgical Changes | Fixes null check, renames 5 variables, refactors 3 signatures | | Goal-Driven Execution | Waits for step-by-step instructions |

Key insight: These four principles fix behavioral defaults, not capability limits. A CLAUDE.md at the top of every session rewires how Claude approaches code before any task-specific context loads.

| |

Karpathy identified four default behaviors that make AI coding tools frustrating. Which principle directly fixes: 'They really like to overcomplicate code and APIs, bloat abstractions'?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-workflows
insight intermediate

Why Svelte 5 runes were the prerequisite for universal rendering

Svelte 4 reactivity was tied to .svelte files — $store only worked inside components. Svelte 5 runes ($state, $derived) work in any .ts file, decoupling reactivity from the DOM entirely. The same createCounter() function with $state(0) works in a browser component, a terminal renderer, or a native Lynx.js target. Reactivity is target-agnostic; only the renderer differs.

// Works in .ts, .svelte, or a custom renderer — same API
export function createCounter() {
  let count = $state(0);
  return { get count() { return count; }, increment: () => count++ };
}


Key insight: You can't build universal renderers if the reactivity system is DOM-coupled. Runes made the reactivity portable first; custom renderers are the second step.

— —
→ frontend/svelte-universal-rendering
insight intermediate

The 512-token chunk size is the empirically confirmed RAG default

The FloTorch benchmark (Feb 2026) tested 7 chunking strategies across 905,746 tokens from 50 academic papers. Recursive splitting at 512 tokens with 50–100 token overlap scored 69% accuracy — beating fixed-size 256 (65%) and semantic chunking (54%, which produced 43-token micro-fragments).

| Strategy | Accuracy | Notes | |----------|----------|-------| | Recursive 512 tokens, 50–100 overlap | 69% | Default — best overall | | Fixed-size 256 tokens | ~65% | Too small per chunk | | Semantic chunking | 54% | Fragments too tiny | | HTML/Markdown-aware | Better for structured docs | Use HTMLHeaderTextSplitter |

Bennani & Moslonka ([arXiv:2601.14123](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.14123), Jan 2026) independently confirmed a context cliff: quality drops beyond ~2.5k tokens. Bigger is not better past that threshold.

Key insight: Chunking and embedding choices are made once at ingest time and are expensive to redo. Deviate from the 512-token recursive default only if you have a benchmark to justify it — not a hunch.

→ ai-agents/rag-chunking-embeddings
quiz intermediate

Shape Up's Circuit Breaker — Preventing Runaway Projects

Shape Up's circuit breaker is a structural safeguard against the most common failure mode in software projects: scope that grows unchecked because it's easier to continue than to stop.

flowchart LR
    B["🎲 Bet\n6-week cycle"] --> D{"Done?"}
    D -->|Yes| S["✅ Ship"]
    D -->|No| R["❌ Circuit Breaker\nRe-pitch required"]
    R --> B2["New betting table\nFresh appetite\nUpdated scope"]

    style R fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style S fill:#166534,color:#fff


Betting table facts: - Small group, meets once per cycle - Reviews a short list of shaped pitches — no backlog - Each bet: clearly stated problem + appetite + uninterrupted cycle

Key insight: "No backlogs." Shape Up deliberately kills the backlog. Backlogs grow stale, create obligation, and crowd out fresh thinking. If an idea is important, it comes back.

Cool-down: A 2-week buffer between cycles for ad-hoc fixes, exploration, and planning the next betting table — prevents the "always running" burnout.

| |

In Shape Up, what happens when a project isn't finished at the end of the six-week cycle?

→ productivity/shape-up
insight intermediate

Viscosity has two distinct flavors — and each needs a different fix

Robert C. Martin's Viscosity symptom — when the hack is faster than the right path — splits into two root causes that require completely different remedies:

graph TD
    V["🍯 Viscosity\nHack beats right way"]

    V --> SV["🏗️ Software Viscosity\nToo much boilerplate\nbetween intention and execution\n(migration + entity + DTO + mapper = 1h)\nvs. text field dump = 5min"]

    V --> EV["⚡ Environment Viscosity\n30-min test suite\nflaky CI · slow compiler\nFormal process is too painful\nso developers avoid it"]

    SV --> FX1["Fix: Automation tools\nLombok · AutoMapper · MapStruct\n→ Right way becomes nearly as fast"]
    EV --> FX2["Fix: Better infra\nFaster hardware · modularize tests\nquicker CI → Formal process becomes viable"]

    style V fill:#0891b2,color:#fff
    style SV fill:#7c3aed,color:#fff
    style EV fill:#d97706,color:#1e1e1e
    style FX1 fill:#166534,color:#fff
    style FX2 fill:#059669,color:#fff


Key insight: Viscosity is the cruelest symptom because it *rewards* bad choices. Developers aren't lazy — the incentive structure is broken. You can't solve environment viscosity with code reviews, or software viscosity with faster hardware. Diagnose which flavor you have before prescribing.

The diagnosis: if the right way is slow because the *codebase architecture* requires too many layers, that's software viscosity. If it's slow because *tooling and infrastructure* make the formal process painful, that's environment viscosity.

| |
→ productivity/symptoms-of-bad-software-design
insight beginner

Design Taste Is Intentionality, Not Preference

Pablo Stanley reframes taste as intentionality — "the act of choosing. Looking at something and going 'no, not that' over and over." It's not subjective preference. It's a skill: the ability to make deliberate choices about every detail.

The telltale sign of AI-generated UI isn't any single flaw — it's the absence of opinionated decisions. Everything looks competent but nothing looks intentional. Design skills work as correction layers that inject knowledge into agents, overriding LLM defaults.

Key insight: While everyone talks about "taste," design hiring has been flat since 2023 while PM hiring surged 20%. The industry values taste in theory but not in practice.

|
→ ai-design/design-taste-for-ai
insight intermediate

Pi's Tree-Structured Sessions Enable Real Exploration

Most coding agents store conversation history as a linear list. Pi stores it as a tree — every turn is a node you can branch from.

Session tree
├── Turn 1: "Build a user auth system"
│   ├── Turn 2a: "Use JWT tokens"         ← branch A
│   │   └── Turn 3a: "Add refresh tokens"
│   └── Turn 2b: "Use sessions instead"   ← branch B (/tree to navigate here)
│       └── Turn 3b: "Add session expiry"


Use /tree to navigate to any previous node and continue from there. All branches persist in a single file — you never lose either path.

Key insight: Linear history forces you to choose before you explore. Tree history lets you try JWT, branch back, try sessions, and compare — without context loss. This is the same value as Git branching, applied to AI conversation.

Sharing: /export → HTML snapshot; /share → GitHub gist with a shareable render URL. The full exploration — branches and all — becomes a reproducible artifact.

| |
→ ai-agents/pi-coding-agent
quiz intermediate

Which AI-specific bias makes you accept the first plausible AI answer without exploring alternatives?

The Decision Lab's cognitive bias catalog now includes AI-specific entries that didn't exist ten years ago. Six are especially relevant to AI product design:

| Bias | What happens | Design response | |------|-------------|----------------| | AI Literacy Gap | Confident use, poor understanding | Surface uncertainty; explain limitations inline | | Automation Bias | Accept first answer, stop searching | Show alternatives; make edit > accept | | Accountability Diffusion | "Not my fault — the AI did it" | Be explicit about human responsibility | | Delegation Creep | Gradually hand over too much | Build check-in points; human override obvious | | Anchoring | First response locks mental frame | Design for iteration — first ≠ final | | Confirmation Bias | Use AI to confirm, not challenge | Frame outputs as hypotheses to test |

Key insight: Classical UX psychology (attention, memory, decision-making) applies to AI products — then six new failure modes layer on top. The same product that suffers from choice overload also suffers from automation bias. Designing for AI means mastering both sets.

A user asks an AI for code help, gets a plausible-looking answer, and stops searching even though a better solution exists. Which cognitive bias explains this?

→ ai-design/ai-ux-psychology
insight intermediate

Why A2UI Uses a Flat List, Not a Nested Tree

A2UI's surfaceUpdate payload uses a flat adjacency list with parentId pointers — not a nested component tree. This is intentional:

[
  { "id": "revenue-card", "component": { "Card": {...} } },
  { "id": "revenue-metric", "parentId": "revenue-card", "component": { "Text": {...} } }
]


The client reconstructs the tree from the flat list. LLMs generate flat structures more reliably than deeply nested JSON — deep nesting creates more tokens, more opportunities for bracket errors, and harder-to-recover parsing failures.

Key insight: The protocol shape is designed for reliable LLM generation, not human readability. Flat adjacency lists are harder for humans to write but dramatically easier for LLMs to produce correctly.

Same design principle appears in A2A task messages (flat parts arrays) and in knowledge wikis (flat file structure with links, not deep folder hierarchies).

| |
→ ai-design/a2ui-implementation
quiz beginner

AT Protocol: What Makes Social Data Portable

at://alice.com/app.bsky.feed.post/...  → Alice's post (always resolves to her PDS)
                                           even after she migrates PDS providers


The AT Protocol stack: DID (cryptographic identity) → PDS (your repository) → Relay/Firehose (global aggregation) → AppViews (Bluesky, Tangled, Leaflet).

Key insight: "What open source did for code, open social does for data." The same arc: initial resistance, sustained community effort, eventually the default.

Open protocols prevent lock-in across multiple domains: AT Protocol for social data, A2A for agent coordination, MCP for AI tools.

| |

In AT Protocol, what does a Personal Data Server (PDS) enable that closed social platforms prevent?

→ web/open-social
insight intermediate

34% of your Claude Code tokens go to hooks fighting each other

An 11-day study of 14 Claude Code setups (all running 3+ plugins) found a median 34% of every session's tokens wasted before Claude generated a single useful line. The culprit: PostToolUse cascade.

When Prettier's write triggers ESLint's hook, which writes again, which triggers Prettier again — a single 200-line edit balloons from ~3,200 to ~9,600 tokens:

graph TD
    EDIT["Claude edits file.ts"]
    PTU1["🔥 PostToolUse fires"]
    PRT["Prettier runs → writes file"]
    PTU2["🔥 PostToolUse fires again"]
    ESL["ESLint runs → writes file"]
    PTU3["🔥 PostToolUse fires again"]
    PRT2["Prettier runs again..."]
    COST["💸 3× token cost\n3,200 → 9,600 tokens"]

    EDIT --> PTU1 --> PRT --> PTU2 --> ESL --> PTU3 --> PRT2 --> COST

    style PTU1 fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style PTU2 fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style PTU3 fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style COST fill:#7f1d1d,color:#fff


Key insight: "You think the model is dumb. It isn't. Your agents are fighting each other." The degraded behavior people attribute to model quality is often hook interference — a measurable, fixable infrastructure problem.

| Metric | Value | |--------|-------| | Median waste | 34% per session | | Worst setup | 52% | | Every setup studied | Lost >15% |

Diagnose with: grep "PostToolUse" ~/.claude/logs/$(ls -t ~/.claude/logs/ | head -1) | wc -l — if >2 per edit, you're in a cascade.

| |
→ ai-agents/claude-code-hook-conflicts
insight intermediate

AI Gives Designers Prototyping Superpowers — But Production Still Needs Review

DHH (April 2026), operating Basecamp: *"Designers have been granted prototyping super powers with AI, but the models are still not good enough to consistently one-shot implementations you'd want to merge to master on large, critical applications without programmer review."*

| Context | AI capability | Human role | |---------|--------------|------------| | Prototyping | Exceptional — designers build functional prototypes without a developer | Direct use; taste applied at the prompt level | | Production (large apps) | Falls short — one-shots frequently need review | Programmer review remains essential |

Key insight: Design skills and DESIGN.md have the highest ROI at the prototyping stage — they dramatically improve prototype quality. For production merges on critical applications, programmer review still closes the gap. The gap is closing, but DHH's calibration from a large, long-lived app is the corrective to the common overclaim.

This asymmetry shapes how to invest in AI design tooling: optimize for fast, high-quality prototyping first; production pipelines still need the human review step that prototyping workflows can skip.

| |
→ ai-design/design-taste-for-ai
insight beginner

AI Finds 27-Year-Old Bugs But Fails 50-Step Math

The AI reliability paradox: the same technology that discovers security vulnerabilities humans missed for decades cannot reliably execute multi-step reasoning tasks.

- Project Glasswing found a 27-year-old OpenBSD bug and a 16-year-old FFmpeg bug that survived 5 million automated tests - 50-step agent task at 95% per-step accuracy → 7.7% success rate (0.95^50)

The resolution: AI excels at pattern matching at scale across vast training data, but fails at sequential reasoning where each step depends on the last.

Key insight: Design for breadth, not depth. Shallow tasks across many documents (security scanning, code review) are reliable. Deep sequential tasks (complex refactors, multi-step math) require architectural mitigation: checkpoints, verification, managed infrastructure.

This is why reliability patterns (knowledge compilation, tool search, memory, managed infrastructure) all share one meta-pattern: move work out of the real-time LLM call into pre-computed, verifiable infrastructure.

| |
→ ai-agents/ai-agent-reliability
quiz intermediate

Framework Portability with any-agent

LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, and LlamaIndex each have incompatible APIs. any-agent provides one interface to run an agent on any framework:

from any_agent import AnyAgent, AgentFramework

agent = AnyAgent.create(
    framework=AgentFramework.LANGCHAIN,  # swap to CREWAI, AUTOGEN, etc.
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    tools=[search_tool, read_tool],
)
result = agent.run("Research and summarize the latest news on AI agents")


Key insight: Mozilla's "choice-first" thesis covers three layers independently: framework (any-agent), provider (any-llm), guardrail (any-guardrail). Swap any layer without touching the other two.

| |

What problem does Mozilla's `any-agent` solve that the A2A protocol does not?

→ ai-agents/mozilla-ai
quiz intermediate

Motivated Constraints: Why Does Giving the WHY Improve Prompt Results?

The Motivated Constraint pattern:

❌ "Keep it under 200 words."
✅ "Keep it under 200 words — this goes into a Telegram post where
    anything longer gets cut off by the platform."

❌ "Avoid jargon."
✅ "Avoid jargon — the reader is a non-technical business owner who
    will disengage if they encounter terms they don't understand."


Rules tell the model what. Motivated constraints tell the model what + why, enabling intelligent judgment at edge cases.

Key insight: Specificity defeats generality. Every detail you add removes a degree of freedom where the model would otherwise default to the most statistically probable (= most generic) output.

This connects to the self-correction loop: add "Reread your response. Rate it 1-10 on accuracy, specificity, and actionability. Fix any dimension below 8." — the revised version is better 85–90% of the time. *(single source — needs independent verification)*

| |

You write: 'Keep it under 200 words.' vs. 'Keep it under 200 words — this goes into a Telegram post where anything longer gets cut off.' Why does adding the reason improve the output?

→ ai-agents/prompt-engineering-mastery
insight intermediate

The Confirmation Trap: Why Memory Systems Learn to Be Cautious, Not Right

The single biggest failure mode of any AI memory system: memory fills with "avoid X" rules and zero "prefer Y" rules. Claude learns to be cautious — not to be right.

Why it happens: When Claude makes a mistake, you push back → it writes a "don't do X" rule. When Claude makes a *good* non-obvious call, you move on → nothing is saved. Next week, Claude is equally likely to make either choice again.

❌ Memory over time (trap):            ✅ Memory over time (fixed):
