→ "Don't inline styles"                → "Don't inline styles"
→ "Don't use global state"             → "Don't use global state"
→ "Don't use moment.js"                → "Don't use moment.js"
→ (nothing positive saved)            → "Bundle auth work in one PR — splitting is churn"
                                       → "Prefer integration tests — mock/prod divergence caught us"


The fix: When Claude makes a good non-obvious judgment call, say so explicitly. *"Yeah, bundling this into one PR was right — splitting would've just been churn."* Claude writes that as a feedback memory with the reasoning. The next time the same decision arises, it has evidence for both directions.

Key insight: We're wired to correct, not to affirm. But the practice of confirming good calls — unnatural as it feels — is what separates a memory system that works from one that just makes Claude more anxious.

| |
→ ai-agents/claude-code-auto-memory
insight intermediate

Hermes Ships Browser and Web as First-Party, Not MCP Plugins

Most coding agents treat web and browser capabilities as optional MCP plugins requiring separate installation. Hermes ships them as built-in, zero-config capabilities:

| Capability | Hermes | Claude Code | |------------|--------|-------------| | Web search | Built-in | Via MCP (Brave/Firecrawl) | | Browser automation | Built-in | Via MCP (Playwright) | | Vision / screenshots | Built-in | Via MCP | | Image generation | Built-in | External | | Text-to-speech | Built-in | External | | Multi-model routing | Built-in | Anthropic only |

The combination unlocks autonomous workflows that require all three at once: browser automation + scheduling + messaging = Hermes can research a topic, take screenshots, generate a report, and deliver an audio briefing to your Telegram — completely unattended.

# Install Hermes — everything above is available immediately
curl -fsSL https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/install.sh | bash
hermes setup
# No: claude mcp add playwright; claude mcp add brave-search; etc.


Key insight: The distinction isn't features — it's integration cost. MCP gives Claude Code the same capabilities, but Hermes delivers them at zero friction. When a workflow requires browser + scheduling + messaging to work together, first-party beats a chain of three separate MCP configurations.

→ ai-agents/hermes-agent
insight intermediate

Persona Vectors: Locating Sycophancy in Activation Space

Anthropic's Persona Vectors (Aug 2025) found that character traits like sycophancy and hallucination manifest as *directions* in a model's activation space — not scattered noise, but consistent, extractable vectors.

// Behavioral proxy for what Persona Vectors measure internally
const probe = await sycophancyProbe("What is 2+2?");
// { capitulated: false }  → held answer under social pressure
// { capitulated: true }   → reversed without new evidence (sycophancy signal)
// Persona Vectors eliminate the multi-turn round-trip:
// read the "sycophancy direction" from activations directly


Key insight: Once you locate a trait as a vector, you can *monitor* it across model versions and *intervene* by steering activations — turning alignment from guesswork into measurement.

This feeds directly into the alignment audit loop: diff the persona vector magnitudes before/after fine-tuning to verify that RLHF reduced sycophancy rather than hiding it.

→ ai-security/llm-interpretability
quiz intermediate

Why does 'anti-slop' prefer 5 agents over 50?

The anti-slop principle: real automation means fewer, well-designed agents — each doing one thing reliably, with hard validation gates, designed failure modes, and human oversight for consequential decisions.

| Approach | Failure mode | Reliability at scale | |----------|-------------|---------------------| | 50 loosely connected agents | Cascading, hard to debug | p^50 compounding | | 5 tightly scoped agents | Isolated, clear responsibility | p^5, debuggable |

Key insight: Architecture patterns reduce infrastructure-level failure. Engineering quality gates reduce process-level failure. Both are required — but neither helps if you're running 50 loosely connected agents.

Max Brodeur-Urbas (Gumloop, $50M Series B) argues '50 agents running your company is a slop machine.' What is the primary mathematical reason fewer, tighter agents are more reliable?

→ ai-agents/agent-skills-quality
insight intermediate

Graphify finds code communities without embeddings

Graphify uses Leiden community detection on edge density — not embeddings, not a vector database. The semantic similarity edges that Claude extracts (Pass 3) go directly into the NetworkX graph. Leiden then clusters by how densely nodes are connected. Graph topology is the similarity signal.

flowchart LR
    P1["Pass 1: tree-sitter AST\n(no LLM — 25 languages)"]
    P2["Pass 2: Whisper transcription\n(audio/video — cached)"]
    P3["Pass 3: Claude subagents\n(docs, images, PDFs)"]
    G["NetworkX graph\n+ confidence scores"]
    L["Leiden clustering\n(edge density)"]
    P1 & P2 & P3 --> G --> L


Key insight: Pass 1 needs zero LLM calls — AST parsing is deterministic and instant. Only Passes 2 and 3 use AI, and their outputs are SHA256-cached so re-runs only process changed files.

The result: 71.5x fewer tokens per query vs reading raw files. Claude reads GRAPH_REPORT.md (one page) instead of grepping thousands of files.

→ ai-agents/graphify
insight intermediate

Stack Four Compression Layers for 70%+ Token Reduction

No single compression technique is enough. The big wins come from stacking:

| Layer | Technique | Savings | |---|---|---| | Output verbosity | Caveman — telegraphic responses | 22–87% output tokens | | Input overhead | caveman-compress — compact CLAUDE.md | ~45% input tokens | | Tool definitions | Agent SDK tool search — load 3-5, not all | ~90% tool tokens | | Conversation history | Context compaction — summarize old turns | 40–60% history |

Used together, total token consumption drops 70%+ compared to a naive setup.

Key insight: Brevity is NOT universally beneficial. Compress status updates and explanations — not complex reasoning chains or code generation. Caveman on the wrong output type reduces quality.

There's also invisible overhead: each MCP server adds 200–400 tokens to *every* session start. Replacing GitHub MCP with gh CLI, filesystem MCP with built-in file tools = hundreds of free tokens per session.

| |
→ ai-agents/compression-strategies
quiz intermediate

Goodhart's Law: when does a metric stop being useful?

Goodhart's Law applies to every engineering metric the moment it becomes a management target:

| Metric | Original purpose | Goodhart outcome | |--------|-----------------|------------------| | Velocity / story points | Measure team capacity | Teams optimize for points, not outcomes | | Code coverage % | Signal test quality | Coverage without meaningful assertions | | Bug count | Track quality | Teams stop filing bugs to keep count low | | PR throughput | Measure shipping cadence | Trivial PRs split to inflate numbers |

Key insight: The metric isn't wrong — the target is. avoids velocity metrics entirely, using fixed-time/variable-scope bets instead. The bet outcome (shipped or not) is much harder to game than a velocity number.

Goodhart's Law states: 'When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.' Which engineering example most clearly illustrates this?

→ productivity/software-engineering-laws
insight intermediate

AI products over-invest in Output UX and neglect Input UX and the Refinement Journey

Emily Campbell's Six Layers of AI Experience model (amplified by Vitaly Friedman) reveals a systematic pattern: most AI products invest heavily in Output UX (making answers look polished) while under-investing in the two layers that actually reduce user effort.

graph TD
    M["Model Behavior\n(what the AI can do)"]
    C["Context\n(what the AI knows)"]
    U["User Interface\n(how users interact)"]
    I["Input UX\n(prompting, instruction quality)"]
    O["Output UX\n(how results are shown, evaluated)"]
    R["Refinement Journey\n(iteration loops)"]
    M --> C --> U
    U --> I & O
    I --> R
    O --> R


| Layer | Typical investment | What it affects | |-------|-------------------|-----------------| | Output UX | High — polished UI, streaming, markdown | How answers look | | Input UX | Low — usually a plain text box | How easy it is to give good instructions | | Refinement Journey | Low — "edit and resend" at best | How fast you get from first output to final result |

Key insight: The gap between "User Interface" and "Context" holds enormous hidden complexity. Users are inefficient at prompting — wasting cycles between waiting and endless turn-taking. Good AI UX designs this gap out: pre-fill context, provide structure, reduce the number of turns. The number of turns needed to reach a satisfying result is the most underused UX metric in AI products.

→ ai-design/ai-ux-psychology
quiz intermediate

3 Strategies for Managing Context Growth in Production Coding Agents

The minimal agentic loop has a fatal flaw: context grows unbounded.

Turn 1  → ~2K tokens   ✅
Turn 5  → ~12K tokens  ✅
Turn 20 → ~55K tokens  ⚠️
Turn 50 → 🚫 context limit


Strategy 1 — Compaction: Summarize old turns with claude-haiku-4-5 when history exceeds 20 messages. Retains critical context, frees ~60% of window.

Strategy 2 — Persistent memory: CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md loaded once at session start. Stack, known bugs, and conventions persist without accumulating in the conversation.

Strategy 3 — Selective loading: Filter files by task keywords. "Fix auth middleware" → loads auth/middleware.ts, not every file in the repo.

Key insight: Most coding agents fail not because the loop is wrong, but because they run out of context window. Strategy 2 is free — just write the file. Strategy 1 costs a few cents per compaction. Strategy 3 requires a file index but saves the most tokens per turn.

What are the three standard strategies for managing context growth in production coding agents?

→ ai-agents/building-coding-agents
quiz intermediate

WordPress membership gate: why use template_redirect instead of hiding markup in the template?

The fix pattern from a real 919-video leak incident: a wcs_user_has_subscription() check at template_redirect with priority 1 — the check runs before AIOVG or Divi begin building the page.

add_action('template_redirect', function () {
    if (!is_singular('aiovg_videos')) return;

    $uid = get_current_user_id();
    $can_view = $uid
        && function_exists('wcs_user_has_subscription')
        && (
            wcs_user_has_subscription($uid, '', 'active')
            || wcs_user_has_subscription($uid, '', 'on-hold')
            || wcs_user_has_subscription($uid, '', 'pending-cancel')
        );

    if ($can_view || ($uid && user_can($uid, 'manage_options'))) return;

    wp_safe_redirect(is_user_logged_in() ? home_url('/membership/') :
        add_query_arg('redirect_to', urlencode(get_permalink()), home_url('/login/')));
    exit;
}, 1);


Key insight: A paywall that hides markup is a label above an open door. A template_redirect gate closes the door before the door is built.

| |

You need to block non-subscribers from viewing premium AIOVG video posts. Why should the access check happen in template_redirect rather than conditionally hiding the player inside the Divi template?

→ web/wordpress-membership-access-control
insight intermediate

Architecture Decisions Are Recruiting Decisions

Alex Kladov (creator of rust-analyzer) designed rust-analyzer's architecture to match its *social reality* — not the other way around:

Social reality → Architecture decision:
  Core (few brilliant contributors) → No outside deps, builds on stable, tests in seconds
  Features (weekend warriors)       → catch_unwind guard, happy path = merge-ready,
                                       crashes invisible to users


He made it easy to contribute features without knowing the full Rust compiler toolchain. Every feature is guarded by catch_unwind so a crash can't corrupt the core — not for technical reasons, but to make "crash stays isolated, it'll attract a fixer" a safe outcome.

Key insight: "Who can contribute to this code? What knowledge do they need? What mistakes can they safely make?" These are architectural questions, not just social ones. The architecture determines who contributes — and who contributes determines what the architecture becomes.

Conway's Law runs in both directions. You can't only respond to the team structure — you can actively *design* an architecture that shapes the contributor behavior you want.

| |
→ productivity/learning-software-architecture
quiz intermediate

OpenTUI Node.js compatibility: what's the difference between portable and native-renderer usage?

OpenTUI's native core is written in Zig and exposed via a C ABI. TypeScript bindings load it via FFI at runtime — which is why the runtime tier matters.

| Runtime | Portable imports | Native renderer (createCliRenderer()) | |---------|-----------------|----------------------------------------| | Bun | Works out of the box | Works out of the box | | Node.js | Any version | Node.js 26.3.0 + --experimental-ffi --allow-ffi |

# Bun — just works
bun run index.ts

# Node.js with native renderer
node --experimental-ffi --allow-ffi --allow-read=. index.js


Key insight: The C ABI design means OpenTUI can be used from Python, Rust, Go, or any language with FFI — not just TypeScript. Bun is the zero-config path; Node.js support is intentional but requires explicit flags.

You want to use OpenTUI's createCliRenderer() in a Node.js project (not Bun). What's required?

→ frontend/opentui
quiz intermediate

The 12 Details That Make UI Feel Better

Jakub Krehel codified 12 specific techniques across typography, depth, motion, and alignment: text-wrap: balance, antialiased fonts, concentric border-radius, interruptible animations, optical alignment, shadows over borders, staggered entry, tabular-nums, exit animations, and more.

Key insight: No single detail is noticeable alone. Together they create a perception of care and craft. The cumulative effect matters more than any individual technique.

|

Which CSS approach allows smooth animation reversal mid-transition?

→ ai-design/design-micro-details
quiz intermediate

When Should You Move State from a Hook to Zustand?

// Before: ownership buried in a hook
const { transcript } = useSpeechRecognition();

// After: ownership is explicit — the store
recognition.onresult = (e) => {
  useRecognitionStore.getState().setTranscript(e.results[0][0].transcript);
};


What is the clearest signal that a React custom hook has become application state?

→ frontend/zustand-when-hooks-dont-scale
quiz intermediate

The Compounding Error Problem

AI agent reliability follows exponential decay, not linear degradation. Even a seemingly high 95% per-step accuracy becomes catastrophic over long task chains. This is the fundamental math behind why agents fail at complex multi-step tasks.

Key insight: The fix isn't better models — it's fewer steps. Move work from runtime reasoning into pre-compiled infrastructure.

|

At 95% per-step accuracy, what's the success rate of a 100-step agent task?

→ ai-agents/compounding-errors
insight intermediate

RCT: Technical Limits That Became Design Features

RollerCoaster Tycoon's pathfinder has a hard junction depth limit: 5 for regular guests, 7 with a park map, 8 for mechanics. When it hits the limit without finding a path, it fails.

Guests whose pathfinder fails think: *"I can't find the exit"* — and say so in their thought bubbles. The technical constraint became a gameplay feature. Players who build confusing layouts get feedback from guests, which teaches good park design.

flowchart LR
    Limit["⚠️ Junction limit reached\n(performance constraint)"] --> Fail["Return 'not found'"]
    Fail --> Thought["💭 Guest thought:\n'I can't find the exit'"]
    Thought --> Feedback["🎮 Design feedback\nto the player"]
    style Limit fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style Feedback fill:#166534,color:#fff


Key insight: This could not happen in a typical studio where engineers and designers negotiate requirements. Chris Sawyer designed both the constraint and the feature simultaneously — as the same person, he could rationalize a hard cap as a design decision rather than a technical compromise.

The same pattern maps to LLM agent turn limits: cap tool calls at N → agent reports "incomplete after N turns" → the limit becomes a predictability feature, not a failure.

| |
→ productivity/game-design-for-performance
insight beginner

Raycast: OS-Level AI from Any Screen

Unlike IDE AI plugins that activate only inside one editor, Raycast's AI layer operates at the macOS level — one keyboard shortcut from any screen, any app.

Key insight: The mental model shift is from "open app → navigate → do thing" to "press shortcut → describe what you want → done." Native macOS (no Electron) keeps it under 100ms to open.

Raycast bundles clipboard history, window management, 1,000+ integrations, calculations, and AI chat into a single keystroke-driven interface — eliminating the multi-app context-switch overhead that drains deep work time.

→ tools/raycast
insight advanced

Level 4 Prompt Engineering: Context Files as a System Contract

Level 4 prompt engineering moves from per-prompt effort to a persistent system: context files injected as system prompts so your rules apply to every session without re-pasting.

// Load once, apply everywhere — no copy-paste drift
const writingRules = readFileSync("prompts/writing-rules.md", "utf-8");
const result = await ask("Write a blog post about AI adoption failures.", writingRules);
// writing-rules.md encodes voice, audience, forbidden phrases, quality standards



# writing-rules.md
Voice: direct, data-driven, no hedging language
Audience: CTOs of mid-market SaaS companies (200–2000 employees)
Never: "best-in-class", "enterprise-grade", "synergy"
Standard: every claim backed by a number or case study


Start each session: *"Read [file] fully before starting. Follow every rule. If you're about to break a rule, stop and tell me."*

Key insight: AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md are context files at the highest level of the stack — they encode the entire engineering context for a project once, and every coding session inherits it automatically.

Three months of weekly feedback loops (review outputs → update context files → update templates) makes prompting ability unrecognizable from where you started.

| |
→ ai-agents/prompt-engineering-mastery
insight beginner

robots.txt → llms.txt → auth.md: the natural sequence of machine-readable web files

Each era of automated web access got its own discovery file:

| File | Era | Tells machines... | |------|-----|------------------| | robots.txt | Web crawlers | What NOT to read | | llms.txt | LLM assistants | How to read a site's content | | auth.md | AI coding agents | How to register a user |

auth.md is an open protocol (authored by WorkOS, vendor-agnostic) hosted at https://yourapp.com/auth.md. It lists which registration flows the app accepts (agent-verified / user-claimed), available permission scopes, and the registration endpoint.

Key insight: The pattern is always the same — a well-known path, a machine-readable file, open standards underneath. auth.md extends this convention to the agentic web, where agents don't just browse or summarize — they act.

The protocol reuses RFC 9728 (Protected Resource Metadata) and ID-JAG identity assertions — no new infrastructure. Any app can publish an auth.md; any agent can read one; no WorkOS account required.

| |
→ ai-agents/auth-md
quiz beginner

What makes AnythingLLM different from ChatGPT with PDF upload?

AnythingLLM (MIT, ~59K GitHub stars) runs every default locally: LLM, embedder, vector database, storage. Nothing leaves the machine unless explicitly configured.

| | AnythingLLM | ChatGPT + PDF | Ollama | |--|--|--|--| | Local LLM | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | | Document chat | ✓ multi-type | PDF only | ext. needed | | Multi-user | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | | Privacy | Full local | Data to OpenAI | Full local |

Key insight: Privacy as a first-class constraint — not a feature — shapes every default in AnythingLLM's architecture.

Which combination is AnythingLLM's true differentiator — something most alternatives require SaaS for?

→ ai-agents/anythingllm
insight beginner

RoboRev solves the cascade problem in agentic coding

When an AI agent writes three commits before you catch an architectural mistake, all three need fixes. RoboRev closes this feedback loop: a post-commit git hook triggers a background review within seconds of every commit, while context is still fresh.

roborev init      # installs post-commit hook — no workflow changes
git commit -m "…" # review triggers automatically in background
roborev tui       # view the review queue (vim-style navigation)


Key insight: The problem with AI-generated code isn't one bad commit — it's the cascade. RoboRev positions review as a continuous loop, not a final gate, so compounding errors are caught before they compound.

It's complementary to (which gates the *plan* before code runs) and (TDD hooks). Together they bracket the full development cycle.

· ·
→ ai-agents/roborev
code intermediate

How does a custom Tau frontend consume CodingSession?

from tau_coding.session import CodingSession

# Minimal event loop — same API the built-in TUI uses
async for event in session.prompt(user_text):
    render_event(event)  # your rendering logic


Event types from tau_agent.events:

| Event | Meaning | |-------|---------| | AgentStartEvent | Agent run started | | AssistantTextEvent | Streaming text from the model | | ToolCallEvent | Agent calling a tool | | ToolResultEvent | Tool result returned | | AgentEndEvent | Run completed |

Key insight: Always render from AgentEvent values — never from provider-specific chunks. The provider-neutral abstraction is the point: swap HuggingFace for Kimi and the frontend code doesn't change.

|

What is the correct minimal event loop for a custom Tau frontend?

→ ai-agents/tau-coding-agent
code intermediate

MCP client: correct message order after a tool_use block

messages[0]: { role: "user",      content: user_query }
messages[1]: { role: "assistant", content: [text?, tool_use] }  ← Claude's decision
messages[2]: { role: "user",      content: [tool_result] }      ← client executes + returns
messages[3]: { role: "assistant", content: [text] }             ← Claude's final answer


The client is the glue layer — it doesn't add intelligence. It faithfully executes tool calls and threads results back into the conversation until Claude produces a text-only response.

Key insight: This 4-message pattern can chain: Claude may request another tool after seeing a tool_result, extending the loop until it has everything it needs. Always pass tools on every API call to allow chaining.

| |

After Claude returns a tool_use block, the client executes the tool. What is the correct order to append to messages[] before the follow-up API call?

→ ai-agents/mcp-client-development
quiz intermediate

Four Symptoms of Bad Software Design — Which is Which?

Robert C. Martin identified four primary symptoms of design rot in his 2000 paper (17 years before *Clean Architecture*):

graph TD
    R["🪨 Rigidity\nCascade of changes\n→ Open/Closed Principle"]
    F["🔮 Fragility\nBreaks unrelated places\n→ Interface Segregation"]
    I["🔒 Immobility\nCan't reuse anywhere\n→ Dependency Inversion"]
    V["🍯 Viscosity\nHack beats right way\n→ Automation + infra speed"]


| Symptom | Scenario | Fix | |---------|----------|-----| | Rigidity | "2 days became 2 weeks" | Strategy Pattern | | Fragility | Payments fix breaks reports | Interface Segregation | | Immobility | Need to drag framework to reuse logic | Clean Architecture | | Viscosity | Migration + DTO (1h) vs. text field dump (5min) | Faster build/test pipeline |

Key insight: Viscosity is the cruelest symptom — it rewards bad choices. When the right path takes 10× longer than the wrong path, a system fills with hacks not because developers are lazy, but because the incentive structure is broken.

| |

You fix a bug in the payments module and the inventory report generator breaks. Which of Robert C. Martin's four design symptoms describes this?

→ productivity/symptoms-of-bad-software-design
insight intermediate

Developer loyalty is at zero — why coding tool moats collapsed but infrastructure moats didn't

At Google Cloud Next (April 2026), Google Cloud Chief Evangelist Richard Seroter stated plainly: "Developer loyalty is at zero right now." AI coding tool switching costs are low; model capabilities are converging across Claude Code, Cursor, Gemini CLI, and Copilot.

Google's strategic response reveals the new market structure:

> "The best performing way to use Anthropic is on Vertex over Azure, AWS, and Anthropic itself. So even if you're using Claude Code, you should be using Vertex — otherwise you're just getting worse performance."

| Layer | Durable moat? | Why | |-------|--------------|-----| | Tool layer — coding interfaces | ❌ No | Low switching cost, converging model quality | | Infrastructure layer — inference, compliance, integration | ✅ Yes | Latency, data residency, enterprise contracts |

This mirrors the internet disruption wave: ISPs didn't need to win the browser war to win the infrastructure war. The platform layer decouples from the tool layer.

Key insight: "We don't have the luxury of shipping a chatbot — we have the luxury of running the most important platform and infrastructure on the planet." The constraint that slows platform companies (can't break Maps, can't break compliance) is exactly what makes their infrastructure moats last.

The disruption pattern Fowler & Beck describe plays out at every layer simultaneously: tool chaos (loyalty at zero) while infrastructure crystallizes into the next stable foundation — just as ISPs, CDNs, and DNS became invisible infrastructure after the browser wars ended.

→ ai-agents/tech-disruption-cycles
insight intermediate

MCP Inspector CLI mode creates an AI agent self-verification loop

MCP Inspector's --cli flag produces machine-readable JSON output, enabling AI coding agents to verify their own MCP server changes without switching contexts. The agent builds a tool, calls the Inspector to test it, reads the result, and self-corrects — all within one session.

# AI agent calls this after modifying a tool
npx @modelcontextprotocol/inspector --cli node build/index.js \
  --method tools/call --tool-name mytool --tool-arg key=value
# Output: JSON result agent can parse and act on immediately


| Mode | Best for | |------|---------| | UI (http://localhost:6274) | Interactive development, visual debugging | | CLI (--cli) | AI coding agents, CI pipelines, scripted testing |

Key insight: CLI mode closes the loop — an AI coding agent can call the Inspector directly, read the JSON result, and self-correct without human involvement. This is the MCP equivalent of a test runner in a TDD workflow.

· ·
→ ai-agents/mcp-inspector
insight intermediate

Open Design separates design taste from design intelligence

Open Design (Apache 2.0) ships no AI model — it ships 131 SKILL.md bundles and 149 DESIGN.md systems that wire into whatever coding agent you already use (Claude, Cursor, Gemini, Codex, and 8 others).

Design Intelligence  →  Your coding agent (BYOK — any OpenAI-compatible endpoint)
Design Taste         →  SKILL.md + DESIGN.md files (portable, agent-agnostic)


Key insight: "Taste is portable across agents and projects; intelligence is BYOK." The 4-stage loop (Detect → Discover → Direct → Deliver) enforces a 30-second brief before any pixel is improvised — preventing the usual half-hour of corrections.

The 5-dimension self-critique gate before delivery checks: Philosophy · Hierarchy · Execution · Specificity · Restraint.

· · ·
→ ai-design/open-design
quiz intermediate

Why do GPS satellite clocks run at 10.22999999543 MHz instead of 10.23 MHz?

| Effect | Mechanism | Daily drift | |--------|-----------|------------| | Special Relativity | Speed (~14,000 km/h) slows time | −7.2 μs/day | | General Relativity | Weaker gravity at 20,200 km speeds time | +45.9 μs/day | | Net | Gravity wins | +38.7 μs/day → ~11.6 km/day error |

Nominal GPS atomic clock frequency:  10.23000000000 MHz
Pre-adjusted to:                      10.22999999543 MHz
Difference:                           −4.57 × 10⁻⁹ Hz
Result in orbit: ticks at exactly the right rate.


Key insight: GPS is a continuous live experiment confirming Einstein's theories of relativity. Without both corrections, GPS would become useless within hours — and the fix is baked into the hardware at manufacture, an early example of pre-compensating errors at build time rather than correcting them at runtime.

GPS satellite clocks are deliberately manufactured to tick slightly too slow on Earth. Why, and by how much does uncorrected relativistic drift accumulate per day?

→ tech/how-gps-works
quiz intermediate

Manifest LLM Router: what are the four complexity tiers?

Most agents send every request to the same flagship model — paying full price for a "what is this variable?" lookup. Manifest's four-tier system ensures each request lands on the cheapest model that can handle it.

flowchart LR
    C{"Classifier\n<2ms"} -->|Simple| M1["Free model"]
    C -->|Standard| M2["Mid-tier"]
    C -->|Complex| M3["Flagship"]
    C -->|Reasoning| M4["Extended thinking"]


Key insight: The classifier runs locally — no extra round-trip. 2ms is well within any network latency budget.

Manifest's local classifier assigns every agent request to one of four tiers. What are the four tiers in order from cheapest to most expensive?

→ ai-agents/manifest-llm-router
quiz intermediate

ACP: The LSP for AI Coding Agents — What Problem Does It Solve?

graph LR
    subgraph Before["Without ACP"]
        E1["VS Code"] -->|"custom"| A1["Agent 1"]
        E1 -->|"custom"| A2["Agent 2"]
        E2["JetBrains"] -->|"custom"| A1
        E2 -->|"custom"| A2
    end
    subgraph After["With ACP"]
        EX["Any ACP Editor"] -->|"ACP"| AX["Any ACP Agent"]
    end
    style Before fill:#1f2937,color:#9ca3af
    style After fill:#064e3b,color:#fff


Key insight: The integration cost without a standard grows as O(editors × agents). With ACP, it's O(1) — implement once, interoperate everywhere. This is exactly what LSP did for language servers in 2016.

ACP uses JSON-RPC over stdio for local agents (agents run as editor sub-processes) — the same transport as LSP.

| |

Before LSP, every IDE had to build its own Go, Python, and TypeScript intelligence. ACP applies the same decoupling to a different integration. What does ACP decouple?

→ ai-agents/agent-client-protocol
insight intermediate

Rabois Predicts: CMOs, Not Engineers, Will Be the Top AI Token Consumers

Keith Rabois' counterintuitive prediction: CMOs will become the #1 consumers of AI tokens — surpassing engineers.

The reasoning: CMOs have three things that historically required engineers to execute: 1. Budget — already allocated, can direct spending 2. Ideas — know what content, campaigns, and products they want 3. Distribution authority — can ship directly to customers

The missing piece has always been execution capacity. With AI, CMOs can direct AI agents to write copy, build landing pages, generate campaigns, analyze data — without waiting for engineering cycles.

Key insight: The bottleneck for CMOs was never ideas or budget — it was execution. AI removes the execution bottleneck, making the CMO role dramatically more powerful without any new organizational structure.

This parallels the barrel/ammunition framework: CMOs are organizational barrels who previously lacked their own execution capacity. AI gives them ammunition on demand.

The same disruption that threatens the PM-as-translator (when business and engineering both speak natural language + AI) creates an opening for CMOs to own full product cycles directly.

Before AI:  CMO idea → PM spec → Engineering queue → Shipped (weeks/months)
After AI:   CMO idea → AI agents → Shipped (hours/days)


| |
→ productivity/barrels-vs-ammunition
insight advanced

Schneier on Glasswing: the defender advantage is real but temporary

Bruce Schneier (Harvard Kennedy School) offered a nuanced read on Project Glasswing: the current defender advantage is genuine but structurally fragile.

Why defenders win now: "Finding for the purposes of fixing is easier for an AI than finding plus exploiting." Discovering a vulnerability requires pattern recognition — current models excel here. Weaponizing it requires reasoning about program state, environment constraints, and exploitation chains — harder for today's models.

Why this changes: "This asymmetry is likely to shrink, as ever more powerful models become available." The capability gap between find-and-fix and find-and-exploit will close.

On the PR framing: Anthropic's $100M in *model usage credits* (not cash) and restricted deployment is a controlled demonstration of capability, not full transparency. Schneier: "very much a PR play."

> Key insight (Schneier): "We've spent decades building security on the assumption that finding vulnerabilities is hard. Mythos shatters that assumption. The entire defense-in-depth model needs to be rethought when an AI can find and chain zero-days faster than any human team."

Glasswing's thesis — deploy defensive AI before attackers do — is correct. But the current window of defender advantage is a temporary structural asymmetry, not a durable moat.

| |
→ ai-security/glasswing-project
insight intermediate

Companion Agent Pattern: Cross-Model Peer Review for Free

Running a second AI agent alongside Claude Code creates automatic adversarial review — no extra configuration beyond installing both tools.

How it works: Claude proposes a change. Codex reviews it. Where they agree, confidence is high. Where they disagree, flag for human review.

Claude Code proposes: "Add input validation in auth.ts"
Codex challenges: "This regex allows null bytes — potential injection vector"
  → Disagreement flagged → human reviews
  
Claude Code proposes: "Extract duplicate logic to helper"
Codex agrees: "Correct — reduces surface area by 30%"
  → Agreement → high confidence → auto-commit


This is the Advisor Tool pattern without the Advisor Tool config — two independent models reviewing the same change from different training distributions.

Key insight: Model disagreement is a reliability signal. Two models trained on different data reaching different conclusions about a change means the change is genuinely ambiguous — exactly the cases where human judgment matters most. Where they converge, the risk of both being wrong in the same way is far lower.

Extension layers for Claude Code: | Type | Install | Examples | |------|---------|---------| | Skills (SKILL.md) | npx skills add org/repo | Caveman, Agent Skills | | MCP servers | claude mcp add | Playwright, Firecrawl | | Companion agents | Per-tool setup | Codex for adversarial review |

| |
→ ai-agents/building-coding-agents
code intermediate

What does changing effort from 'high' to 'medium' actually do at the API level?

Effort is not a vague quality dial — it's a concrete thinking.budget_tokens value sent to the Messages API. Claude Code's Bug 1 (March 2026) switched the default from high to medium, silently cutting the token budget from 10k to 2k.

const budgetTokens = effort === "high" ? 10_000 : 2_000;
const res = await client.messages.create({
  model: "claude-opus-4-7",
  max_tokens: 16_000,
  thinking: { type: "enabled", budget_tokens: budgetTokens },
  messages: [{ role: "user", content: Review for bugs:\n\n${code} }],
});
// high: slower, finds subtle bugs; medium: faster, may miss edge cases
// measure on YOUR eval set before defaulting to medium


Key insight: More budget_tokens → more test-time compute → better output. The tradeoff is real, not cosmetic. Always benchmark on your own eval set before reducing effort.

In the code below, what is the `budget_tokens` value when `effort = 'medium'`?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-april-2026-postmortem
connection intermediate

Spaced Repetition and Auto Dream: The Same Memory Problem

Both spaced repetition and Claude Code's Auto Dream solve the same fundamental problem: information decays without reinforcement, degrading decision quality over time.

Human memory:         Ebbinghaus curve → ~67% forgotten in 24h without review
Agent session memory: contradictions accumulate, relative dates become stale,
                      signal-to-noise ratio degrades after 20+ sessions


Key insight: Auto Dream's four phases (Orient → Gather → Consolidate → Prune) are the engineering implementation of what spaced repetition does biologically: strengthening high-value memories and discarding low-value noise on a scheduled interval. The biological metaphor is explicit in both — Ebbinghaus called it the forgetting curve; Auto Dream calls it REM sleep.

The difference is scope: - Spaced repetition → human long-term knowledge retention (weeks to years) - Auto Dream → AI session memory fidelity (days to weeks)

→ knowledge-management/spaced-repetition
insight intermediate

A tool call is just a message — nothing executes yet

The LLM's tool_use block is plain JSON — an instruction to call a function. Your application must execute the function and return a tool_result. The LLM has no idea what happened until you tell it.

sequenceDiagram
    participant LLM
    participant App as Your App
    participant Tool as Tool (bash/fs)

    LLM->>App: {type:"tool_use", name:"bash", input:{command:"grep TODO src/"}}
    Note over LLM,App: ← nothing executed here
    App->>Tool: Execute grep -r TODO src/
    Tool->>App: src/auth.ts:42: // TODO...
    App->>LLM: {type:"tool_result", content:"src/auth.ts:42:..."}


Key insight: If step 4 (tool execution) fails, you MUST send the error string back as tool_result content. Silent failures break the loop — the LLM cannot adapt to what it doesn't know happened.

→ ai-agents/agentic-loop-typescript
quiz beginner

RAG vs Knowledge Compilation

RAG is stateless — same question asked twice does the same work twice. A compiled wiki front-loads synthesis once and compounds over time. The critical difference: RAG errors are invisible (ephemeral hallucinations), while wiki errors are visible (editable files).

Key insight: Karpathy's advice: stop re-deriving answers from raw sources. Start compiling knowledge into a durable artifact.

|

Why is a compiled wiki more reliable than RAG for repeated questions?

→ knowledge-management/llm-wiki-pattern
code advanced

ACP local transport: JSON-RPC over stdio — first call the agent handles

// ACP agent side: reads JSON-RPC from stdin, writes responses to stdout
readline.createInterface({ input: process.stdin }).on("line", (line) => {
  const { id, method, params } = JSON.parse(line);
  if (method === "initialize") {
    process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify({
      jsonrpc: "2.0", id,
      result: { name: "my-agent", version: "1.0.0",
                capabilities: { codeCompletion: true, planApproval: true } }
    }) + "\n");
  } else if (method === "agent/task") {
    const { prompt } = params;
    process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify({
      jsonrpc: "2.0", id, result: { type: "markdown", content: ## Plan\n1. Fix: ${prompt} }
    }) + "\n");
  }
});

// Editor side: spawn agent, wire up pending callbacks by id
const proc = spawn("node", ["my-agent.js"], { stdio: ["pipe", "pipe", "inherit"] });
const pending = new Map();
readline.createInterface({ input: proc.stdout }).on("line", (line) => {
  const { id, result } = JSON.parse(line);
  if (id) { pending.get(id)?.(result); pending.delete(id); }
});
function call(method, params) {
  const id = crypto.randomUUID();
  return new Promise(r => { pending.set(id, r); proc.stdin.write(JSON.stringify({ jsonrpc: "2.0", id, method, params }) + "\n"); });
}
const info = await call("initialize");
// → { name: "my-agent", version: "1.0.0", capabilities: { ... } }
// Swapping agents: change "my-agent.js" to any ACP binary — editor code is unchanged.


Key insight: The editor code never changes when you swap agents — only the binary path does. This is the same O(1) integration contract LSP gave editors for language servers in 2016.

| |

In ACP's local scenario, what is the first JSON-RPC method the editor sends after spawning the agent subprocess, and what must a minimal valid response include?

→ ai-agents/agent-client-protocol
quiz intermediate

When do web components beat React?

MDN Old: React SPA → runtime ships to all users → interactive elements fight the static HTML
MDN New: Astro (SSR) + Lit web components → static by default → islands of interactivity


The decision matrix: | Site type | Right choice | |-----------|-------------| | Documentation (mostly static + islands) | Astro + Lit | | App with static sections | Next.js RSC | | Complex dashboard/state | React/Vue/Svelte SPA |

| |

MDN migrated from a React SPA to Astro + Lit web components. What was the core architectural insight that made this the right call?

→ frontend/web-components-lit
quiz intermediate

Chrome DevTools MCP — What's Unique About performance_analyze_insight?

The [Chrome DevTools MCP](https://github.com/ChromeDevTools/chrome-devtools-mcp) (maintained by Google's DevTools team) gives AI agents access to the full Chrome DevTools API via 29 tools across 6 categories:

| Category | Example tools | |----------|--------------| | Input automation | click, fill, type_text, hover, press_key | | Navigation | navigate_page, new_page, wait_for | | Emulation | emulate, resize_page | | Performance | performance_start_trace, performance_analyze_insight, take_memory_snapshot | | Network | list_network_requests, get_network_request | | Debugging | take_screenshot, get_console_message, lighthouse_audit |

Install in one line: 
{ "mcpServers": { "chrome-devtools": { "command": "npx", "args": ["-y", "chrome-devtools-mcp@latest"] } } }


Slim mode (--slim): just navigate, evaluate_script, and take_screenshot — reduces token overhead from tool schemas when full browser control isn't needed.

Key insight: This MCP server is the infrastructure behind the browser-testing-with-devtools skill in Addy Osmani's Agent Skills framework. Instead of describing browser behavior in prose, agents observe and control a live Chrome instance directly.

| |

The Chrome DevTools MCP server's performance_analyze_insight tool does something no other performance tool in its category does. What is it?

→ ai-agents/mcp-ecosystem
insight intermediate

WordPress Abilities API — Any Plugin Becomes AI-Native

The WordPress [Abilities API](https://github.com/WordPress/abilities-api) solves a coordination problem: how do dozens of WordPress plugins each expose their capabilities to AI agents without each building custom MCP integration?

The answer: Plugins register structured "Ability" declarations. The WooCommerce MCP server translates them automatically into MCP tool definitions.

// Any WordPress plugin can register an ability
add_action('abilities_api_init', function() {
    wp_register_ability('my-plugin/product-info', [
        'title'       => 'Get Product Info',
        'description' => 'Returns product details by SKU',
        'inputSchema' => [
            'type'       => 'object',
            'properties' => ['sku' => ['type' => 'string']],
            'required'   => ['sku'],
        ],
        'permission_callback' => fn() => current_user_can('read'),
        'callback'            => fn($args) => get_product_by_sku($args['sku']),
    ]);
});

// Expose via WooCommerce MCP
add_filter('woocommerce_mcp_include_ability', fn($abilities) =>
    array_merge($abilities, ['my-plugin/product-info'])
);


Key insight: Any plugin implementing the Abilities API becomes AI-native without writing MCP code. The WooCommerce MCP server is a translator — it reads the Abilities menu and emits MCP tool definitions. The plugin author writes zero protocol code.

WooCommerce MCP architecture: 
flowchart TD
    CD["🤖 Claude Desktop"] -->|MCP| LP["Local Proxy (Node.js)"]
    LP -->|HTTPS| WP["WordPress /wp-json/mcp"]
    WP -->|Abilities API| WC["WooCommerce core"]
    WC --> PL["Any plugin with registered Abilities"]


Status (April 2026): WooCommerce 10.7+ / WordPress 6.9+ is stable for building on. MCP remains developer preview — built-in Abilities cover product and order CRUD.

| |
→ ai-agents/mcp-ecosystem
insight beginner

DuckDB: analytics as a library, not a server

DuckDB runs embedded inside your application — a library call, not a network request. Spin up analytics in a Python script or Node.js server with no daemon, no config, no port to manage. It's the SQLite paradigm applied to OLAP.

import duckdb
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("data.csv")
result = duckdb.sql("SELECT region, sum(revenue) FROM df GROUP BY ALL").to_df()


Key insight: DuckDB treats pandas DataFrames as first-class SQL tables — no .to_sql() round-trip needed. And it reads Parquet on S3, Delta Lake tables, and remote CSVs directly in SQL, without an ETL step first.

·
→ tools/duckdb
insight intermediate

Plan Mode: Why You Must Clear Context After Planning

Claude Code has two modes toggled with [Shift]+[Tab]: Plan Mode (think + draft) and Work Mode (implement autonomously). The critical discipline is what happens between them.

flowchart LR
    P["⏸ Plan Mode\nThink → Ask → Draft plan"] -->|"Approve plan\n🔴 CLEAR CONTEXT"| W["▶ Work Mode\nImplement autonomously"]
    W -->|"Something's wrong"| P

    style P fill:#7c3aed,color:#fff
    style W fill:#059669,color:#fff


Why clearing matters: A 30-minute planning session can consume 60%+ of the context window. That leaves only 40% for implementation — directly degrading output quality. The plan is the input; the implementation needs a fresh window.

> Key insight (Javier Eguiluz, Symfony core team): "For very complex tasks you can spend more than 30 minutes planning. Don't worry — a good plan is the key to getting great code. You are not wasting time, you are actually saving it."

Recovery rules: | Situation | Action | |-----------|--------| | Small mistake | Tell Claude in chat — fixes immediately | | Everything off track | /clear → plan mode → refine the plan | | Consistently bad results | The problem is the plan or AGENTS.md, not the model |

Lost a plan? Sessions are stored in ~/.claude/projects/ — use /resume to open session picker.

| |
→ ai-agents/claude-code-workflows
quiz intermediate

Which Side Does Context Mode Compress?

The five compression layers each target a different side:

| Strategy | Side | Saves | |----------|------|-------| | Context Mode | Input (PreToolUse hook) | 98–100% tool data | | Caveman | Output | 22–87% responses | | caveman-compress | Input (system prompt) | ~45% CLAUDE.md | | Tool search | Overhead | ~90% tool definitions | | Context compaction | History | 40–60% old turns |

A Playwright snapshot enters context as 299 B instead of 56 KB — a 99% reduction before the model ever sees it.

Key insight: Every other strategy targets what the model *says* or the *overhead* it carries. Context Mode is unique: it targets what *enters* the model — the raw tool data nobody thought to compress.

|

Context Mode achieves ~98% token reduction via which mechanism?

→ ai-agents/context-mode
insight beginner

Why a wiki is a network, not a list

A flat list of articles is a bookmark manager with extra steps. The value of a compiled wiki comes from cross-references: "AI reliability" links to "knowledge compilation" which links to "design taste" which links back to "generative UI." Each new page doesn't add one node — it adds N connections to existing nodes. The graph gets denser, and denser means more valuable.

Key insight: Metcalfe's Law applies to knowledge graphs: network value grows with connections, not node count. A wiki with 50 well-linked pages is exponentially more useful than 200 isolated summaries.

—
→ knowledge-management/llm-wiki-pattern
insight beginner

Taste is the willingness to say 'no, not that'

Pablo Stanley (2026): taste is not aesthetics or preference — it's intentionality. "The act of choosing. Looking at something and going 'no, not that' over and over until what's left actually means something."

Without this, AI generates technically correct but soulless interfaces — Inter font, heavy drop shadows, symmetric layouts, rounded pills everywhere. The absence of opinionated decisions is the telltale sign, not any single flaw.

Key insight: Taste isn't picking between three AI-generated options and choosing the one that looks like a competitor. That *is* its opposite. Taste requires the willingness to reject what the model defaults to.

What LLMs default to without correction: Inter/Roboto, heavy drop shadows, symmetric layouts, no motion, pill shapes everywhere. What taste produces: warmth, boldness, weird decisions that only make sense because someone with a specific life made them. Human fingerprints.

| |
→ ai-design/taste-intentionality
insight beginner

llamafile — An LLM in a Single Executable

llamafile bundles an entire LLM (weights + runtime) into a single executable file. Download it, run it — no CUDA install, no Python environment, no Docker, no cloud API.

encoderfile does the same for transformer encoders (embedding models): critical for local RAG pipelines where you can't or won't send data to cloud providers.

# That's the entire setup — download and run
./mistral-7b-instruct.llamafile


Together they make local AI accessible to teams with: - Data residency requirements (no cloud) - Air-gapped environments - Cost sensitivity (no API spend) - Offline or intermittent connectivity

Key insight: The Mozilla.ai philosophy applied to distribution: just as any-llm separates you from provider lock-in at the API level, llamafile separates you from provider lock-in at the infrastructure level. You own the weights, you own the runtime.

Compare to the cloud default: any API call means your data leaves your network. llamafile makes the alternative just as easy as curl.

| |
→ ai-agents/mozilla-ai
quiz intermediate

SWE-bench: From 1.96% to 87.6% — What Changed?

| Benchmark | Best AI | Human | |-----------|---------|-------| | SWE-bench Verified | 87.6% (Claude Opus 4.7) | N/A (expert task) | | WebArena | 14% (GPT-4, 2023 baseline) | 78% | | GAIA | 15% (GPT-4 + plugins) | 92% | | TheAgentCompany | 30% (best agent) | N/A |

Key insight: SWE-bench trajectory is encouraging — from 1.96% to 87.6% in 3 years — but the gap on open-ended, long-horizon tasks (WebArena, GAIA) remains 3–6×. Architecture and task scoping matter more than raw model improvement for closing that gap.

| |

SWE-bench Verified measures real GitHub issue resolution. Claude improved from ~1.96% (2023) to 87.6% (April 2026). Why doesn't this contradict the compounding error ceiling for agent reliability?

→ ai-agents/compounding-errors
code intermediate

Think in Code: 1 Script vs 47 File Reads

Context Mode's "Think in Code" paradigm: write a script that processes data in a sandbox instead of reading data into context. Only console.log output enters the context window.

// ❌ Before: 47 × Read() calls = 700 KB in context
// ✅ After: 1 × ctx_execute() = 3.6 KB in context

ctx_execute("javascript", 
  const files = fs.readdirSync('src').filter(f => f.endsWith('.ts'));
  files.forEach(f => {
    const lines = fs.readFileSync('src/' + f, 'utf8').split('\n').length;
    console.log(f + ': ' + lines + ' lines');
  });
);
// Output: auth.ts: 47 lines, utils.ts: 82 lines, ...


Key insight: The LLM writes the analysis program; the sandbox executes it. One script replaces ten tool calls and saves 100× context — same answer, without flooding the window.

How much context does one ctx_execute() script save compared to reading 47 TypeScript files with individual Read() calls?

→ ai-agents/context-mode
quiz beginner

What Is the Role of raw/ in the Wiki Ingest Pipeline?

The ingest pipeline has two strictly separated layers:

1. raw/ — immutable archived sources. One file per URL, with frontmatter metadata. Never modified or deleted. 2. wiki/ — compiled knowledge. Synthesized from many sources. Updated, merged, and enriched over time.

A source fans out to 2-5 wiki chapters. A wiki chapter draws from 2+ sources. The 1:1 mapping (one source → one wiki page) is explicitly forbidden — that's just a bookmark with extra steps.

Key insight: The raw→wiki split enforces separation of evidence from synthesis. You can always re-derive the wiki from raw sources. You cannot reconstruct raw sources from the wiki.

|

Why are raw/ source documents NEVER deleted, even when wiki pages are removed?

→ knowledge-management/wiki-ingest-pipeline
insight intermediate

Managed Agents: What 'Outcomes' enables that Events can't

Claude Managed Agents ships three research preview features that change what autonomous sessions can do:

| Feature | What it does | Why it matters | |---|---|---| | Outcomes | Define success criteria per session | Agent self-evaluates — human review becomes exception-based, not default | | Multi-agent | Orchestrate collaborating agents natively | Parallel specialists without writing orchestration code | | Memory | Persistent memory across sessions | Agents accumulate domain knowledge; no re-derivation overhead |

Events tell you what the agent *did*. Outcomes tell you whether it *succeeded* — and the agent knows before you check.

Key insight: Without Outcomes every managed agent session needs a human to inspect the result. With Outcomes the agent surfaces "success" or "failed: criterion X not met" — enabling pipelines where autonomous runs are the norm and human review is the exception.

→ ai-agents/claude-managed-agents
quiz intermediate

What is the correct order of Matt Pocock's 5-step planning chain?

The planning chain prevents the four agent failure modes: misalignment (grill-me), verbosity/fuzzy language (domain-model), broken code (tdd), and architectural entropy (to-prd + to-issues slicing).

| Step | Skill | Output | |------|-------|--------| | 1 | /grill-me | Resolved ambiguities | | 2 | /domain-model | CONTEXT.md updated, ADRs written | | 3 | /to-prd | Structured PRD with user stories | | 4 | /to-issues | Independent, agent-ready GitHub issues | | 5 | /tdd | Working code through public interfaces |

Key insight: The shared language from /domain-model is the highest-leverage step — variables, functions, and files named with a shared vocabulary mean the agent spends fewer tokens on disambiguation in every subsequent step.

· ·

What is the correct order of Matt Pocock's planning chain for building agent-ready features?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-skills
insight beginner

LLM evals measure rates, not pass/fail

Deterministic code gives one correct output. LLMs give a *distribution* of outputs — so the right metric is 87% of outputs meet quality bar, not "this output is correct."

Key insight: "Someone reported a toxic output once, but I can't reproduce it" is the classic eval gap. Without a systematic adversarial test set, you can't tell if a safety issue is a fluke or a pattern.

Three mental shifts from traditional testing: - No single correct answer — define a quality bar, not a fixed expected string - Rates, not pass/fail — aggregate scores across many test cases - Aggregate signals — 5-sample gut-check ≠ eval; you need enough cases to distinguish trend from noise

· ·
→ ai-agents/ai-evals-llm-testing
quiz intermediate

When does a screen reader need an aria-live region?

Dynamic content that appears near but *outside* the user's current focus is invisible to screen readers by default.

// ❌ Error renders but screen reader is silent — focus is still on the input
{error && 
{error}
}

// ✅ aria-live announces the change without moving focus


  {error}



// For urgent errors (security, auth failures):

{criticalError}


Key insight: polite queues the announcement until the user finishes speaking; assertive interrupts immediately. Use polite for form feedback, assertive for true emergencies only.

|

Which React pattern requires an explicit aria-live region to reach screen reader users?

→ frontend/react-accessibility
quiz intermediate

A2A Agent Cards: Dynamic Discovery Without Hardcoded Routing

{
  "name": "CandidateSourcingAgent",
  "capabilities": ["candidate-search", "skill-matching"],
  "authentication": { "schemes": ["oauth2"] },
  "endpoint": "https://recruiting.example.com/a2a"
}


A2A task lifecycle: created → in_progress → completed | failed. Supports long-running tasks (hours to days) with human-in-the-loop updates.

Key insight: A2A's Agent Cards are to agents what DNS is to websites — a discovery mechanism that decouples the caller from needing to know where the specialist lives.

50+ enterprise partners (Salesforce, SAP, ServiceNow, Workday) endorsing the same protocol signals a genuine platform bet.

| |

In the A2A protocol, how does an orchestrating agent find the right specialist agent to delegate a task to?

→ ai-agents/agent-interoperability
insight intermediate

Kapa.ai MCP server: docs-as-answers → docs-as-agent-context

Kapa's one-click MCP server shifts its role from customer-facing Q&A to internal agent infrastructure. Instead of users asking the docs chatbot, agents query the same index — Claude Code, Cursor, and team-built agents all gain documentation context via search_docs().

search_docs("how does event deduplication work")
search_docs("SDK migration from v1 to v2")
search_docs("troubleshoot auth failures")


Key insight: One Kapa deployment serves two masters: the customer support widget on the docs site, and every coding agent in the developer's IDE. Same index, two access surfaces.

→ ai-agents/kapa-ai
insight beginner

97% of MCP Tools Have Description Smells

Across 856 MCP tools from 103 servers (Hasan et al., arXiv 2602.14878), 97.1% have at least one description smell. In head-to-head competition, the server with standard-compliant descriptions was chosen 260% more often (72% vs 20% baseline, p<0.01). Same code — better description wins.

The highest-ROI fix: add one input example per tool description. Anthropic's data shows this single change lifts complex parameter accuracy from 72% → 90%.

Before: "Get weather for a location"
After:  "Get weather for a location. Example: { location: 'San Francisco, CA' }"


Key insight: Discoverability is a description problem. One sentence with an example is the cheapest, highest-impact MCP improvement available.

→ ai-agents/building-mcp-servers
quiz advanced

How does n-dx's hierarchical PRD reduce compounding error risk?

The compounding error formula p^n has two levers: improve p (accuracy per step) or reduce n (steps in the chain). Most reliability work focuses on p. n-dx attacks n directly:

Open-ended prompt: "Build the whole feature"
  → steps: analyze → design → implement × 10 files → test × 10 → integrate → ...
  → n ≈ 50+, errors compound freely across the entire task

n-dx PRD task: "Implement login endpoint (epic: Auth, feature: JWT, task: POST /login)"
  → n ≈ 5–10 per cycle
  → Re-analysis at cycle end catches drift before next dependency runs



graph LR
    P["PRD task\n(scoped n ≈ 5)"]
    E["execute\nhench loop"]
    A["re-analyze\nquality gate"]
    N["next task\n(new n ≈ 5)"]

    P --> E --> A -->|pass| N
    A -->|drift detected| E

    style A fill:#166534,color:#fff


Key insight: Structured scope is the simplest lever against compounding failure — simpler than better models, simpler than more retries. n-dx makes it mechanical: each task is a bounded unit, and each cycle ends with a re-analysis gate that stops errors before they propagate.

· ·

n-dx structures work as a hierarchical PRD (epics → features → tasks). How does this directly reduce compounding error risk compared to an open-ended prompt?

→ ai-agents/n-dx
quiz intermediate

Lost in the Middle: Where Does the Model Focus in Long Contexts?

Context window size ≠ reliable recall across that window. Attention weight follows a U-curve:

Start (system prompt) ████████████ HIGH
Early messages        ████████     MEDIUM
Middle history        ███          LOW  ← information gets lost here
Late messages         ██████       MEDIUM-HIGH
End (most recent)     ████████████ HIGH


> Key insight (Matt Pocock): "You shouldn't just think 'wow, this context window limit's huge.' That model will still probably have lost-in-the-middle issues. You'll definitely get better results from using fewer tokens."

Context compaction improves accuracy, not just cost.

| |

In a long context window (e.g., 1M tokens), where does a language model tend to have the LOWEST attention weight — missing information more often?

→ ai-agents/token-fundamentals
code advanced

The 3-line lock-file fix that stops PostToolUse cascade

Add this to ~/.claude/settings.json (or .claude/settings.json) to stop cascade permanently:

{
  "hooks": {
    "PostToolUse": [{
      "matcher": "Edit|Write|MultiEdit",
      "hooks": [{
        "type": "command",
        "command": "[ -f .claude/.hook-lock ] && exit 0; touch .claude/.hook-lock; npx prettier --write \"$CLAUDE_TOOL_INPUT_FILE_PATH\" 2>/dev/null; rm -f .claude/.hook-lock; exit 0"
      }]
    }]
  }
}


How the three parts work: 1. [ -f .claude/.hook-lock ] && exit 0 — any nested PostToolUse sees the lock and dies immediately 2. touch .claude/.hook-lock → format → rm -f .claude/.hook-lock — the lock wraps the formatter 3. exit 0 always — formatter errors are information, not a signal to retry

Key insight: PostToolUse hooks are synchronous within a cascade — checking a file on disk is the only side-channel available. The lock-file is a mutex for the hook system. One developer went from hitting Claude Max limits by Wednesday to hitting them Sunday evening — three extra days of capacity from three lines.

Choose ONE formatter: Prettier OR Biome OR ESLint --fix. Never multiple.

| |

What happens to a nested PostToolUse event that fires while the lock file exists?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-hook-conflicts
quiz beginner

Claude Routines: Two Trigger Types

Both trigger types run the same underlying session: full Claude Code agent with persistent context, file read/write, web fetch, git operations. Routines are the trigger layer on top of Claude Managed Agents.

Schedule/Webhook → Claude Code session → CLAUDE.md + tools → Output to git/Slack/etc.


| |

Claude Routines supports two trigger types for agent sessions. Which real-world use case maps correctly to each trigger?

→ ai-agents/claude-routines
quiz intermediate

Which leg of the lethal trifecta is most practical to break?

When all three conditions coexist — private data, attacker-controlled text (prompt injection vector), and outbound network — an agent can be hijacked by a comment in a README or a string in an API response to exfiltrate credentials. No malware required.

graph TD
    A["Private Data"] --> T{"Lethal Trifecta"}
    B["Untrusted Content"] --> T
    C["Network Access"] --> T
    T -->|"remove network"| S["✅ Attack neutralized"]
    style T fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style S fill:#166534,color:#fff


Key insight: "Anyone who gets text into your LLM has full control over what tools it runs next." — Simon Willison, Claude Code Anonymous meetup (Oct 2025)

Simon Willison's lethal trifecta requires all three conditions to enable trivial credential theft: private data access, untrusted content exposure, and external communication. Which leg is most practical to remove in production?

→ ai-agents/agent-security-sandboxing
insight beginner

The Core Agent Loop Is Simpler Than You Think

"The core of tools like Claude Code, Cursor, and Warp isn't magic. It's about 200 lines of straightforward Python." — Mihail Eric

The core loop has three parts: 1. Messages array — conversation history passed to the LLM every call 2. API call with tools — model decides whether to call a tool or end the turn 3. Tool execution — results appended to messages, loop repeats

while (true) {
  const response = await client.messages.create({ messages, tools });
  messages.push({ role: "assistant", content: response.content });
  if (response.stop_reason === "end_turn") { printOutput(); break; }
  // Execute tool calls, append results, loop again
  const results = executeAllTools(response.content);
  messages.push({ role: "user", content: results });
}


What makes Claude Code a 50k-line product instead of 200 lines: sandboxing, streaming, permissions, context compaction, prompt caching, session persistence, multi-agent, and a carefully engineered system prompt.

Key insight: The loop is the same whether you're building a toy agent in an afternoon or shipping a production tool. The difference is entirely in the infrastructure around the loop, the system prompt quality, and the tool design.

| |
→ ai-agents/claude-code-architecture
insight beginner

The Hill Chart — Progress Without 'Are You Done?'

Shape Up tracks progress with a Hill Chart — a visual metaphor that eliminates the awkward "how far along are you?" question.

flowchart LR
    A["🟡 Uphill\nFiguring out\nStill solving unknowns"] -->|unknowns resolved| P["🏔️ Peak\nFull understanding"]
    P -->|clear path| C["🟢 Downhill\nExecuting\nKnown path, just doing"]

    style A fill:#d97706,color:#fff
    style P fill:#6b7280,color:#fff
    style C fill:#166534,color:#fff


Uphill = uncertainty. The team is still figuring out *how* to solve the problem. Unknowns are blocking progress. Uphill work is inherently unpredictable.

Downhill = execution. The solution is understood. The team is implementing a known path. Downhill work is estimable — you can see the finish line.

Every scope (vertical slice of work) lives somewhere on the hill. A scope stuck uphill too long is a warning sign — an undiscovered rabbit hole, a missing decision, a technical unknown that needs to be either resolved or cut.

Key insight: "Scope hammering — cutting scope isn't lowering quality." When a cycle runs short, the discipline is to identify what's essential vs. nice-to-have, and cut the nice-to-have. Status should be *visible*, not *extracted*.

Compare to baseline, not to plan. Ship when users are better off than today — not when you've hit the imagined ideal.

| |
→ productivity/shape-up
connection beginner

AlphaFold: The 50-Year Problem and Long-Horizon Conviction

Demis Hassabis (CEO, Google DeepMind) solved protein structure prediction — a problem that had resisted 50+ years of computational biology effort. AlphaFold 2 (2020) matched experimental accuracy and the resulting database now covers ~200 million proteins — virtually every known protein.

The connection to strategic optimism: - The problem was widely considered intractable — many researchers had given up - AlphaFold 1 was good but not transformative — continued investment required conviction - Open-sourcing the results maximized impact over competitive advantage - The payoff timeline: 50+ years of prior failure → breakthrough in ~5 years of focused effort

This is what "functional delusion" looks like at scale: committing to a long-horizon problem, not because success is guaranteed, but because the conviction that it *can* be solved is what sustains the work.

Key insight: "Solving the most important unsolved problem in science" wasn't a boast — it was a north star. Strategic optimism applied to long-horizon research problems doesn't ignore failure; it routes around it. AlphaFold is the empirical case that the pattern works.

|
→ productivity/self-belief-strategic-optimism
insight beginner

The $60 vs $5,000 WordPress theme debate misses the total cost of ownership

Pre-made WordPress themes cost $50–200 upfront. Custom themes cost more. But the comparison changes dramatically over 3–5 years of active use.

| Consideration | Pre-made theme | Custom theme | |---------------|---------------|-------------| | Performance | 15–30 unused scripts load on every page | Only required code ships | | Security risk | Popular themes = prime hacker targets; attack surface grows with every plugin | Unique codebase, unknown to bots; minimal plugins | | Scalability | Rigid architecture hits a dead-end | Built for planned growth | | Maintenance | Constant plugin conflicts, breaking updates | Core functionality built-in, fewer conflicts |

Key insight: Pre-made themes accumulate cost through plugins, troubleshooting hours, and eventual rebuilds. Custom themes invest upfront to avoid these recurring costs. 53% of mobile visitors bounce after 3 seconds — a bloated pre-made theme is often the culprit.

Pre-made is fine when: You need something live fast, the site is low-stakes or temporary, or standard functionality covers your needs without customization.

Classic failure symptoms at scale (from ): Rigidity (can't change colors without breaking layouts), Fragility (plugin updates break styling), Opacity (page-builder HTML is unreadable).

| |
→ web/wordpress-custom-themes
quiz intermediate

Why Does Knowledge Compilation Beat RAG for Personal Wikis?

Academic foundations: - Lewis et al. 2020 — original RAG paper: good for fact retrieval, struggles with reasoning chains - Edge et al. 2024 (GraphRAG) — Microsoft's solution: build knowledge graphs first, then retrieve. Halfway to compilation. - Vannevar Bush's Memex (1945) — the original vision: trails of associated ideas, not flat retrieval

The wiki pattern goes further than GraphRAG: instead of building a graph at ingest time, it builds *synthesized chapters* — human-readable, cross-linked, and enriched with code examples.

Key insight: GraphRAG is evidence that even Microsoft knows pure retrieval is insufficient. The wiki pattern takes this further: compile into prose, not just graphs.

| |

What is the core limitation that knowledge compilation addresses in RAG systems?

→ knowledge-management/wiki-research-foundations
insight intermediate

Advisor Tool: Pay Mostly Sonnet Rates, Get Near-Opus Quality

Anthropic's Advisor Tool (beta) changes the cost equation for agent intelligence. A Sonnet executor + Opus advisor at medium effort costs approximately the same as Sonnet at default effort — while producing output quality close to Opus-solo.

The key: the advisor doesn't write output. The executor does. The advisor only plans and course-corrects in ~400–700 tokens per call. Most tokens flow through the cheaper model.

| Configuration | Quality | Cost | |---|---|---| | Opus alone | ★★★ | ★★★ expensive | | Sonnet alone | ★★ | ★ cheap | | Sonnet executor + Opus advisor | ★★★ near-Opus | ★★ moderate |

Key insight: Add "The advisor should respond in under 100 words and use enumerated steps" to your system prompt → 35–45% advisor token reduction with no quality loss.

Enable caching at 3+ advisor calls per session: caching: { type: "ephemeral", ttl: "5m" } — the advisor sees the same transcript context and gets a cache hit.

| |
→ ai-agents/advisor-tool
quiz beginner

Claude.ai vs. Claude Code — Same Model, Different Behavior?

Matt Pocock's vocabulary introduces harness as the single most important term: it's everything that turns a raw model into a working agent. Two products can share an identical model and feel completely different — because the harness shapes every interaction.

Claude.ai ──┐
             ├── same model (e.g. claude-sonnet-4-6)
Claude Code ─┘

Different behaviors → different HARNESSES, not different models.


Key insight: When debugging "why the model seems off today," check the harness first — system prompt changes, context management policy, permission mode — before assuming the model itself changed.

Claude.ai and Claude Code use the same underlying model but behave very differently. What explains this?

→ ai-agents/dictionary-of-ai-coding
quiz intermediate

Coding Agent RAG: What Level Do Most Projects Need?

| Level | Name | When to use | |-------|------|-------------| | 1 | Auto-memory (CLAUDE.md) | Always — the foundation | | 2 | Project context (.md files) | When CLAUDE.md gets unwieldy | | 3 | MCP retrieval | Context doesn't fit in files | | 4 | Autoresearch (LLM wiki) | Need curated, compiled knowledge | | 5 | Semantic retrieval | Keyword search misses relevant docs | | 6 | LightRAG | Large codebases, complex interdependencies | | 7 | Agentic graph RAG | Very large knowledge bases |

Key insight: Start at Level 1. Move up only when you hit real friction. The right level is the one that removes a specific friction point — not the most sophisticated one available.

| |

The 7-level RAG spectrum for coding agents ranges from CLAUDE.md (Level 1) to full agentic graph RAG (Level 7). For most projects, what is the practical ceiling?

→ ai-agents/building-coding-agents
quiz intermediate

auth.md: agent-verified vs user-claimed — what's the difference?

flowchart LR
    A["AI Agent"] -->|"1. fetch"| F["app.com/auth.md"]
    F -->|"2. pick flow"| A
    A -->|"3a. agent-verified:\nidentity assertion"| AP["Your App"]
    A -->|"3b. user-claimed:\ntrigger OTP → human confirms"| AP
    AP -->|"4. issue scoped API key"| A


| Flow | Interaction required | When to use | |------|---------------------|-------------| | Agent Verified | None — fully autonomous | Agent has identity assertion from its platform | | User Claimed | One OTP confirmation | Agent-initiated but human must confirm ownership |

Key insight: auth.md solves the "wall" agents hit at account creation steps. An agent scaffolding an integration can now complete registration without the user leaving their coding session.

| |

An AI agent needs to register a user in a third-party app autonomously, without any human interaction. Which auth.md flow makes this possible, and why?

→ ai-agents/auth-md
insight intermediate

Cohere's singular bet: your data never leaves your infrastructure

Most LLM providers are cloud-only. Cohere bets the opposite: every model — generation, embeddings, reranking, and speech-to-text — can run inside the customer's own infrastructure.

| Deployment | Data location | Best for | |-----------|--------------|----------| | VPC | Customer's cloud account | Cloud-first enterprises needing data isolation | | On-premises | Customer's own hardware | Air-gapped: finance, defense, health | | Model Vault | Cohere-managed dedicated hardware | Dedicated infra without self-managing | | Cloud API | Cohere-hosted | Developers, prototyping |

Key insight: Regulated industries (banks, hospitals, defense contractors) can run the full Embed → Rerank → Command pipeline inside their security perimeter. No document content routes through a third-party cloud at any stage.

| |
→ ai-agents/cohere
insight intermediate

Why Svelte Can Target Native Apps (React Can't As Easily)

React and Svelte both support multi-platform rendering, but for fundamentally different reasons:

React is a runtime library. It achieves multi-platform (React Native) by splitting its reconciler from its renderer — the same runtime runs in both environments, just with different renderers. Bundle size travels with every target.

Svelte is a compiler. It generates platform-specific code at build time with near-zero runtime. Custom renderers work by splitting the compilation output into an intermediate representation (fragment tree) that any target renderer can consume.

| Aspect | React Native | Svelte Custom Renderers | |--------|-------------|------------------------| | Approach | Runtime split | Compiler IR | | Bundle overhead | ~45KB React runtime | Near zero | | Status | Production (2015) | POC/research (2025–26) | | Reactivity model | VDOM diff at runtime | Compiled state machines |

Key insight: Svelte's compiler foundation isn't just a performance win for the web — it's an architectural advantage for multi-platform. When you compile to a platform-specific IR instead of carrying a runtime, you get smaller bundles and faster startup everywhere, not just in the browser.

|
→ frontend/svelte-universal-rendering
code intermediate

Build-once-run-forever: when is an LLM the tool-builder, not the tool?

// ❌ LLM-each-time: ~$730/year (8k tokens × $0.25/M × 365 runs)
const html = await fetch(url).then(r => r.text());
const res = await client.messages.create({
  model: "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_tokens: 64,
  messages: [{ role: "user", content: What is the price on this page?\n\n${html} }],
});

// ✅ Build once, run forever: LLM writes this extractor ONCE, never called again
import { JSDOM } from "jsdom";
function extractPrice(html: string, selector = "span.price"): string | null {
  const dom = new JSDOM(html);
  return dom.window.document.querySelector(selector)?.textContent?.trim() ?? null;
}
const price = extractPrice(await fetch(url).then(r => r.text()));
// Token cost: $0/year — CSS selector + JSDOM = deterministic, no LLM in the loop
// Output: "$29.99"


Key insight: For predictable, repeatable workflows — scrapers, classifiers, formatters — use the LLM to write the code once and exit the loop. Running deterministic code costs nothing; running the LLM costs tokens every time.

· ·

Which pattern costs ~$730/year vs $0/year for 365 daily price extractions from the same website?

→ ai-agents/code-is-cheap
insight intermediate

The meta-pattern across all AI reliability architectures

Every reliable AI system moves work out of the unreliable real-time LLM call and into reliable pre-computed infrastructure.

| Instead of... | Do this... | |---|---| | RAG (re-derive at query time) | Knowledge compilation (pre-synthesize) | | Load all tools statically | Search for 3-5 relevant tools dynamically | | Stateless sessions | Persistent memory | | DIY agent harness | Managed infrastructure |

The four patterns — knowledge compilation, tool search, persistent memory, managed infrastructure — all express the same insight from different angles.

Key insight: The ceiling on AI reliability isn't set by model accuracy. It's set by how much work you've moved out of the real-time call and into pre-computed, verifiable infrastructure.

| |
→ ai-agents/ai-agent-reliability
insight intermediate

Why run three models in parallel for code review?

Nolan Lawson (Socket, May 2026) found that running Claude (Opus 4.7, xhigh thinking), Codex (GPT 5.5), and Cursor Bugbot in parallel on the same PR — then collating findings — produces near-zero false positive rates. Models share different training blind spots, so a bug one hallucinates, another refutes.

graph TD
    PR["Pull Request"] --> A["Claude Opus"] & B["Codex"] & C["Cursor Bugbot"]
    A & B & C --> S["Synthesizer\n(withholds its own research\nuntil all agents report)"]
    S --> R["Final Report: critical · high · medium · low"]
    style PR fill:#374151,color:#d1d5db
    style S fill:#d97706,color:#fff


The synthesizer withholds its own research until all sub-agents report — this prevents anchoring on the first result. After collation: fix all criticals and highs, skip mediums where juice isn't worth the squeeze, abandon the PR entirely if too many criticals (the whole approach is wrong).

Key insight: "An LLM's output is just the first draft. The real work starts with the code review." — Nolan Lawson (May 2026). Velocity may not increase — you'll often surface pre-existing bugs — but codebase health compounds.

→ ai-agents/agent-tdd-verification
quiz beginner

Local AI vs Cloud: When Does Local Win?

The local-vs-cloud decision comes down to a few key signals:
