| Use local when | Use cloud when | |---------------|----------------| | Data can't leave the machine | Non-sensitive workloads | | High volume + simple tasks | Bursty, low-volume | | Custom fine-tuning needed | General frontier capability | | Offline or air-gapped | Always-connected ok |

graph TD
    Q1{Privacy-sensitive?} -->|Yes| Q2{Simple or complex task?}
    Q1 -->|No| CLOUD["☁️ Cloud — use frontier model"]
    Q2 -->|Simple/medium| LOCAL["🖥️ Gemma 4 E4B\nApache 2.0 · 3–6 GB VRAM"]
    Q2 -->|Very complex| HYBRID["⚡ Manifest routing\nLocal simple + cloud complex"]


Key insight: The cloud-vs-local decision isn't about capability — Gemma 4 E4B competes with cloud AI on many tasks. It's about privacy constraints, cost at scale, and offline requirements.

| |

You need to process sensitive medical records and extract structured data. Token volume is high but requests are simple extractions. What's the best local AI strategy?

→ ai-agents/local-ai-landscape
code intermediate

OKLCH: Perceptually Uniform Color

/* HSL: yellow and blue at same "lightness" look wildly different */
.yellow { color: hsl(60, 100%, 50%); }  /* looks bright */
.blue   { color: hsl(240, 100%, 50%); } /* looks dark */

/* OKLCH: same lightness actually looks the same */
.yellow { color: oklch(0.80 0.18 95); }
.blue   { color: oklch(0.80 0.18 260); }
/* Both genuinely appear same brightness */


For AI-generated UI, OKLCH is essential. When agents pick colors programmatically, perceptual uniformity prevents the "why does this palette look unbalanced?" problem that plagues HSL-based generation.

|

Why is OKLCH superior to HSL for generating color palettes?

→ ai-design/design-code-examples
quiz beginner

What does Unsloth's '2× speed, 70% less VRAM' apply to?

Unsloth Studio trains 500+ models at 2× speed with 70% less VRAM than standard approaches — without accuracy loss — specifically on NVIDIA GPUs.

curl -fsSL https://unsloth.ai/install.sh | sh  # one-line install
unsloth studio -H 0.0.0.0 -p 8888              # serve on LAN


After training, Unsloth exposes an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint, letting local Qwen or Gemma models replace Claude inside Claude Code — 100% offline, no data leaves the machine.

Key insight: The no-accuracy-loss claim means you're not trading quality for VRAM savings — it's a pure efficiency win from kernel-level optimizations.

Unsloth Studio's '2× speed with 70% less VRAM (no accuracy loss)' claim applies specifically to:

→ ai-agents/unsloth-studio
insight intermediate

Why LLM-generated code has no complexity ceiling

Human coders have a built-in brake: cognitive overhead. When complexity grows, confusion slows output — the developer stops and refactors. LLMs have no such brake.

| Attribute | Human coder | LLM | |---|---|---| | Fear of complexity | Yes — cognitive load acts as a natural brake | None — generates endlessly | | Self-limiting? | Yes — confusion slows output, signals a stop | No — prolific by design | | Complexity growth | Sub-linear (slows as things get hairy) | Geometric — no ceiling | | Result at scale | Manageable, author-legible | Unmodifiable steady state |

Key insight: Code has gotten much cheaper to generate. The bottleneck moved from production to comprehension. A developer who doesn't apply the Subtractive Engineer's "no, not that" judgment ends up with a codebase nobody — including the LLM — can navigate.

The corollary: spend LLM tokens once to build a deterministic tool. Run it forever with zero ongoing cost and zero ongoing complexity accumulation.

·
→ ai-agents/code-is-cheap
insight intermediate

`disable-model-invocation: true` — Skills That Never Auto-Fire

Skill files in .claude/commands/ are loaded into the session context. Without disable-model-invocation: true, Claude can invoke a skill automatically mid-task — not just when you call it explicitly.

---
allowed-tools: Read, Bash
disable-model-invocation: true
description: Tighten Python type annotations
---


> Key insight (honnibal): All 7 skills in honnibal/claude-skills ship with this flag. A /pre-mortem skill generating a full PRE-MORTEM.md incident report shouldn't fire mid-refactor — only when you explicitly invoke it. You control when the skill fires; the model never invokes it on your behalf.

Skills with large outputs — mutation test suites, stub packages, full incident reports — are the ones that matter most. Unintended invocations pollute the context window with output you didn't ask for and can't undo without starting a new session.

| |
→ ai-agents/claude-code-engineering-skills
insight advanced

Cavekit Speculative Review: Zero Gate Latency via Background Adversarial Checks

Cavekit runs three tiers of cross-model review (Codex auditing Claude's output), but the most sophisticated is speculative review: while Tier 2 builds, Codex reviews Tier 1 in the background simultaneously. When Tier 2 finishes, the Tier 1 review is already done — zero added latency.

| Review type | When | What it catches | |-------------|------|-----------------| | Design Challenge | Before any building | Decomposition flaws, missing requirements | | Tier Gates | Between tiers | P0/P1 findings block advancement | | Speculative Review | Background (overlaps current tier) | Prior-tier issues found before they cascade forward |

sequenceDiagram
    participant T1 as Tier 1 Build
    participant T2 as Tier 2 Build
    participant Codex as Codex Review

    T1->>T2: Tier 1 complete → start Tier 2
    T1-->>Codex: Speculative: review Tier 1 in background
    T2->>T2: Building...
    Codex->>Codex: Reviewing Tier 1...
    Note over T2,Codex: Both run in parallel
    T2-->>T2: Tier 2 done
    Codex-->>T2: Tier 1 review ready — P0/P1 findings surface immediately


Key insight: Sequential review adds wall-clock time to every tier gate. Speculative review eliminates the wait entirely by overlapping Codex's check of the previous tier with the next tier's build. Gracefully degrades if Codex is unavailable — the pipeline continues with internal review only.

| |
→ ai-agents/cavekit
insight beginner

Goal-Driven Execution: Give Success Criteria, Not Steps

Karpathy's fourth principle flips the default prompting model: don't tell the AI what to do — give it success criteria and watch it loop until done.

> "LLMs are exceptionally good at looping until they meet specific goals. Don't tell it what to do. Give it success criteria and watch it go." > — Andrej Karpathy

Before (step-by-step instructions): 
1. Open auth/validator.ts
2. Find the timestamp comparison on line 47
3. Change > to >=
4. Run the tests


After (success criteria): 
The auth module has a timing-attack vulnerability — fix it.
All existing tests must pass. No new dependencies.


The agentic loop handles the steps. You review the outcome.

Key insight: Step-by-step instructions constrain the solution space before Claude has explored it. Success criteria give Claude the goal and let the loop figure out the path — which is often shorter than the path you'd prescribe.

Three observable changes after adopting all four principles: diffs are clean, clarifying questions come first, and code is simpler on the first pass.

| |
→ ai-agents/claude-code-workflows
code beginner

Concentric border-radius: what's the formula?

:root { --r: 8px; --p: 12px; }
.card       { border-radius: calc(var(--r) + var(--p)); /* 20px */ padding: var(--p); }
.card-inner { border-radius: var(--r); /* 8px — curves run parallel */ }


Without this formula, nested corners look "pinched" — one of the telltale signs of AI-generated UI.

Key insight: This single rule separates amateur from polished. It's invisible when done right, jarring when done wrong.

| |

What is the correct formula for concentric border-radius (nested elements)?

→ ai-design/design-code-examples
quiz intermediate

Which eval use case catches regressions when switching models?

The three core eval use cases each address a different launch risk:

| Use case | What you measure | When to run | |---|---|---| | Production readiness | Output quality consistency across diverse inputs | Before any launch | | Model upgrade regression | Quality delta between model versions | Before swapping models | | Safety verification | Toxic / policy-violating output rate | Continuous + pre-launch |

· ·

You're switching from a managed LLM service to a self-hosted model to cut costs. Which core eval use case should you run first?

→ ai-agents/ai-evals-llm-testing
quiz intermediate

IntersectionObserver vs CSS view() — which tool for which job?

| Scenario | Use | |----------|-----| | Add/remove a CSS class on element enter | CSS view() | | Pure animation synced to element visibility | CSS view() | | Load data when element enters view | IntersectionObserver | | Fire analytics events | IntersectionObserver | | Lazy-load images | IntersectionObserver | | Infinite scroll trigger | IntersectionObserver |

// Infinite scroll sentinel — classic IntersectionObserver use case
const sentinel = new IntersectionObserver(([entry]) => {
  if (!entry.isIntersecting) return;
  loadNextPage();
}, { rootMargin: "300px" });

sentinel.observe(document.querySelector("#load-more"));


Key insight: CSS Scroll-driven Animations obsoletes IntersectionObserver for scroll-reveal effects. IntersectionObserver remains essential for any JavaScript side effect that must fire when visibility changes.

| |

You need to fire a network request and load more posts when the user scrolls near the bottom. Which tool is correct?

→ frontend/intersection-observer
quiz intermediate

Why does hybrid search beat pure vector search for RAG?

Hybrid search is the first lift to apply to any RAG pipeline. The standard RRF constant k=60 is robust across most corpora — change it only if you have a benchmark.

// Reciprocal Rank Fusion: fuse BM25 + dense ranked lists
function rrf(lists: string[][], k = 60) {
  const scores = new Map();
  for (const list of lists)
    list.forEach((id, rank) => scores.set(id, (scores.get(id) ?? 0) + 1 / (k + rank + 1)));
  return [...scores.entries()].map(([id, score]) => ({ id, score })).sort((a, b) => b.score - a.score);
}
// doc appearing in both BM25 and dense → amplified combined score


Key insight: "Most RAG bugs are search bugs." Plain cosine similarity is the floor, not the ceiling. Hybrid search + reranking + query transformation are the three primary levers for retrieval quality.

Lexical search (BM25) and dense semantic search have complementary failure modes. Which statement correctly describes the hybrid search advantage?

→ ai-agents/rag-retrieval-techniques
quiz beginner

WordPress Classic Theme vs Full Site Editing (FSE): Which approach uses theme.json?

// theme.json: single source of truth for FSE themes
{
  "version": 3,
  "settings": {
    "color": {
      "palette": [{ "slug": "primary", "color": "#0ea5e9", "name": "Primary" }]
    },
    "typography": {
      "fontSizes": [{ "slug": "lg", "size": "1.25rem", "name": "Large" }]
    }
  }
}
// → generates: var(--wp--preset--color--primary), var(--wp--preset--font-size--lg)



Classic theme (PHP)          Block theme (FSE)
├── functions.php            ├── theme.json         ← global config
├── index.php                ├── templates/         ← HTML templates
├── page.php                 ├── parts/             ← header, footer
└── single.php               └── custom blocks (PHP still needed for logic)


Key insight: FSE replaces the *template file* layer — the layout and visual assembly — but deep PHP knowledge is still required for complex, unique functionality. "WordPress is easy" creates false expectations: ease of setup ≠ ease of professional use.

| |

A WordPress developer wants to control global typography and colors from a single config file. Which theme approach enables this?

→ web/wordpress-custom-themes
quiz intermediate

Voting Pattern: Which Model for Candidates vs. Judge?

The Split-and-Merge voting variant generates N independent implementations of the same task, then uses a judge model to pick the best one.

// Haiku: N cheap candidates, all fire in parallel
const candidates = await Promise.all(
  Array.from({ length: 3 }, () =>
    client.messages.create({ model: "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_tokens: 512, /* ... */ })
  )
);

// Sonnet: one judge call — number only, no explanation
const res = await client.messages.create({
  model: "claude-sonnet-4-6",
  max_tokens: 64,
  messages: [{ role: "user", content: ${criterion}. Reply with just the number of the best option.\n\n${optionList} }],
});


Key insight: The judge prompt asks for a number only — not an explanation. Explanation bloat wastes tokens and does not improve selection quality.

|

In the Split-and-Merge voting variant, which model pairing minimizes cost without sacrificing selection quality?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-workflow-patterns
insight intermediate

Tracer Bullets: Validate Architecture Before You Build It

Matt Pocock's ralph/prompt.md — an AFK agent that works through a task queue unattended — prioritizes tasks in this order:

1. Critical bugfixes
2. Dev infrastructure (tests, types, dev scripts)
3. Tracer bullets — thin end-to-end slices of features
4. Polish and quick wins
5. Refactors


Tracer bullets (priority 3) are tiny end-to-end feature slices through all system layers. They validate the full architecture stack with minimal investment before committing to the full implementation.

Key insight: A tracer bullet is breadboarding applied to execution order. Build the thinnest possible path from input to output across every layer first. If the architecture is wrong, you discover it on 5% of the work, not 95%.

Before committing, the ralph agent always runs feedback loops: npm run test + npm run typecheck. Commit messages include decisions made, files changed, and blockers for the next iteration — giving the next session full context without conversational context.

This is why infrastructure (item 2) comes before feature work — broken tests mean the tracer bullet has no way to verify itself.

| |
→ ai-agents/claude-code-skills
insight intermediate

Subagent trees are exactly one level deep — and that's the point

A subagent is an agent spawned by another agent via a tool call. It runs in its own isolated context window and returns exactly one tool_result back to the parent. That result is all the parent ever sees — not the subagent's full session history, not its intermediate reasoning, not its tool call log.

This enforces a one-level-deep tree:

graph TD
    PA["🤖 Parent Agent\n(Session A, Context A)"]
    PA -->|"tool_use: spawn"| SA1["🤖 Subagent 1\nisolated context\n→ one tool_result"]
    PA -->|"tool_use: spawn"| SA2["🤖 Subagent 2\nisolated context\n→ one tool_result"]
    SA1 -.->|"❌ cannot spawn"| GC["grandchild"]
    SA2 -.->|"❌ cannot spawn"| GC2["grandchild"]

    style PA fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style SA1 fill:#0891b2,color:#fff
    style SA2 fill:#0891b2,color:#fff
    style GC fill:#374151,color:#d1d5db
    style GC2 fill:#374151,color:#d1d5db


Key insight: The parent's context window grows by exactly one tool result — not by the subagent's entire session history. That's the architecture win: subagents parallelise work and isolate context, but the parent stays lightweight because it only receives compressed results, not full transcripts.

Subagents cannot spawn further subagents — the tree is exactly one level deep. They exist to isolate context and parallelise work, not to compose hierarchies.

· ·
→ ai-agents/dictionary-of-ai-coding
connection beginner

Every Tech Wave Follows the Same Cycle

From the 1970s microprocessor to 1990s OOP to 2000s Agile to 2020s AI — every major tech disruption follows: Disruption → Chaos → Practitioners keep building → Good practices emerge → New equilibrium.

The "this time is different" illusion repeats too. OOP prompted abandoning structured programming. AI is prompting abandoning tests ("the model will handle it"). The pattern says: craft never becomes obsolete. It becomes more visible.

Key insight: Well-structured code (small functions, DRY, CI/CD) helps agents MORE than it helps humans. Messy codebases that humans navigate via tribal knowledge become blockers when agents try autonomous changes.

|
→ ai-agents/tech-disruption-cycles
quiz intermediate

Which memory architecture is most practical for production AI agents?

Without persistent memory, agents re-derive context on every interaction — users repeat themselves and the agent can't build on validated prior work. Four architectures address this:

| Architecture | Best for | |---|---| | File-based | Stable knowledge, version control, transparency (CLAUDE.md pattern) | | Database-backed | High-volume structured data, user preferences at scale | | Vector stores | Fuzzy semantic recall across large interaction histories | | Hybrid | Production: stable files + vector search via RRF |

graph TD
    QUERY["Agent query\n'What did user say about performance?'\n'What is MAX_RETRIES value?'"]
    VEC["Vector search\n(semantic intent)"]
    BM25["BM25 keyword\n(exact tokens)"]
    RRF["RRF fusion\n1/(K + rank)"]
    OUT["Top-K results\n(both failure modes covered)"]

    QUERY --> VEC & BM25
    VEC --> RRF
    BM25 --> RRF
    RRF --> OUT

    style RRF fill:#166534,color:#fff


Key insight: Each session should compound the last. Stateless agents re-derive everything — stateful agents build a validated baseline. The hybrid memory pattern ensures both semantic queries ("what does the user prefer?") and exact queries ("what's the value of MAX_RETRIES?") are handled without configuration.

An agent needs to track user preferences (stable facts) AND recall relevant past interactions fuzzy-matched by meaning. Which architecture fits best?

→ ai-agents/pattern-persistent-memory
code intermediate

Enforcing Fan-Out: The Self-Check That Prevents Bookmark-Wiki Syndrome

# Step 6 of the ingest pipeline — the fan-out self-check
assert len(updates) >= 3, f"Only updated {len(updates)} pages — review needed"
assert len(new_pages) <= 1, f"Creating {len(new_pages)} new pages — merge candidates?"


The first assertion catches under-ingestion: if a source only touched 1-2 pages, it wasn't truly compiled into the wiki — it was filed. The second catches over-creation: more than 1 new page per source means 1:1 mapping between source and wiki page, which produces a bookmark manager, not a second brain.

Key insight: The assertions encode the fan-out principle as a hard constraint, not a guideline. A source fans out to 2-5 wiki chapters. A wiki chapter draws from 2+ sources. The assertions fail the ingest if either rule breaks.

The self-check in TypeScript: 
if (updates.size < 3) throw new Error(Only updated ${updates.size} pages — too narrow);
if (newPages.length > 1) throw new Error(${newPages.length} new pages — look for merge candidates);


Both throw with an informative message, stopping the pipeline before the update is committed. This is the difference between a wiki and a bookmark manager: the wiki's ingest process structurally refuses to create isolated pages.

| |

The ingest pipeline has two assertions at the end. What does each one enforce, and why?

→ knowledge-management/wiki-ingest-pipeline
quiz intermediate

Why is output compression the first lever in token efficiency?

The four stackable token reduction levers, from easiest to apply:

| Layer | Strategy | Typical Savings | |-------|----------|----------------| | Output | Caveman compression | 22–87% per response | | Input | caveman-compress on CLAUDE.md | ~45% per session | | Session init | Remove unused MCPs/skills | 200–400 tokens per server | | Tool context | Agent SDK tool search | ~90% of tool definitions |

Key insight: Stacked together these achieve 70%+ total reduction in production. But brevity hurts complex reasoning — apply Caveman selectively (simple tasks yes; deep analysis no).

Output tokens cost how much more than input tokens across current Claude models (Haiku/Sonnet/Opus)?

→ ai-agents/token-efficiency
quiz intermediate

Which Gemma 4 models support audio input?

| Model    | Params | Architecture | Audio | VRAM (Q4) |
|----------|--------|-------------|-------|-----------|
| E2B      | ~2B    | Dense        | ✅   | ~1–2 GB   |
| E4B      | ~4B    | Dense        | ✅   | ~3–6 GB   |
| 26B A4B  | ~26B   | MoE (4B act) | ❌   | ~14+ GB   |
| 31B      | ~31B   | Dense        | ❌   | ~18+ GB   |


Key insight: This is an architecture inversion — the *smallest* models do more modalities. Audio support is an edge-computing feature, not a scale feature.

·

The Gemma 4 family has four models. Which ones support audio input?

→ ai-agents/gemma-4
quiz beginner

Agent Skills: The 6-Phase Engineering Lifecycle

📋 Define → 🗂️ Plan → 🔨 Build → 🔍 Verify → 👁️ Review → 🚀 Ship


7 slash commands map to this lifecycle: /spec, /plan, /build, /test, /review, /code-simplify, /ship.

Why the order matters: AI agents are capable but undisciplined — they skip specs, bypass tests, and optimize for "done" over "correct." Agent Skills encodes the engineering discipline as process constraints agents can't negotiate around.

Key insight: The framework includes "anti-rationalization tables" — every skill documents common excuses agents use to skip steps, with documented rebuttals. The agent cannot argue its way out.

|

Addy Osmani's Agent Skills framework enforces a 6-phase lifecycle on AI coding agents. What is the correct order of phases?

→ ai-agents/agent-quality-gates
insight advanced

Nested subagent token cost grows geometrically — nesting is for isolation, not parallelism

Subagent-heavy workflows already run at roughly 7× the tokens of a single-threaded session. Add nesting and each layer can fan out again — cost grows geometrically, not linearly.

> *"One token here costs 20k on the main thread."* — developer reaction to v2.1.172

Nest when: - Task has genuinely noisy investigation steps that would pollute the parent's context - Work is hierarchical: planner → domain investigators → evidence gatherers - You need distilled verdicts, not raw tool output

Don't nest when: - Task fits one context window — depth adds cost and latency for nothing - You're chasing parallelism — parallel dispatch from a single level is cheaper - Work is shallow — a two-step lookup doesn't need a three-layer tree - You haven't scoped the children — imprecision compounds faster than tokens do

Key insight: The signal to nest is *isolation need*, not speed. If you only want parallelism, dispatch multiple subagents at a single level. Depth is for pushing noisy work far enough from the context that matters that only a clean verdict surfaces.

| |
→ ai-agents/nested-subagents
code intermediate

Why does the Python MCP client use AsyncExitStack instead of nested async with?

class MCPClient:
    def __init__(self):
        self.exit_stack = AsyncExitStack()  # tracks cleanup handlers

    async def connect_to_server(self, path: str):
        # Registration order matters — cleanup runs in REVERSE
        transport = await self.exit_stack.enter_async_context(
            stdio_client(params)             # registered 1st → cleaned up 2nd
        )
        self.session = await self.exit_stack.enter_async_context(
            ClientSession(*transport)        # registered 2nd → cleaned up 1st
        )
        await self.session.initialize()

    async def cleanup(self):
        await self.exit_stack.aclose()
        # Cleanup order (LIFO):
        # 1. ClientSession.__aexit__ → sends MCP shutdown, closes session gracefully
        # 2. stdio_client.__aexit__  → terminates the server subprocess


Key insight: AsyncExitStack lets you compose async context managers at runtime without nesting. Each enter_async_context() call pushes a cleanup handler onto the stack. aclose() pops them in reverse — ensuring the protocol layer shuts down cleanly before the transport is destroyed.

Without the stack, you'd need nested async with — which can't be built dynamically (e.g., connecting to 3 servers from a list).

An AsyncExitStack registers cleanup handlers in LIFO order. When the Python MCP client calls `await stack.aclose()`, what happens first: the MCP session closes or the subprocess is killed?

→ ai-agents/mcp-client-python
quiz beginner

Claude Code config types: passive, reactive, or active?

Claude Directory's taxonomy makes the four config types concrete:

graph LR
    P["📄 Passive\nCLAUDE.md templates\nRead at session start"] 
    H["⚡ Reactive\nHooks: lint, format,\nTDD guard on events"]
    S["🤖 Active\nSkills / Plugins\nCalled via /commands"]
    M["🔌 Connectors\nMCP Servers\nFilesystem, GitHub, Figma"]


Key insight: Hooks are the enforcement layer — they *cannot be skipped* because they fire on tool events, not on AI judgment. Skills can be rationalized away; hooks return exit code 2.

A Claude Code *hook* that runs ESLint after every file edit is best described as which config type?

→ ai-agents/claude-directory
quiz beginner

Which color space eliminates muddy gradient transitions?

ColorFlow (a free mesh gradient editor) exposes 6 color blending modes. Most designers default to RGB — but RGB mixes through gray between complementary colors:

| Mode | Result between purple + yellow | |------|-------------------------------| | RGB | Muddy brown in the middle | | OKLab | Clean, vibrant transition |

Key insight: Choosing OKLab vs RGB is a *taste decision*, not a technical one — both are valid. But if your gradient looks dirty, this is usually the fix. The same principle applies to CSS oklch() for static gradients in code.

· ·

Your gradient between purple and yellow looks muddy brown in the middle. Which color space fixes this?

→ ai-design/colorflow
quiz intermediate

Claude Code nested subagents: what is the depth limit and where is it enforced?

Nested subagents shipped in Claude Code v2.1.172 (June 9, 2026). Any subagent can now spawn its own subagents, up to five levels deep.

| | Claude Code | OpenAI Codex | |---|---|---| | Max depth | 5 (hard cap) | 1 (default, configurable) | | Config knob | None — server-enforced | max_depth in ~/.codex/config.toml | | Philosophy | Fixed ceiling, no reasoning required | Conservative default, opt in deliberately |

Key insight: Anthropic locks the ceiling server-side after earlier runaway parallel spawning issues. Codex trusts the user to opt into depth and own the cost.

# Codex contrast — user-configurable depth
[agents]
max_depth = 1    # default; root sessions start at depth 0
max_threads = 6  # concurrent agent thread ceiling


| |

Claude Code v2.1.172 ships nested subagents. What is the hard depth limit, and where is it enforced?

→ ai-agents/nested-subagents
connection beginner

Prompt → Context → Harness: the three-phase evolution of AI engineering

Three roles mark the evolution of AI engineering, each abstracting one level higher than the last:

flowchart LR
    PE["Prompt Engineer\n2022–2023\nCraft the right words"]
    CE["Context Engineer\n2024–2025\nDesign the information system"]
    HE["Harness Engineer\n2025–2026\nControl the autonomous agent"]
    PE --> CE --> HE


- Prompt Engineer — craft instructions to get good outputs from a model - Context Engineer (Karpathy, 2025) — design systems that fill the context window with the right info, right format, right time; builds RAG, context window management, memory hierarchies - Harness Engineer (Böckeler/Fowler, 2026) — build infrastructure that keeps autonomous agents under architectural control; prevents drift when agents generate most of the code

Key insight: Each role exists because the previous one became insufficient. Prompts don't scale to multi-step agents. Context systems don't prevent architectural entropy. The harness is what makes autonomous code generation sustainable at scale.

→ ai-agents/ai-roles-career-paths
quiz intermediate

Consumer vs. B2B: When Is Customer Feedback Harmful?

Steve Jobs famously rejected customer research for truly new products — "People don't know what they want until you show it to them." But Apple runs structured research for existing product improvements.

| Product type | Feedback | Reason | |---|---|---| | Novel consumer (iPhone, iPod) | ❌ Harmful | Customers describe what they know, not what's possible | | Existing consumer features | ✅ Valuable | Improvement space is known | | B2B/enterprise | ✅ Valuable | Real operational pain → actionable improvements |

Key insight: The distinction is whether you're building something that exists (improve it — use feedback) or something that doesn't yet (invent it — trust product intuition over research). Barrel-level product judgment means knowing which mode you're in.

|

Keith Rabois argues that direct customer feedback is actively harmful for consumer products but valuable for B2B. Why the opposite treatment?

→ productivity/barrels-vs-ammunition
quiz beginner

What does Rating.Again do in FSRS?

The four FSRS ratings map to different memory states:

import { Rating } from 'ts-fsrs';
// Rating.Again → "I couldn't recall this at all"  → tomorrow
// Rating.Hard  → "I struggled significantly"       → 2-3 days
// Rating.Good  → "I recalled with effort"          → 3-7 days  
// Rating.Easy  → "I recalled instantly"            → 7+ days


Each successful review multiplies the spacing interval — Again breaks the chain and resets it.

Key insight: The FSRS algorithm optimizes for a ~90% recall rate at review time. "Again" tells it you're well below that threshold — so it brings the card back immediately. Fighting the urge to rate Hard when you should rate Again leads to a buildup of poorly-learned cards that feel like they're in your queue but aren't actually retained.

|

In the FSRS algorithm (used in ts-fsrs), what happens when you rate a card as Rating.Again?

→ knowledge-management/spaced-repetition
connection intermediate

Agent teams go wide; nested subagents go deep — two axes of multi-agent coordination

Claude Code gives you two independent axes for scaling multi-agent work:

| | Nested Subagents | Agent Teams | |---|---|---| | Structure | Strict hierarchy (parent → children) | Peer network (teammates message each other) | | Coordination | Distillation — only results flow up | Full communication — teammates share context | | Best for | Pushing noise down, clean signal up | Parallel exploration, competing hypotheses | | Token cost | Geometric with depth | Linear with team size | | Context isolation | Each layer throwaway | Each teammate independent, persistent |

Main conversation (clean)
  └─ Subagent L1: "investigate incident" (focused)
       ├─ Subagent L2: check DB metrics       (noisy — 50 log reads)
       ├─ Subagent L2: check cache hit rates   (noisy — 30 Redis checks)
       └─ Subagent L2: check third-party API   (noisy — 15 web fetches)
            └─ Subagent L3: grep server logs   (very noisy)


Parent gets four clean verdicts. The main thread was never polluted.

Key insight: Nesting goes *deep* — hierarchical isolation. Teams go *wide* — peer coordination. They compose: a team lead can spawn teammates whose own investigations use nested subagents internally to isolate noise.

| |
→ ai-agents/nested-subagents
quiz intermediate

Why is Bonsai's ternary model '1.58-bit' but stored as 2-bit?

Bonsai from PrismML pushes quantization to its theoretical limit in two families:

| Family | Bit depth | Format | 8B VRAM | |--------|-----------|--------|---------| | Bonsai | 1-bit (Q1_0) | GGUF + MLX | ~1.15 GB | | Ternary-Bonsai | 1.58-bit (Q2_0) | GGUF + MLX | ~1.6 GB | | Standard 8B | 4-bit | GGUF | ~4.5 GB |

At 1-bit, weights are only +1 or −1 — matrix multiplication becomes population count (integer addition). No floating-point unit needed. An 8B model runs on a Raspberry Pi 5 or an older MacBook.

Key insight: The 14× size reduction over FP16 isn't a rounding trick — it's a different class of arithmetic. 1-bit inference is dominated by integer ops, not FP multiply-accumulate.

|

Ternary-Bonsai weights have 3 states (+1, 0, −1). Why are they called '1.58-bit' but packed in 2-bit (Q2_0) slots?

→ ai-agents/bonsai-llm
quiz intermediate

What does `[P]` mean in a Spec-Kit tasks.md file?

## Phase 2: Core Services (parallel — independent)
- [ ] [P] 3. Implement AuthService     src/services/AuthService.ts
- [ ] [P] 4. Implement TokenService    src/services/TokenService.ts
- [ ] [P] 5. Write AuthService tests   src/services/__tests__/AuthService.test.ts
- [ ] [P] 6. Write TokenService tests  src/services/__tests__/TokenService.test.ts

## Phase 3: API Endpoints (sequential — depends on Phase 2)
- [ ] 7. POST /auth/login  src/routes/auth.ts   ← no [P]: needs Phase 2 done first


Key insight: Sequential is the safe default. [P] is explicit permission for parallelism — the developer signals "these tasks have no shared state" and the agent fan-out collapses wall-clock time. TDD structure: test tasks ([P] 5, 6) run concurrently with implementation tasks ([P] 3, 4).

·

In a Spec-Kit `tasks.md`, what does the `[P]` marker on a task tell `/speckit.implement`?

→ ai-agents/spec-driven-development
quiz intermediate

git reset --soft vs --hard: what happens to your files?

git reset has two modes with very different blast radii:

git reset --soft HEAD~2  # → files are STAGED (safe, rewrite history)
git reset --hard HEAD~2  # → files are GONE  (destructive, use reflog to recover)


Key insight: If you accidentally do a --hard reset, git reflog shows every HEAD movement for ~90 days — you can recover "lost" commits with git checkout .

·

After `git reset --soft HEAD~2`, what state are your files in?

→ productivity/git-commands-visualized
quiz beginner

Pi vs. Claude Code — Two Rational Bets

| Dimension | Claude Code | Pi | |-----------|-------------|-----| | Built-in features | Sub-agents, plan mode, permissions UI, MCP | Minimal — build what you need | | Extension model | Hooks (shell scripts) | TypeScript modules (full API) | | Provider support | Anthropic only | 15+ providers | | Session model | Linear history | Tree-structured | | Skill loading | At startup or on-demand | On-demand only |

Key insight: Pi is the "loop is commodity" thesis taken to its logical extreme. The harness is minimal; all behavior above the core loop is either an extension you write, a skill you load, or a package you install. No feature debates — build it your way.

| |

What is the core philosophical difference between Pi and Claude Code?

→ ai-agents/pi-coding-agent
insight beginner

agents-cli Equips Your Agent — It Isn't One

agents-cli is a CLI that equips other coding agents (Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Codex) with the skills to build, eval, and deploy ADK agents on Google Cloud. It is not itself an agent.

# Install the skill set — then ask your coding agent to build something
npx skills add google/agents-cli


When you add the skill set, your coding agent gains seven capabilities: workflow, ADK code, scaffolding, evaluation, deployment, publishing, and observability. Skills load progressively — you only pay token cost for skills actually activated in a session (ADK's L1/L2/L3 progressive disclosure applied to the CLI's own docs).

| Layer | What it is | |-------|-----------| | ADK | Agent framework (tools, routing, callbacks) | | agents-cli | Lifecycle CLI (create → eval → deploy) | | Your coding agent | The one doing the work |

Key insight: The architecture inverts the usual mental model — the CLI teaches your coding agent, rather than the CLI being the agent. This makes agents-cli compatible with any agent following the agentskills.io format.

| |
→ ai-agents/google-agents-cli
quiz intermediate

What links an MCP tool to its interactive UI in MCP Apps?

MCP Apps extends MCP beyond text — tools can return interactive UIs (charts, forms, canvases, video players) that render inline in any compliant chat client. Claude Desktop, Claude Code, ChatGPT, VS Code, and Goose all support it.

registerAppTool(server, 'show_widget', {
  description: 'Show interactive widget',
  inputSchema: { query: z.string() },
  _meta: { ui: { resourceUri: 'ui://my-server/widget' } } // <-- the link
}, async ({ query }) => {
  return { content: [{ type: 'text', text: Query: ${query} }] };
});


Key insight: The tool and UI are built once but run everywhere — any MCP-compliant client renders the same interactive View, just as any browser renders the same HTML.

· ·

What links an MCP tool to its interactive UI in MCP Apps?

→ ai-agents/mcp-apps
quiz intermediate

What is the correct order to adopt the Headless workflow pattern?

graph LR
    I["1️⃣ Interactive\nValidate golden path\n+ every failure mode"] -->|"all modes proven"| V["2️⃣ Validate\nOutput reliable &\npredictable across runs"] -->|"confident"| A["3️⃣ Headless\n--print flag · cron · CI\nAutomated deployment"]

    style I fill:#0891b2,color:#fff
    style V fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style A fill:#166534,color:#fff


Key insight: Headless is the last pattern you adopt, not the first. The maturity ladder isn't optional — each step proves the next one is safe.

# ✘ Wrong: headless on an unvalidated task
claude --print "Refactor the entire auth module"

# ✓ Right: validated interactively first, then automated
git diff main...HEAD | claude --print "List security issues in this diff." | gh pr comment $PR_NUMBER --body-file -


The Headless pattern (fully autonomous Claude Code) has a three-step maturity ladder. What is the correct order?

→ ai-agents/claude-code-workflow-patterns
insight beginner

Why Architecture Diagrams in Code Never Go Stale

Software architecture diagrams have a chronic accuracy problem: created during design, they become authoritative, then slowly diverge from reality as the system evolves. Teams stop trusting them.

Key insight: Architecture diagrams that live alongside code never drift from reality — because they *are* the code.

LikeC4's model: DSL files sit in the same repo as the code. PRs that change architecture include DSL changes. CI can verify diagram consistency. The playground visualizes the current state — not the state from last quarter's off-site.

npx likec4 start  # live preview of current architecture


·
→ tools/likec4
insight intermediate

/grill-with-docs — Shared Domain Language as Token Compression

/grill-with-docs (Matt Pocock) evolves /grill-me for existing codebases. It maintains a CONTEXT.md glossary — a shared vocabulary used identically by the codebase, developers, and the AI agent.

The token compression payoff:

| Without shared language | With CONTEXT.md | |------------------------|------------------| | "a standalone video with no lesson association that was associated with a pitch (the packaging artifact around one or more videos)" | "an Unattached Standalone Video" |

Once pitchId IS NULL is defined as "Unattached Standalone Video," the agent uses that term everywhere — in reasoning, in code, in responses. The glossary persists across sessions; you stop re-explaining context from scratch every time.

> Key insight (Matt Pocock): Shared domain language reduces tokens in the model's own chain-of-thought — not just in the user's prompts. The agent reasons more concisely when it has precise vocabulary. This matters at scale.

When to use which skill:

Existing codebase → /grill-with-docs  (reads CONTEXT.md, updates it live)
New project       → /grill-me         (no prior glossary to build on)


|
→ ai-agents/claude-code-skills
connection intermediate

Agent teams vs long-running agents — adjacent problem spaces

Agent teams and long-running agents solve adjacent but distinct problems:

Agent Teams          →  within-context-window parallelism
Long-Running Agents  →  across-context-window persistence


Agent teams run multiple independent Claude instances simultaneously in a single session — useful for competing hypotheses, parallel research, parallel code review. They're bounded by the session's lifetime.

Long-running agents extend work across sequential context windows via checkpointing, the brain/hands/session split, and resume patterns. They solve time/cost, not concurrency.

Key insight: A multi-day project needs both. Use agent teams for parallel exploration within each context window. Use the checkpoint-resume pattern + brain/hands/session split to link sessions together across context boundaries.

Session 1: [team: 3 agents explore approaches] → checkpoint saved
Session 2: brain agent reads checkpoint, spawns new team for implementation
Session 3: judge agent verifies, closes the loop


The team lead's coordination overhead is bounded per session. The long-running harness manages what survives across context windows.

→ ai-agents/claude-code-agent-teams
code intermediate

HF featureExtraction returns nested arrays — unwrap before using

import { HfInference } from "@huggingface/inference";

const hf = new HfInference(process.env.HF_TOKEN);

// featureExtraction returns number[] | number[][] — model-dependent
const raw = (await hf.featureExtraction({
  model: "google/embeddinggemma-300m",
  inputs: "How do embedding models work?",
})) as number[] | number[][];

// Unwrap if pooled single-input model returns  instead of [768 floats]
const embedding = (Array.isArray(raw[0]) ? (raw as number[][])[0] : raw) as number[];

console.log(embedding.length); // Output: 768
// Without unwrap: embedding would be  and embedding.length would return 1, not 768


Key insight: The HF featureExtraction response shape is not standardized across models. EmbeddingGemma (pooled, single-input) returns ` — one nested level deeper than you might expect. Always guard: Array.isArray(raw[0]) ? raw[0] : raw`.

·

The HF Inference API's featureExtraction returns vectors for EmbeddingGemma. What shape does the response have for a single input, and how must you handle it?

→ ai-agents/embedding-gemma
insight beginner

MCP Isn't Just for SaaS APIs — Wrap Any CLI Tool

The Model Context Protocol has two valid deployment patterns — and the simpler one often gets overlooked:

| Pattern | Example | What's on the other side | |---|---|---| | Hosted API MCP | WooCommerce MCP | Claude → proxy → live store REST API | | Local CLI MCP | WordPress Studio MCP | Claude → thin adapter → local CLI commands |

The WordPress Studio MCP wraps the studio CLI: Claude asks to create a site, the MCP server runs studio create my-site --php=8.2 and returns the output. No API, no auth tokens, no remote server.

Key insight: Any command-line tool becomes AI-native with a thin MCP adapter. The complexity of the integration doesn't depend on the protocol — it depends on what's being wrapped.

// The entire pattern in ~10 lines:
server.tool("wp_create_site", "Create local WP site",
  { name: z.string() },
  async ({ name }) => {
    const out = execSync(studio create ${name}, { encoding: "utf8" });
    return { content: [{ type: "text", text: out }] };
  }
);


| |
→ ai-agents/mcp-ecosystem
code intermediate

MCP server security: fail-closed path traversal prevention

The schema regex rejects obvious traversal characters, but path.resolve() alone isn't enough — you must assert the resolved path stays inside the allowed root.

// BUGGY — missing boundary check:
server.tool("read_report", "...", { filename: z.string() },
  async ({ filename }) => {
    const resolved = path.resolve(BASE_DIR, filename); // resolves '../' but doesn't check!
    return { content: [{ type: "text", text: await fs.readFile(resolved, "utf-8") }] };
  }
);

// CORRECT — schema + boundary + fail closed:
server.tool(
  "read_report",
  "Read a report. Example: { filename: 'Q1-2026.md' }",
  { filename: z.string().regex(/^[\w-]+\.(?:md|txt)$/) },  // schema-level: reject traversal chars
  async ({ filename }) => {
    const resolved = path.resolve(BASE_DIR, filename);
    if (!resolved.startsWith(BASE_DIR + path.sep)) {       // boundary check: fail closed
      return { content: [{ type: "text", text: "Error: access outside allowed directory" }], isError: true };
    }
    return { content: [{ type: "text", text: await fs.readFile(resolved, "utf-8") }] };
  }
);


Key insight: Three guardrails for every MCP tool that touches the filesystem: (1) schema-level regex rejects traversal chars before handler logic runs, (2) resolve then verify the path stays inside BASE_DIR, (3) fail closed — anything ambiguous returns an error, not partial data.

LLM inputs are untrusted. Treat them the same as user input from an HTTP endpoint — validate at the boundary, allow-list rather than deny-list.

·

This MCP tool handler has a security bug. What's wrong, and what's the fix?

→ ai-agents/building-mcp-servers
quiz beginner

What problem does Plannotator solve in AI coding agent workflows?

flowchart TD
    PROP["Agent proposes a plan"] --> INT["🔍 Plannotator intercepts\n(automatic)"]
    INT --> REV["Review workspace\nAnnotate · Delete · Replace"]
    REV --> APP{Approve?}
    APP -->|Yes| RUN["✅ Agent runs"]
    APP -->|No| REVISE["Agent revises + re-proposes"]
    REVISE --> INT

    style INT fill:#7c3aed,color:#fff
    style REV fill:#0891b2,color:#fff


Key insight: Plannotator converts "squinting at terminal text" into a structured PR-style review. You own the specification before execution — the same ownership model that makes human code review valuable.

· ·

When an AI coding agent proposes a plan, Plannotator intercepts it and provides:

→ ai-agents/plannotator
connection advanced

How does tool output bloat trigger compounding errors?

Two chapters that look unrelated — Context Mode (compression) and Compounding Errors (reliability math) — describe the same failure at different levels of abstraction.

The mechanism: 1. Each tool call adds raw output to the context window (e.g. 56 KB Playwright snapshot) 2. After several turns, the context is dominated by tool data, not instructions 3. The model has the information but attention is spread too thin to act on it reliably — faithfulness hallucination: the model ignores facts present in context 4. Accuracy per step drops → p^n compounding kicks in → end-to-end success collapses

Tool call 1: +56 KB → context 10% tool data
Tool call 5: +56 KB → context 50% tool data
Tool call 10: +56 KB → context 80% tool data → attention fragmented
                         ↳ p_step drops from 0.95 → 0.80
                         ↳ 10-step success: 0.80^10 = 10.7% (vs 0.95^10 = 59.9%)


Key insight: Context-mode addresses a root cause of compounding errors, not just a token-cost problem. By compressing 56 KB → 299 B before it enters context, it keeps per-step accuracy high — which has an exponential effect on end-to-end reliability. The two chapters are the same problem at different zoom levels.

· ·
→ ai-agents/context-mode
quiz intermediate

The wall that can't be fixed with external storage

Every long-running agent hits three walls: context windows fill up, every new session starts blank, and models grade their own work too optimistically.

graph TD
    W3["🧱 No Self-Verification\nModels skew positive on self-grading.\n'Are you done?' → 'Yes' (at 30% complete)"] --> FIX3["Separate evaluation signal (judge agent / test ratchet)"]
    style W3 fill:#7f1d1d,color:#fff
    style FIX3 fill:#064e3b,color:#fff


Key insight: A test ratchet — blocking deletion of passing tests — is the practical anti-self-verification mechanism. The agent can't declare victory while tests still fail.

Which of the three walls of long-running agents is hardest to fix — because the model skews positive on self-grading?

→ ai-agents/long-running-agents
insight intermediate

Bonsai's 1-Bit Architecture: Why Integer Ops Change Everything

Bonsai uses genuine 1-bit weights (+1 or -1), not standard 4-bit or 8-bit quantization. At this level, inference becomes dominated by integer addition rather than floating-point multiplication.

| Model | Size (8B) | Precision | Inference math | |-------|-----------|-----------|----------------| | FP16 Llama 3 8B | ~16 GB | Full | Float32/Float16 multiply-accumulate | | 4-bit Llama 3 8B | ~5 GB | Quantized | Float dequantize + multiply | | Bonsai 8B (Q1_0) | ~1.15 GB | 1-bit | Integer add only |

Key insight: Integer addition is fundamentally cheaper than floating-point multiplication. CPU inference becomes 8× faster because every multiply-accumulate becomes an addition. This is why Bonsai runs fast on CPUs with no GPU — not just "smaller" but a different computational primitive.

Standard inference: weight × activation → float multiply-accumulate every step
Bonsai inference:   +1 or -1 × activation → just add or subtract (no float multiply)


All Bonsai backends (CPU, Metal, CUDA) merged into upstream llama.cpp — no extra setup required.

The tradeoff: Lower quality ceiling than 4-bit quantization. Designed for tasks where speed and privacy outweigh absolute accuracy (classification, extraction, formatting on constrained hardware).

| |
→ ai-agents/bonsai-llm

All done for this session! Come back later when cards are due.
